Worked Example Stimulus Specification:
Overview:
Generates a worked-example coordinate plane image showing a single preimage point (black) and its rotated image point (blue) after a rotation about the origin. The image includes connecting lines from the origin to both points (solid gray to preimage, dashed blue to image), a rotation arc (or right-angle marker for 90°) in purple, and an angle label. Both points display their letter labels with coordinate annotations (e.g. "T (−1, 2)" and "T' (1, −2)"). The rotated coordinates are computed automatically from the input — the caller only provides the preimage.

JSON Input Format:

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| label | string | Yes | A single uppercase letter label for the preimage point (e.g. "T", "A"). |
| x | integer | Yes | X-coordinate of the preimage point (−10 to 10). |
| y | integer | Yes | Y-coordinate of the preimage point (−10 to 10). |
| rotation_angle | 90, 180, or 270 | Yes | Rotation angle in degrees. |
| rotation_direction | "clockwise" or "counterclockwise" | Yes | Direction of rotation about the origin. |

Visual Output:
- Square coordinate plane centered at the origin with gridlines at every integer.
- Axes with bidirectional arrows and italic x / y labels.
- Preimage point as a black dot with bold label and coordinate annotation (e.g. "T (−1, 2)").
- Image point as a blue dot with bold primed label and coordinate annotation (e.g. "T' (1, −2)").
- Solid gray line from origin to preimage point.
- Dashed blue line from origin to image point.
- For 90° rotations: a purple right-angle square marker at the origin.
- For 180° / 270° rotations: a purple curved arc at the origin showing the sweep.
- Purple bold angle label (e.g. "180°") near the arc with a white background box.
- Axis range is computed automatically to fit both points with padding.

Constraints:
- The point must NOT be at the origin (0, 0).
- Label must be a single uppercase letter (A–Z).
- Choose coordinates so that the rotated point also falls within [−10, 10].
- Use a variety of quadrants and labels across questions.
- Avoid placing the preimage point directly on an axis when possible, as connecting lines overlap the axis and become invisible.

Rotation rules:
- 90° counterclockwise (or 270° clockwise): (x, y) → (−y, x)
- 180°: (x, y) → (−x, −y)
- 90° clockwise (or 270° counterclockwise): (x, y) → (y, −x)

Examples:

Example 1 — 180° counterclockwise, Quadrant II
{"label":"T","x":-1,"y":2,"rotation_angle":180,"rotation_direction":"counterclockwise"}
Produced image: Preimage T at (−1, 2) in black, image T' at (1, −2) in blue, gray line from origin to T, dashed blue line from origin to T', purple 180° semicircular arc, coordinate labels on both points.

Example 2 — 90° counterclockwise, Quadrant I
{"label":"J","x":5,"y":3,"rotation_angle":90,"rotation_direction":"counterclockwise"}
Produced image: Preimage J at (5, 3) in black, image J' at (−3, 5) in blue, purple right-angle square marker at origin, coordinate labels on both points.

Example 3 — 90° clockwise, Quadrant I
{"label":"A","x":3,"y":4,"rotation_angle":90,"rotation_direction":"clockwise"}
Produced image: Preimage A at (3, 4) in black, image A' at (4, −3) in blue, purple right-angle square marker at origin, coordinate labels on both points.

Example 4 — 270° counterclockwise, Quadrant III
{"label":"P","x":-4,"y":-3,"rotation_angle":270,"rotation_direction":"counterclockwise"}
Produced image: Preimage P at (−4, −3) in black, image P' at (−3, 4) in blue, purple curved arc spanning 270°, coordinate labels on both points.

Example 5 — 270° clockwise, Quadrant I
{"label":"B","x":2,"y":5,"rotation_angle":270,"rotation_direction":"clockwise"}
Produced image: Preimage B at (2, 5) in black, image B' at (5, −2) in blue, purple curved arc spanning 270°, coordinate labels on both points.

Lesson Title: Rotations: Graphing from PreImages and Given Rules
Substandard Description: Graph the results of a rotation about the origin of an image 90°, 180°, 270°, and 360°, clockwise or counterclockwise.
Task:
Determine the coordinates of one rotated vertex from a graphed polygon and a stated rotation about the origin.
Write the ordered pair for the unknown primed vertex in the response blank.
Stimulus Specification:
Stimulus Function: draw_coordinate_plane_pt_distance (polygon mode, fixed axis range)

Overview:
Generates a coordinate plane image with a polygon (triangle, quadrilateral, or pentagon) drawn by connecting 3–5 labeled vertices in order. Uses the "polygon" draw type of the draw_coordinate_plane_pt_distance function. The image shows the preimage polygon only — the student must determine the coordinates of all vertices after a rotation about the origin (given in the question text) and write the ordered pairs for the primed vertices.
All images for this LO use the same fixed −10 to 10 axis range so that the student can see where the rotated polygon will land.

JSON Input Format:

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| draw_type | "polygon" | Yes | Must be "polygon". |
| fixed_axis_range | boolean | Yes | Must be true. Forces the axis range to −10 to 10 regardless of point positions. |
| points | array of 3–5 point objects | Yes | The vertices of the polygon, listed in the order they should be connected. |

Each point object in the points array:

| Field | Type | Constraints | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| label | string | Unique across all points | A capital letter label (e.g. "A", "B"). |
| x | integer | −10 ≤ x ≤ 10 | X-coordinate of the vertex. |
| y | integer | −10 ≤ y ≤ 10 | Y-coordinate of the vertex. |

Constraints:
- 3 to 5 points are required (triangle = 3, quadrilateral = 4, pentagon = 5).
- All labels must be unique capital letters.
- All coordinates must be integers in the range [−10, 10].
- Points are connected in the order given: point 1 → point 2 → … → last point → back to point 1. The vertex order determines the shape — choose the order carefully to avoid crossed/self-intersecting edges.
- fixed_axis_range must always be true for this LO.
- No vertex may be at the origin (0, 0) — rotating the origin about itself is trivial.
- All preimage vertices must satisfy: −8 ≤ x ≤ 8 and −8 ≤ y ≤ 8.
- All rotated vertices (after the stated rotation) must also satisfy: −8 ≤ x ≤ 8 and −8 ≤ y ≤ 8.
  This margin ensures both the preimage and rotated image are fully visible with readable labels on the fixed grid without crowding the border.
- Use a variety of polygon types (triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons) and quadrant placements across questions.

Visual Output:
- Coordinate plane with x and y axes through the origin, drawn as solid black lines with arrowheads.
- Integer tick marks on both axes, labeled at every integer from −10 to 10.
- Gridlines at every integer in medium gray, confined within the border.
- Black border around the plane, one unit beyond the axis range.
- LaTeX axis labels: italic x at the right end, italic y at the top end, outside the border.
- Blue dots at each vertex, each with a bold capital-letter label in LaTeX, automatically positioned to avoid overlapping with other points, edges, and axes.
- Solid blue line segments connecting the vertices in order to form a closed polygon.
- Numeric coordinates are never shown next to points — only the letter labels are visible. The axis tick labels provide the numeric scale so that coordinates can be read from the grid.
- All four quadrants are always visible.

Axis Range (fixed):
The coordinate plane is rendered using a fixed square axis range:
- x-axis range is always −10 to 10.
- y-axis range is always −10 to 10.
- Both axes share the same min and max (plane is square).
- The origin is always visible, and all four quadrants are shown.
- Tick labels appear at every integer from −10 to 10.
- The image does not auto-fit or crop based on polygon point coordinates.

Rotation rules (for question text, not the image):
- 90° counterclockwise (or 270° clockwise): (x, y) → (−y, x)
- 180°: (x, y) → (−x, −y)
- 90° clockwise (or 270° counterclockwise): (x, y) → (y, −x)

Guidelines for Generating Good Inputs:
1. Vertex order matters. List the vertices in the order you want them connected around the perimeter (clockwise or counter-clockwise). Incorrect ordering produces self-intersecting shapes.
2. Spread vertices across quadrants when appropriate. A polygon spanning multiple quadrants makes for a richer question than one confined to a single quadrant.
3. Avoid collinear vertices. Don't place three or more consecutive vertices on the same line — the shape should look clearly multi-sided.
4. Avoid vertices on the axes or origin when possible, as tick labels and axis lines can crowd the vertex labels. If a vertex must sit on an axis, the smart label-placement algorithm will handle it, but the image is cleaner when vertices have some clearance.
5. Keep coordinates within [−8, 8]. This ensures labels have room inside the fixed −10 to 10 border and the rotated image also fits cleanly.
6. Choose labels that match the problem. Typically consecutive letters (A, B, C for a triangle; J, K, L, M for a quadrilateral; P, Q, R, S, T for a pentagon). Always capital letters.
7. Ensure the shape is non-degenerate. The vertices should form a proper polygon with positive area — avoid placing all vertices on a single line.

Examples:

Example 1 — Rectangle in Quadrant II (axis-aligned)
{"draw_type":"polygon","fixed_axis_range":true,"points":[{"label":"B","x":-7,"y":3},{"label":"C","x":-5,"y":3},{"label":"D","x":-5,"y":5},{"label":"E","x":-7,"y":5}]}
Produced image: A coordinate plane (fixed range [−10, 10]) with rectangle BCDE in quadrant II. Tick labels at every integer from −10 to 10 on both axes. All four quadrants visible. After a 90° clockwise rotation: B'(3, 7), C'(3, 5), D'(5, 5), E'(5, 7).

Example 2 — Triangle in Quadrant I
{"draw_type":"polygon","fixed_axis_range":true,"points":[{"label":"A","x":2,"y":1},{"label":"B","x":6,"y":1},{"label":"C","x":4,"y":5}]}
Produced image: A coordinate plane (fixed range [−10, 10]) with triangle ABC in quadrant I. Tick labels at every integer from −10 to 10. All four quadrants visible. After a 180° rotation: A'(−2, −1), B'(−6, −1), C'(−4, −5).

Example 3 — Triangle spanning Quadrants I and IV
{"draw_type":"polygon","fixed_axis_range":true,"points":[{"label":"P","x":1,"y":3},{"label":"Q","x":5,"y":-2},{"label":"R","x":2,"y":-4}]}
Produced image: A coordinate plane (fixed range [−10, 10]) with triangle PQR spanning quadrants I and IV. Tick labels at every integer from −10 to 10. All four quadrants visible. After a 90° counterclockwise rotation: P'(−3, 1), Q'(2, 5), R'(4, 2).

Example 4 — Quadrilateral spanning Quadrants I and II
{"draw_type":"polygon","fixed_axis_range":true,"points":[{"label":"W","x":-3,"y":2},{"label":"X","x":1,"y":4},{"label":"Y","x":4,"y":2},{"label":"Z","x":1,"y":1}]}
Produced image: A coordinate plane (fixed range [−10, 10]) with quadrilateral WXYZ. Tick labels at every integer from −10 to 10. All four quadrants visible. After a 90° clockwise rotation: W'(2, 3), X'(4, −1), Y'(2, −4), Z'(1, −1).

Example 5 — Quadrilateral in Quadrant III
{"draw_type":"polygon","fixed_axis_range":true,"points":[{"label":"J","x":-6,"y":-2},{"label":"K","x":-3,"y":-2},{"label":"L","x":-2,"y":-5},{"label":"M","x":-7,"y":-5}]}
Produced image: A coordinate plane (fixed range [−10, 10]) with trapezoid JKLM entirely in quadrant III. Tick labels at every integer from −10 to 10. All four quadrants visible. After a 180° rotation: J'(6, 2), K'(3, 2), L'(2, 5), M'(7, 5).

Example 6 — Pentagon in Quadrant I
{"draw_type":"polygon","fixed_axis_range":true,"points":[{"label":"A","x":2,"y":1},{"label":"B","x":5,"y":2},{"label":"C","x":6,"y":5},{"label":"D","x":3,"y":7},{"label":"E","x":1,"y":4}]}
Produced image: A coordinate plane (fixed range [−10, 10]) with pentagon ABCDE in quadrant I. Tick labels at every integer from −10 to 10. All four quadrants visible. After a 90° counterclockwise rotation: A'(−1, 2), B'(−2, 5), C'(−5, 6), D'(−7, 3), E'(−4, 1).

Example 7 — Triangle with vertex on an axis
{"draw_type":"polygon","fixed_axis_range":true,"points":[{"label":"F","x":3,"y":0},{"label":"G","x":6,"y":4},{"label":"H","x":1,"y":3}]}
Produced image: A coordinate plane (fixed range [−10, 10]) with triangle FGH. Tick labels at every integer from −10 to 10. All four quadrants visible. After a 90° clockwise rotation: F'(0, −3), G'(4, −6), H'(3, −1).
Allowed Content Types: Text Entry Question with multiple blanks with stimulus
Allowed Generator Configs: Text Entry - Math - Multiple Blanks - SB
Question Requirements:
The question must contain exactly these components in order:

1. A command statement with TWO sentences:
   - First sentence: "A [polygon] is shown on the coordinate grid and rotated [angle]° [clockwise/counterclockwise] around the origin."
   - Second sentence: "Write the coordinates of the rotated vertex [label]'."
   Both sentences MUST appear in the question stem, on separate lines, before the response line.

2. The visual stimulus image is generated automatically. Do not describe the polygon's coordinates or appearance; referencing the coordinate grid and the rotation is allowed only in the required two-sentence command statement.

3. A response frame containing EXACTLY ONE line — the unknown rotated vertex shown as a single blank ordered pair, like:
   [label]'(<blank-1>, <blank-2>)
   The example above uses [label]' for illustration only — the unknown vertex may be ANY vertex of the polygon.

QUESTION REQUIREMENTS:
- RANDOMIZE the unknown vertex across generated questions. The unknown rotated vertex MUST be chosen uniformly at random from ALL vertices of the preimage. Do NOT default to a single label — vary the unknown across triangles (e.g., A', B', C'), quadrilaterals (e.g., A'-D', J'-M', W'-Z'), and pentagons (e.g., A'-E', P'-T'). Across a batch of generated questions, every vertex position should appear at roughly equal frequency.
- The vertex label in the second sentence (with prime) MUST match the label on the response line.
- The response frame MUST contain exactly one line. Do NOT prefill or list any other rotated vertices in the stem; the student reads the other vertices from the graphed polygon.
- Use exactly two blanks for the target rotated vertex: <blank-1> for the x-coordinate and <blank-2> for the y-coordinate.
- Do NOT include section headers, labels like "PART 1", "PART 2", or any structural keywords in the output.
- Angle set constraint: [angle] must be one of {90, 180, 270}.
- Direction constraint for 180°: If [angle] = 180, allow either clockwise or counterclockwise (both are valid and equivalent).
- The coordinate grid must use a fixed -10 to 10 window on both axes, and all preimage and rotated vertices must fall within -8 to 8.
- The preimage must be a valid simple polygon: edges may intersect only at shared endpoints (no crossing/self-intersections).
- Vertices must be connected in boundary order (clockwise or counterclockwise) to form the polygon perimeter.
- No degenerate polygons: no repeated vertices, no zero-length sides, and polygon area must be > 0.
- If the stem says "quadrilateral," the preimage must have exactly 4 vertices/4 sides and be non-self-intersecting. (Likewise triangle = 3, pentagon = 5.)
- VARY the polygon labeling scheme too — do not always use A/B/C/D. Acceptable label sets include consecutive letters such as J/K/L/M, P/Q/R/S, W/X/Y/Z, etc., chosen to match the expected vertex count.
Example Question:
A quadrilateral is shown on the coordinate grid and rotated 90° clockwise around the origin.
Write the coordinates of the rotated vertex K'.

K'(__, __)
Worked Example Stimulus Specification:
Overview: Generates a worked-example coordinate plane image showing a preimage polygon (black, solid edges) and its rotated image polygon (blue, dashed edges) after a rotation about the origin. The image includes connecting lines from the origin to a selected vertex pair, a rotation arc (or right-angle marker for 90°) in purple, and an angle label. Vertex labels use prime notation for the image (A → A'). The rotated coordinates are computed automatically — the caller only provides the preimage vertices. A smart vertex-selection algorithm picks the vertex whose arc and connecting lines have maximum visual clearance from the axes. JSON Input Format: | Field | Type | Required | Description | |---|---|---|---| | preimage_vertices | array of 3–5 objects | Yes | Vertices of the preimage polygon listed in connection order. Each has `label` (single uppercase letter), `x` (int, −10 to 10), `y` (int, −10 to 10). | | rotation_angle | 90, 180, or 270 | Yes | Rotation angle in degrees. | | rotation_direction | "clockwise" or "counterclockwise" | Yes | Direction of rotation about the origin. | Visual Output: - Square coordinate plane centered at the origin with gridlines at every integer. - Axes with bidirectional arrows and italic x / y labels. - Preimage polygon with black solid edges, black dots at vertices, and bold black vertex labels pushed outward from the centroid. - Image polygon with blue dashed edges, blue dots at vertices, and bold blue primed vertex labels (e.g. A', B'). - Solid gray line from origin to the selected preimage vertex. - Dashed blue line from origin to the corresponding image vertex. - For 90° rotations: a purple right-angle square marker at the origin. - For 180° / 270° rotations: a purple curved arc at the origin showing the sweep. - Purple bold angle label (e.g. "90°") near the arc with a white background box. - Axis range is computed automatically to fit both polygons with padding. Constraints: - 3 to 5 vertices required (triangle = 3, quadrilateral = 4, pentagon = 5). - All labels must be unique single uppercase letters. - All coordinates must be integers in [−10, 10]. - Vertices are connected in the given order to form a closed polygon — choose the order carefully to avoid self-intersecting edges. - No vertex may be at the origin (0, 0). - All rotated vertices must also fall within [−10, 10]. - Avoid placing vertices directly on axes when possible — the arc and connecting lines become invisible when a vertex sits on an axis. - Use a variety of polygon types (triangles, quadrilaterals) and quadrant placements across questions. - Keep coordinates in [−8, 8] for best label readability. Rotation rules: - 90° counterclockwise (or 270° clockwise): (x, y) → (−y, x) - 180°: (x, y) → (−x, −y) - 90° clockwise (or 270° counterclockwise): (x, y) → (y, −x) Examples: Example 1 — Triangle 180° counterclockwise {"preimage_vertices":[{"label":"R","x":1,"y":4},{"label":"S","x":4,"y":2},{"label":"T","x":2,"y":1}],"rotation_angle":180,"rotation_direction":"counterclockwise"} Produced image: Black solid triangle RST in quadrant I, blue dashed triangle R'S'T' in quadrant III. 180° purple arc at origin with connecting lines to the best vertex. R'(−1,−4), S'(−4,−2), T'(−2,−1). Example 2 — Triangle 270° counterclockwise {"preimage_vertices":[{"label":"D","x":0,"y":3},{"label":"E","x":2,"y":1},{"label":"F","x":1,"y":5}],"rotation_angle":270,"rotation_direction":"counterclockwise"} Produced image: Black solid triangle DEF, blue dashed triangle D'E'F'. Smart vertex selection avoids D (on y-axis) and picks E or F for the arc display. D'(−3,0), E'(−1,−2), F'(−5,−1). Example 3 — Quadrilateral 90° clockwise {"preimage_vertices":[{"label":"A","x":1,"y":1},{"label":"B","x":4,"y":1},{"label":"C","x":4,"y":3},{"label":"D","x":1,"y":3}],"rotation_angle":90,"rotation_direction":"clockwise"} Produced image: Black solid rectangle ABCD in quadrant I, blue dashed rectangle A'B'C'D' in quadrant IV. Purple right-angle marker at origin. A'(1,−1), B'(1,−4), C'(3,−4), D'(3,−1). Example 4 — Quadrilateral 90° counterclockwise {"preimage_vertices":[{"label":"W","x":2,"y":3},{"label":"X","x":5,"y":3},{"label":"Y","x":5,"y":6},{"label":"Z","x":2,"y":6}],"rotation_angle":90,"rotation_direction":"counterclockwise"} Produced image: Black solid rectangle WXYZ in quadrant I, blue dashed rectangle W'X'Y'Z' in quadrant II. Purple right-angle marker at origin. W'(−3,2), X'(−3,5), Y'(−6,5), Z'(−6,2). Example 5 — Triangle spanning quadrants, 90° clockwise {"preimage_vertices":[{"label":"P","x":-3,"y":4},{"label":"Q","x":2,"y":5},{"label":"R","x":1,"y":1}],"rotation_angle":90,"rotation_direction":"clockwise"} Produced image: Black solid triangle PQR spanning quadrants I and II, blue dashed triangle P'Q'R' spanning quadrants I and IV. Purple right-angle marker. P'(4,3), Q'(5,−2), R'(1,−1). ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ WORKED EXAMPLE TEXT STYLE (required; complements the image spec above) ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ The image generated above is shown ALONGSIDE the worked-example text. The student can see the preimage polygon, the rotated image polygon (dashed blue), and the arc. Therefore the WE TEXT must lean on the image rather than restating coordinates as given facts. STEP STRUCTURE (exactly 3 steps; "Step N:" literal prefix required): Step 1 — Recall the rotation rule in plain English. Open with a single instruction sentence: "Recall the rotation rule for a $[angle]^\circ$ [direction] rotation about the origin." Then a blank line, then ONE supporting sentence stating the rule in words plus math notation. Math notation MUST use balanced $...$ pairs (every opening $ has a closing $). Use \to (NOT \rightarrow). The rule line must look exactly like one of: For a $90^\circ$ counterclockwise (or $270^\circ$ clockwise) rotation, the rule is $(x, y) \to (-y, x)$. For a $180^\circ$ rotation, the rule is $(x, y) \to (-x, -y)$. For a $90^\circ$ clockwise (or $270^\circ$ counterclockwise) rotation, the rule is $(x, y) \to (y, -x)$. NEVER end a math-mode segment without a closing $. Step 2 — Read the preimage vertex from the image; describe its rotation using SPATIAL REASONING. Open with: "Find the position of [label] on the image, then apply the rule." Then a blank line, then a paragraph that: • References the image to locate the preimage vertex (e.g. "Looking at the image, [label] is N units to the right of the origin and M units above the x-axis."). • Describes how the rotation moves it spatially (e.g. "After a $90^\circ$ clockwise rotation, that point swings down into Quadrant IV; the vertical distance becomes the new horizontal distance, and the horizontal distance becomes the new vertical distance with its sign flipped."). • Concludes by stating the rotated coordinates in PLAIN parentheses, not math mode: write (N, -M) — NOT $(N, -M)$. ABSOLUTE PROHIBITION: Do NOT write "[label] is at (a, b)" or "[label] is at $(a, b)$" or any phrasing that asserts the preimage vertex's coordinates as a stated given. The validator reads such phrasing as a hallucinated fact because the question text does not restate the coordinates. Always frame coordinates as something the student READS from the image via spatial reasoning, never as a given premise. Step 3 — State the rotated coordinates as the answer. Open with: "State the coordinates of the rotated vertex." Then a blank line, then ONE line giving the answer in plain text: [label]'(N, -M) NO LaTeX wrapper around the tuple. NO additional commentary. FORMATTING RULES (apply to every step): 1. Each step's `step` field starts with the literal prefix "Step N: " followed by the instruction sentence, a blank line (\n\n), then the supporting paragraph. 2. Coordinate tuples in step text MUST use plain ASCII parentheses: (3, -5) — NOT $(3, -5)$. 3. LaTeX is permitted ONLY for: degree measures ($90^\circ$), single variable letters in prose ($x$, $y$), and the rotation rule when written as a mapping. Every $ must be closed. 4. Use ASCII hyphen-minus '-' for negative numbers. Do NOT use the Unicode minus '−' (U+2212). Do NOT use the Unicode degree symbol '°' outside math mode — write $90^\circ$ instead. 5. Use \n for line breaks. Do NOT use HTML
or
. 6. Do NOT include \rightarrow arrows in step prose. The mapping rule may appear once in Step 1 using \to inside balanced math mode; otherwise describe transitions in plain English. 7. Each step must read as a self-contained, image-aware explanation. Never say "as computed above" or "from step N"; the validator evaluates each step's groundedness independently. EXAMPLE (illustrative, do not copy verbatim): Step 1: Recall the rotation rule for a $90^\circ$ clockwise rotation about the origin. For a $90^\circ$ clockwise rotation, the rule is $(x, y) \to (y, -x)$. The new x-coordinate is the old y-coordinate, and the new y-coordinate is the old x-coordinate with its sign flipped. Step 2: Find the position of L on the image, then apply the rule. Looking at the image, L is 5 units to the right of the origin and 2 units above the x-axis. After a $90^\circ$ clockwise rotation, that horizontal distance of 5 becomes the new vertical distance below the x-axis, and the vertical distance of 2 becomes the new horizontal distance to the right. So L' is 2 units right and 5 units below the origin: (2, -5). Step 3: State the coordinates of the rotated vertex. L'(2, -5) ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Substandard Description: Identify whether a scatterplot shows a linear pattern and classify it as positive, negative, or no association.
Task: Determine which scatterplot displays a nonlinear relationship.
Stimulus Specification:
## Rules
- **Exactly one** graph MUST be **nonlinear** — this is the correct answer.
- The other **three** graphs MUST be **linear**.

## Available `relationship_type` Values

**Linear** (use for the three distractors — you MUST pick three different ones):

| Value | Description |
|---|---|
| `positive_linear` | Clear upward trend with natural scatter (r ≥ 0.97) |
| `negative_linear` | Clear downward trend with natural scatter (r ≤ −0.97) |
| `constant` | Tight horizontal band — a linear relationship with slope ≈ 0 |

**Nonlinear** (use for the correct answer — pick ONE):

| Value | Description |
|---|---|
| `nonlinear_positive` | Hockey-stick exponential growth: flat on the left, shoots up on the right |
| `nonlinear_negative` | Reverse hockey-stick: starts high on the left, drops sharply, flattens on the right |
| `parabola_up` | U-shape (quadratic opening upward) |
| `parabola_down` | Inverted U (quadratic opening downward) |

## Example

```json
[
  { "relationship_type": "positive_linear" },
  { "relationship_type": "constant" },
  { "relationship_type": "parabola_down" },
  { "relationship_type": "negative_linear" }
]
```
## IMPORTANT  NOTE: the stimulus_description objects must ONLY  contain this field: "relationship_type"; do NOT include any other fields in this stimulus_description object, otherwise, the generation will fail
Question Requirements: - Question stem MUST be exactly EITHER: "Which scatterplot does NOT suggest a linear relationship between $x$ and $y$?" OR "Which scatterplot best represents a nonlinear relationship between the $x$- and $y$-values?"
Example Question:
Which scatterplot does NOT suggest a linear relationship between $x$ and $y$?

A) [Scatterplot: ~13 data points trending upward from lower-left to upper-right, suggesting a positive linear relationship]
B) [Scatterplot: ~13 data points trending downward from upper-left to lower-right, suggesting a negative linear relationship]
C) [Scatterplot: ~13 data points forming a curved pattern arching upward, suggesting a nonlinear relationship]
D) [Scatterplot: ~13 data points scattered in a roughly horizontal band, suggesting a constant/horizontal linear relationship]

Correct Answer: C

Which scatterplot best represents a nonlinear relationship between the $x$- and $y$-values?

A) [Scatterplot: ~10 data points trending downward in a straight-line pattern (negative slope)]
B) [Scatterplot: ~10 data points trending upward in a roughly straight-line pattern (positive slope)]
C) [Scatterplot: ~10 data points forming a roughly horizontal straight-line pattern (zero slope)]
D) [Scatterplot: ~10 data points forming a curved (decreasing exponential-like) pattern]

Correct Answer: D

|---|---|
| `positive_linear` | Clear upward trend with natural scatter (r ≥ 0.97) |
| `negative_linear` | Clear downward trend with natural scatter (r ≤ −0.97) |
| `constant` | Tight horizontal band — a linear relationship with slope ≈ 0 |

**Nonlinear** (use for the three distractors — pick three DIFFERENT ones):

| Value | Description |
|---|---|
| `nonlinear_positive` | Hockey-stick exponential growth: flat on the left, shoots up on the right |
| `nonlinear_negative` | Reverse hockey-stick: starts high on the left, drops sharply, flattens on the right |
| `parabola_up` | U-shape (quadratic opening upward) |
| `parabola_down` | Inverted U (quadratic opening downward) |

## Example

```json
[
  { "relationship_type": "nonlinear_positive" },
  { "relationship_type": "negative_linear" },
  { "relationship_type": "parabola_up" },
  { "relationship_type": "nonlinear_negative" }
]
```

## IMPORTANT  NOTE: the stimulus_description objects must ONLY  contain this field: "relationship_type"; do NOT include any other fields in this stimulus_description object, otherwise, the generation will fail
Question Requirements: - Question stem MUST be exactly EITHER: "Which scatterplot suggests a linear relationship between $x$ and $y$?" OR "Which scatterplot best represents a linear relationship between the $x$- and $y$-values?"
Example Question:
Which scatterplot suggests a linear relationship between $x$ and $y$?

A) [Scatterplot: ~12 data points forming a U-shaped curved pattern, suggesting a nonlinear relationship]
B) [Scatterplot: ~12 data points trending downward from upper-left to lower-right in a straight-line pattern, suggesting a negative linear relationship]
C) [Scatterplot: ~12 data points forming an arching curve that rises then falls, suggesting a nonlinear relationship]
D) [Scatterplot: ~12 data points scattered randomly with no clear trend, suggesting no association]

Correct Answer: B

Substandard Description: Identify whether a scatterplot shows a linear pattern and classify it as positive, negative, or no association.
Task: Classify the association shown in a real-world scatterplot as positive linear, negative linear, nonlinear, or no apparent association.
Stimulus Specification:
## ScatterPlot Stimulus Specification
### Axis Rules
- Both axes **must** start at 0 (`min_value = 0`).
- Preferred axis ranges (pick one consistently for both axes unless noted):
  - 0–9 with `x_axis_step=1`, `y_axis_step=1`
  - 0–8 with `x_axis_step=2`, `y_axis_step=2`
  - 0–12 with `x_axis_step=2`, `y_axis_step=2`
- x and y ranges may differ (e.g., x 0–11 step 1, y 0–70 step 10) when context requires it, but keep tick counts between 5 and 11.
- Axes should be labeled.
- x-axis limited to numbers under 15, y-axis limited to numbers under 100

### Point Rules
- Use **9–15 points** per scatterplot.
- All coordinates must be non-negative integers within the axis range.
- Points should clearly convey the intended relationship type:
  - **Positive linear** — points trend upward left-to-right with tight spread.
  - **Negative linear** — points trend downward left-to-right with tight spread.
  - **No association** — points scattered randomly with no visible trend.
  - **Nonlinear** — points follow a visible curve (e.g., U-shape, clear L shape, clear J shape). Spread points along the curve so the shape is obvious.
- Spread points across the full axis range; avoid clustering in one corner.
- Scatter around the trend is realistic; avoid perfectly collinear points.
- Data points in scatterplot MUST approximate the pattern with natural scatter, not form a perfect line or perfect curve.

### Gridlines
- Always set `show_minor_gridlines: false`.

### Title
- Make sure the scatterplot has a title.

}
Question Requirements:
- The question stem must begin with 1–2 sentences describing a real-world data collection scenario, ending with what the scatterplot shows.
- The question stem must end with exactly "Which statement is best supported by the scatterplot?" and this should be on a new line
- Answer choices MUST include one each of: a positive linear association, a negative linear association, a non-linear association, and no apparent association.
- Each answer choice MUST follow the phrasing pattern: "There is [type] association between [variable 1] and [variable 2]."
- The correct answer MUST clearly and unambiguously match the pattern shown in the scatterplot.
- Real-world contexts MUST be age-appropriate, familiar to 8th-grade students, and realistic.

Substandard Description: Calculate the volume of cones.
Task: Calculate the volume of a cone when the radius and height are provided.
Stimulus Specification:
# Stimulus Function: draw_labeled_cone

## Overview

Generates a single 2D outline of a cone with labeled dimensions. The
cone is drawn proportionally to the provided measurements. The cone
can be oriented **inverted** (default — base at top, apex at bottom) or
**upright** (apex at top, base at bottom).

In inverted orientation the base elliptical face is fully visible at the
top while the apex is a single point at the bottom. In upright
orientation the base at the bottom is shown with a dashed front arc
(hidden behind the cone body) and a solid back arc, while the apex is
at the top.

Dimension labels are displayed alongside bracket lines that indicate
the measured length; the label text sits at the centre of the bracket
with a white background so it cleanly covers the line beneath it.

Key characteristics:
- Single cone per image.
- Inverted orientation (default): height bracket on the left, radius/diameter
  bracket at the top (when provided), slant height label to the right of the slant edge.
- Upright orientation: height bracket on the right (or left when slant
  height is also shown), radius/diameter bracket at the bottom (when provided).
- When radius is used, a small black dot marks the centre of the base and
  dashed construction lines show the height and radius.
- Both `radius` and `diameter` may be omitted. The cone is still drawn
  using `radius_draw_value` for proportions, but no base dimension bracket
  or label is rendered.
- Labels can be integers, single-decimal numbers, or LaTeX variables.
- All labels include units.
- Optional **ice_cream_cone** detail that draws a waffle crosshatch pattern
  on the cone surface and forces inverted orientation.

**Output of these specifications:** A single **JSON object** that can be
passed to the drawing function (and used to instantiate `LabeledCone`).
Do not produce Python code or other formats — only valid JSON.

---

## Variable Label Guidance

When a label is a variable, the corresponding `*_draw_value` field should
be provided to control the drawing proportions (sensible defaults exist,
but explicit values are preferred to match the intended visual).

---

## JSON Object Specification

The input to the function is one JSON object. All keys are strings. Types
and requirements below.

### Required Keys

| Key | JSON type | Description |
|-----|-----------|-------------|
| `height` | `string` | Label for the height dimension. May be an integer (`"12"`), a number with one decimal place (`"7.5"`), or a LaTeX variable (`"h"`). |
| `units` | `string` | Unit of measurement appended to every label (e.g. `"cm"`, `"in."`, `"m"`). |

### Optional Keys

| Key | JSON type | Default | Description |
|-----|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `radius` | `string` | `null` | Label for the radius dimension of the circular base. Mutually exclusive with `diameter`. A small dot is drawn at the centre of the base. |
| `diameter` | `string` | `null` | Label for the diameter dimension of the circular base. Mutually exclusive with `radius`. |
| `slant_height` | `string` | `null` | Label for the slant height along the side of the cone. When provided, the label is placed alongside the right slant edge. |
| `height_draw_value` | `number` | `6.0` | Numeric value used for drawing proportions when the height label is a variable. Must be > 0 and ≤ 20. Ignored when height is numeric. |
| `radius_draw_value` | `number` | `3.0` | Numeric value used for drawing proportions when the radius label is a variable. Must be > 0 and ≤ 10. Ignored when radius is numeric. |
| `diameter_draw_value` | `number` | `6.0` | Numeric value used for drawing proportions when the diameter label is a variable. Must be > 0 and ≤ 20. Ignored when diameter is numeric. |
| `slant_height_draw_value` | `number` | `null` | Numeric value used for drawing proportions when the slant_height label is a variable. When `null`, the slant height is computed from height and radius via the Pythagorean theorem. Must be > 0 and ≤ 30. |
| `detail` | `string` | `null` | Visual detail type. Currently supports `"ice_cream_cone"` which draws a waffle crosshatch pattern and forces inverted orientation. When omitted or `null`, draws a plain outline cone. |
| `orientation` | `string` | `null` | Orientation of the cone. Must be `"upright"` or `"inverted"`. When omitted or `null`, defaults to inverted. When `detail` is set, the detail type determines the orientation and this field is ignored. |

### Validation Rules

- At most one of `radius` or `diameter` may be provided (not both).
  When neither is given, the cone is drawn using `radius_draw_value`
  for proportions but no base dimension label or bracket is rendered.
- The height-to-radius ratio must be between 1:4 and 4:1 to ensure
  reasonable cone proportions. Ratios outside this range are rejected.
- Labels must not be empty strings.
- Numeric and string inputs are accepted for `height`, `radius`,
  `diameter`, and `slant_height`; numeric inputs are automatically
  converted to strings.
- `orientation`, if provided, must be `"upright"` or `"inverted"`.
- `detail`, if provided, must be `"ice_cream_cone"`.

---

## Detail Types

### `"ice_cream_cone"`

When `detail` is `"ice_cream_cone"`, the cone is styled to resemble an
ice cream cone:
- A waffle crosshatch pattern of diagonal rib lines and curved horizontal
  bands fills the cone surface.
- Orientation is forced to inverted (base at top, point at bottom).
- Internal construction lines (dashed height line, dashed radius line,
  centre dot) are suppressed for visual clarity.

---

## Orientation

The `orientation` field controls how the cone is drawn:

- **`"inverted"`** (default): The cone points downward. The base
  elliptical face is at the top (fully drawn). Two slant edges converge
  to the apex at the bottom. The height bracket appears on the left side;
  the radius/diameter bracket appears at the top.

- **`"upright"`**: The cone points upward. The base elliptical face is
  at the bottom (front arc dashed, back arc solid). Two slant edges
  converge to the apex at the top. The height bracket appears on the
  right side (or left when slant height is also present); the
  radius/diameter bracket appears at the bottom.

---

## Example JSON Inputs

### Example 1: Inverted cone with radius and slant height

```json
{
  "height": "12",
  "radius": "5",
  "slant_height": "13",
  "units": "cm"
}
```

Draws an inverted cone with height 12 cm (bracket on the left), radius
5 cm (bracket at the top), and slant height 13 cm (label alongside the
right slant edge). Dashed construction lines show the height and radius
inside the cone.

### Example 2: Upright cone with diameter

```json
{
  "height": "7.5",
  "diameter": "5.625",
  "units": "in.",
  "orientation": "upright"
}
```

Draws an upright cone with height 7.5 in. (bracket on the right) and
diameter 5.625 in. (bracket at the bottom).

### Example 3: Ice cream cone

```json
{
  "height": "4.5",
  "radius": "2",
  "units": "in.",
  "detail": "ice_cream_cone"
}
```

Draws an inverted cone with a waffle crosshatch pattern, height 4.5 in.
on the left, and radius 2 in. at the top. The ice cream cone detail
forces inverted orientation.

### Example 4: Inverted cone with diameter

```json
{
  "height": "10",
  "diameter": "8",
  "units": "ft"
}
```

Draws an inverted cone with height 10 ft and diameter 8 ft. The diameter
bracket spans the full width at the top.

### Example 5: Variable labels

```json
{
  "height": "h",
  "radius": "r",
  "units": "m",
  "height_draw_value": 8.0,
  "radius_draw_value": 3.0
}
```

Draws an inverted cone with variable height *h* m and variable radius
*r* m. The draw values control the visual proportions.

### Example 6: Height only (no base dimension label)

```json
{
  "height": "10",
  "units": "cm",
  "radius_draw_value": 4.0
}
```

Draws an inverted cone with height 10 cm (bracket on the left). No
radius or diameter label is shown. The cone is drawn using
`radius_draw_value` (4.0) for proportions.

### Example 7: Height and slant height only

```json
{
  "height": "12",
  "slant_height": "13",
  "units": "cm",
  "radius_draw_value": 5.0
}
```

Draws an inverted cone with height 12 cm and slant height 13 cm. No
base dimension bracket is rendered; the cone proportions use
`radius_draw_value` (5.0).
Question Requirements:
- The question stem must follow this format: "The diagram shows an ice cream cone and its dimensions. Which measurement is closest to the volume of the cone in cubic [units]?"
- All answer options must follow this format: "$[value] \text{ [units]}^3$"
- Be sure to set "detail": "ice_cream_cone" in the stimulus description
- The correct answer must be an integer.

# Stimulus Function: draw_labeled_cone (Ice Cream Cone, Height Only)

## Overview

Generates a single 2D ice cream cone image with a labeled height
dimension. The cone is drawn in **inverted** orientation (base at top,
apex at bottom) with a waffle crosshatch pattern on the surface.

The base elliptical face is fully visible at the top while the apex is
a single point at the bottom. No radius or diameter label is shown —
the only dimension label displayed is the height, shown alongside a
bracket on the left side of the cone.

The height label text sits at the centre of its bracket with a white
background so it cleanly covers the line beneath it.

Key characteristics:
- Single ice cream cone per image.
- Always inverted orientation (base at top, apex at bottom).
- Waffle crosshatch pattern fills the cone surface.
- Height bracket on the left side.
- No radius or diameter label or bracket is rendered. The cone's width
  is controlled by `radius_draw_value`, which affects only the drawing
  proportions — it does not produce a visible label.
- Internal construction lines (dashed height line, dashed radius line,
  centre dot) are suppressed for visual clarity.
- The height label can be an integer or single-decimal number.
- The height label includes units.

**Output of these specifications:** A single **JSON object** that can be
passed to the drawing function (and used to instantiate `LabeledCone`).
Do not produce Python code or other formats — only valid JSON.

---

## JSON Object Specification

The input to the function is one JSON object. All keys are strings. Types
and requirements below.

### Required Keys

| Key | JSON type | Description |
|-----|-----------|-------------|
| `height` | `string` | Label for the height dimension. May be an integer (`"12"`) or a number with one decimal place (`"7.5"`). |
| `units` | `string` | Unit of measurement appended to every label (e.g. `"cm"`, `"in."`, `"m"`). |
| `detail` | `string` | Must be `"ice_cream_cone"`. Draws a waffle crosshatch pattern and forces inverted orientation. |
| `radius_draw_value` | `number` | Controls how wide the cone is drawn. This affects only the visual proportions — no radius label or bracket is rendered. Must be > 0 and ≤ 10. |

### Keys That Must Be Omitted

Do **not** include the following keys. They are not needed for this
use case:

- `radius` — omit entirely (no radius label).
- `diameter` — omit entirely (no diameter label).
- `diameter_draw_value` — omit entirely.
- `slant_height` — omit entirely (no slant height label).
- `slant_height_draw_value` — omit entirely.
- `orientation` — omit entirely (the ice cream cone detail forces
  inverted orientation automatically).

### Validation Rules

- `detail` must be `"ice_cream_cone"`.
- `radius` and `diameter` must not be provided.
- The height-to-radius ratio (using `radius_draw_value` for the radius)
  must be between 1:4 and 4:1 to ensure reasonable cone proportions.
  Ratios outside this range are rejected.
- `height` must not be an empty string.
- `height` must be numeric (integer or single-decimal). Variable labels
  are not supported.
- Numeric and string inputs are accepted for `height`; numeric inputs
  are automatically converted to strings.

---

## Example JSON Inputs

### Example 1

```json
{
  "height": "10",
  "units": "cm",
  "detail": "ice_cream_cone",
  "radius_draw_value": 4.0
}
```

Draws an ice cream cone with height 10 cm (bracket on the left). The
cone width is determined by `radius_draw_value` (4.0). No other
dimension labels are shown.

### Example 2

```json
{
  "height": "4.5",
  "units": "in.",
  "detail": "ice_cream_cone",
  "radius_draw_value": 2.0
}
```

Draws an ice cream cone with height 4.5 in. (bracket on the left). The
cone width is determined by `radius_draw_value` (2.0). No other
dimension labels are shown.

- The question stem must follow this format: "The diagram shows the height of an ice cream cone that has a volume of $[value]$ cubic [units]. Which measurement is closest to the radius of the cone in [units]?"
- All answer options must follow this format: "$[value]$ [units]"
- Be sure to set "detail": "ice_cream_cone" in the stimulus description

C. 1.0 in.
D. 0.5 in.

# Stimulus Function: draw_labeled_sphere

## Overview

Generates a single 2D outline of a sphere with a labeled diameter. The
sphere is drawn as a circle with a dashed equator ellipse to convey 3D
perspective and a dashed horizontal diameter line spanning the full width.
The dimension label sits centred on the diameter line with a white
background box.

Key characteristics:
- Single sphere per image.
- Dashed equator ellipse (both front and back halves) for 3D depth cue.
- Dashed horizontal diameter line from edge to edge.
- Label centred on the diameter line with a white background.
- Labels can be integers, single-decimal numbers, or LaTeX variables.
- All labels include units.
- Optional **marble** detail that fills the sphere with a gradient and
  glossy white highlights to resemble a glass marble.

**Output of these specifications:** A single **JSON object** that can be
passed to the drawing function (and used to instantiate `LabeledSphere`).
Do not produce Python code or other formats — only valid JSON.

---

## Variable Label Guidance

When the diameter label is a variable, the `diameter_draw_value` field
should be provided to control the drawing proportions (a sensible default
of 6.0 exists, but explicit values are preferred to match the intended
visual).

---

## JSON Object Specification

The input to the function is one JSON object. All keys are strings. Types
and requirements below.

### Required Keys

| Key | JSON type | Description |
|-----|-----------|-------------|
| `diameter` | `string` | Label for the diameter dimension. May be an integer (`"15"`), a number with one decimal place (`"8.5"`), or a LaTeX variable (`"d"`). |
| `units` | `string` | Unit of measurement appended to the label (e.g. `"cm"`, `"in."`, `"mm"`). |

### Optional Keys

| Key | JSON type | Default | Description |
|-----|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `diameter_draw_value` | `number` | `6.0` | Numeric value used for drawing proportions when the diameter label is a variable. Must be > 0 and ≤ 20. Ignored when diameter is numeric. |
| `detail` | `string` | `null` | Visual detail type. Currently supports `"marble"` which fills the sphere with a gradient and glossy white highlights so it resembles a glass marble. When omitted or `null`, draws a plain outline sphere. |

### Validation Rules

- `diameter` must not be an empty string.
- Numeric and string inputs are accepted for `diameter`; numeric inputs
  are automatically converted to strings.

---

## Detail Types

### `"marble"`

When `detail` is `"marble"`, the sphere is styled to resemble a glass
marble:
- A light gray base fill covers the sphere interior.
- Subtle radial shading darkens the edges.
- Three white highlight patches (upper-left, upper-center, lower-left)
  simulate specular reflections.
- The sphere outline is drawn in a slightly softer dark gray.

---

## Example JSON Inputs

### Example 1: Basic integer diameter

```json
{
  "diameter": "15",
  "units": "in."
}
```

Draws a plain sphere outline with diameter 15 in. labelled at the centre.

### Example 2: Decimal diameter

```json
{
  "diameter": "8.5",
  "units": "cm"
}
```

Draws a sphere with diameter 8.5 cm.

### Example 3: Variable diameter

```json
{
  "diameter": "d",
  "units": "m",
  "diameter_draw_value": 10.0
}
```

Draws a sphere with a variable diameter *d* m. The `diameter_draw_value`
controls the visual size of the sphere.

### Example 4: Marble with integer diameter

```json
{
  "diameter": "13",
  "units": "mm",
  "detail": "marble"
}
```

Draws a marble-styled sphere with diameter 13 mm. The sphere has a
gradient fill and glossy highlights.

### Example 5: Marble with variable diameter

```json
{
  "diameter": "x",
  "units": "cm",
  "diameter_draw_value": 8.0,
  "detail": "marble"
}
```

Draws a marble-styled sphere with a variable diameter *x* cm. The
`diameter_draw_value` sets the visual size.

# Stimulus Function: draw_labeled_rectangular_prism (To Scale)

## Overview

Generates a single 3D rectangular prism rendered as a wireframe with black
edges and labeled dimensions. Visible edges are drawn as solid lines and
hidden edges (back-left vertical, bottom-back, bottom-left depth) are drawn
as dashed lines. Faces are white/unfilled so the wireframe is clearly
visible.

The prism is drawn proportionally to the provided dimension values — the
visual shape faithfully represents the relative magnitudes of length, width,
and height.

All three dimensions (length, width, height) are labeled automatically
using the dimension value and the unit of measurement.

Key characteristics:
- Black wireframe style — no colored fill on faces.
- Solid visible edges, dashed hidden edges.
- Dimension labels showing the numeric value and unit.
- Float dimensions (supports decimals like 7.5, 6.5).
- Drawn to scale — the shape proportions match the dimension values.

**Output of these specifications:** A single **JSON object** that can be
passed to the drawing function (and used to instantiate
`LabeledRectangularPrism`). Do not produce Python code or other formats —
only valid JSON.

---

## JSON Object Specification

### Required Keys

| Key | JSON type | Description |
|-----|-----------|-------------|
| `length` | `number` | The length (front bottom edge) of the prism. Must be between 0.1 and 100. |
| `width` | `number` | The width (side/depth edge) of the prism. Must be between 0.1 and 100. |
| `height` | `number` | The height (vertical edge) of the prism. Must be between 0.1 and 100. |
| `unit_label` | `string` | Unit of measurement appended to every label (e.g. `"in."`, `"cm"`, `"ft"`, `"m"`). |

---

## Example JSON Inputs

### Example 1: Basic prism with integer dimensions

```json
{
  "length": 16,
  "width": 8,
  "height": 10,
  "unit_label": "in."
}
```

Draws a to-scale rectangular prism with length 16 in., width 8 in., and
height 10 in.

### Example 2: Prism with decimal dimensions

```json
{
  "length": 7.5,
  "width": 5,
  "height": 6.5,
  "unit_label": "in."
}
```

Draws a to-scale rectangular prism with length 7.5 in., width 5 in., and
height 6.5 in.

### Example 3: Cube-like prism in centimeters

```json
{
  "length": 4,
  "width": 4,
  "height": 4,
  "unit_label": "cm"
}
```

Draws a to-scale cube with all edges 4 cm.

### Example 4: Tall narrow prism

```json
{
  "length": 8,
  "width": 4,
  "height": 20,
  "unit_label": "in."
}
```

Draws a tall, narrow prism with length 8 in., width 4 in., and
height 20 in.

### Example 5: Flat wide prism in feet

```json
{
  "length": 12,
  "width": 10,
  "height": 3,
  "unit_label": "ft"
}
```

Draws a flat, wide prism with length 12 ft, width 10 ft, and height 3 ft.
Question Requirements: - The question stem must follow this format: "A rectangular prism and its dimensions are shown in the diagram. What is the total surface area of this prism in square [units]?"

# Stimulus Function: draw_labeled_rectangular_prism (Not to Scale)

## Overview

Generates a single 3D rectangular prism rendered as a wireframe with black
edges and labeled dimensions. Visible edges are drawn as solid lines and
hidden edges (back-left vertical, bottom-back, bottom-left depth) are drawn
as dashed lines. Faces are white/unfilled so the wireframe is clearly
visible.

The prism is **not drawn to scale** — a normalised shape is used regardless
of the actual dimension values. This means extreme ratios between
dimensions (e.g. length 100, width 2, height 50) still produce a
clear, readable diagram. The dimension labels always display the actual
values provided.

All three dimensions (length, width, height) are labeled automatically
using the dimension value and the unit of measurement.

Key characteristics:
- Black wireframe style — no colored fill on faces.
- Solid visible edges, dashed hidden edges.
- Dimension labels showing the numeric value and unit.
- Float dimensions (supports decimals like 7.5, 6.5).
- Not drawn to scale — the shape uses a normalised form while labels
  reflect the actual dimension values.

**Output of these specifications:** A single **JSON object** that can be
passed to the drawing function (and used to instantiate
`LabeledRectangularPrism`). Do not produce Python code or other formats —
only valid JSON.

---

## JSON Object Specification

### Required Keys

| Key | JSON type | Description |
|-----|-----------|-------------|
| `length` | `number` | The length (front bottom edge) of the prism. Must be between 0.1 and 100. |
| `width` | `number` | The width (side/depth edge) of the prism. Must be between 0.1 and 100. |
| `height` | `number` | The height (vertical edge) of the prism. Must be between 0.1 and 100. |
| `unit_label` | `string` | Unit of measurement appended to every label (e.g. `"in."`, `"cm"`, `"ft"`, `"m"`). |
| `drawn_to_scale` | `boolean` | **Must always be `false`**. The prism shape is normalised so that extreme dimension ratios still produce a readable diagram. |

---

## Example JSON Inputs

### Example 1: Large disparity between dimensions

```json
{
  "length": 30,
  "width": 2,
  "height": 39,
  "unit_label": "cm",
  "drawn_to_scale": false
}
```

Draws a normalised prism shape. Labels display the actual values: 100 cm,
2 cm, and 50 cm.

### Example 2: Prism with decimal dimensions

```json
{
  "length": 3.5,
  "width": 12.25,
  "height": 0.5,
  "unit_label": "in.",
  "drawn_to_scale": false
}
```

Draws a normalised prism shape. Labels display 3.5 in., 12.25 in., and
0.5 in.

### Example 3: Very tall prism

```json
{
  "length": 5,
  "width": 5,
  "height": 30,
  "unit_label": "ft",
  "drawn_to_scale": false
}
```

Draws a normalised prism shape. Labels display 5 ft, 5 ft, and 80 ft.

### Example 4: Large prism in meters

```json
{
  "length": 25,
  "width": 39,
  "height": 15,
  "unit_label": "m",
  "drawn_to_scale": false
}
```

Draws a normalised prism shape. Labels display 25 m, 40 m, and 15 m.

### Example 5: Small prism in millimeters

```json
{
  "length": 0.3,
  "width": 0.8,
  "height": 0.1,
  "unit_label": "mm",
  "drawn_to_scale": false
}
```

Draws a normalised prism shape. Labels display 0.3 mm, 0.8 mm, and
0.1 mm.

---

## Prism Dimension Pairs with Equal Total Surface Area

The following 20 pairs of rectangular prism dimensions produce the **same
total surface area** despite having different length, width, and height
values. Surface area formula: **SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)**.

All dimensions are ≤ 40 and decimals use only the tenths place.

| Pair | SA (sq units) | Prism A (l × w × h) | Prism B (l × w × h) |
|------|---------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 52 | 1 × 2 × 8 | 2 × 3 × 4 |
| 2 | 126 | 3 × 3 × 9 | 3 × 5 × 6 |
| 3 | 144 | 3 × 6 × 6 | 4 × 4 × 7 |
| 4 | 192 | 4 × 4 × 10 | 5 × 6 × 6 |
| 5 | 378 | 6 × 9 × 9 | 7 × 7 × 10 |
| 6 | 384 | 6 × 6 × 13 | 8 × 8 × 8 |
| 7 | 480 | 7 × 10 × 10 | 8 × 8 × 11 |
| 8 | 594 | 8 × 11 × 11 | 9 × 9 × 12 |
| 9 | 586 | 7 × 11 × 12 | 8 × 9 × 13 |
| 10 | 858 | 10 × 13 × 13 | 11 × 11 × 14 |
| 11 | 1000 | 10 × 10 × 20 | 10 × 14 × 15 |
| 12 | 1008 | 11 × 14 × 14 | 12 × 12 × 15 |
| 13 | 1170 | 12 × 15 × 15 | 13 × 13 × 16 |
| 14 | 1344 | 13 × 16 × 16 | 14 × 14 × 17 |
| 15 | 1530 | 14 × 17 × 17 | 15 × 15 × 18 |
| 16 | 1728 | 15 × 18 × 18 | 16 × 16 × 19 |
| 17 | 1938 | 16 × 19 × 19 | 17 × 17 × 20 |
| 18 | 72 | 2 × 2 × 8 | 2 × 3 × 6 |
| 19 | 76 | 1 × 2 × 12 | 2 × 4 × 5 |
| 20 | 100 | 1 × 2 × 16 | 2 × 4 × 7 |

### JSON for each pair

**Pair 1** — SA = 52 sq units

```json
{"length": 1, "width": 2, "height": 8, "unit_label": "in.", "drawn_to_scale": false}
{"length": 2, "width": 3, "height": 4, "unit_label": "in.", "drawn_to_scale": false}
```

```json
{"length": 5, "width": 6, "height": 6, "unit_label": "in.", "drawn_to_scale": false}
```

**Pair 5** — SA = 378 sq units
