  - **“The perimeter of the larger [figure] is [blank-2] times the perimeter of the smaller [figure].”**
- **Blank 1 (area):** **$k^2$** (decimal to hundredths when needed, e.g. **70.56**).
- **Blank 2 (perimeter):** **$k$** (e.g. **8.4**).
- **Do NOT** use drop-down menus or MCQ.
- **Do NOT** ask for actual area or perimeter values — only **multipliers**.
Example Question:
Two parallelograms are similar. The dimensions of the larger parallelogram are 8.4 times the corresponding dimensions of the smaller parallelogram.

Complete the sentences about the area and perimeter of the two parallelograms.

The area of the larger parallelogram is <blank-1> times the area of the smaller parallelogram.

The perimeter of the larger parallelogram is <blank-2> times the perimeter of the smaller parallelogram.

Answer: 70.56; 8.4

Lesson Title: Loan Cost: Interest Rate and Length
Substandard Description: Solve real-world problems comparing how interest rate and loan length affect the cost of credit.
Task: Compare total simple interest at two banks and determine which charges less and by how much.
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST describe a borrower who wants to borrow a stated **principal** (dollars, may include cents) for a real-world purchase.
- The stem MUST give **two loan offers** as bullet points or sentences, each naming a **bank** (or lender), a **loan length in years**, and a **simple interest rate** as a **percent** — **2025 Item 34**.
- The question MUST ask: **“At which bank will [name] pay less interest over the life of the loan, and how much less interest will he [or she] pay?”**
- **Each answer option** MUST be a **complete two-sentence statement** matching the STAAR logic:
  - **“[Name] will pay less interest at [Bank ___].”**
  - **“He [or She] will pay $[amount] less interest over the life of the loan.”**
- **Exactly four options:** **two options for Bank X** (or the first bank named) and **two options for Bank Y** (or the second bank named).
  - **One correct option:** the bank with **less total interest** and the **difference** between the two total interest amounts (rounded to the **nearest cent** when needed).
  - **One distractor per bank:** pair that bank with the **wrong dollar amount** — typically that bank's **own total interest** instead of the **difference**, or the **correct difference** paired with the **wrong bank** (**2025 Item 34** family).
- **Do NOT** use compound interest or compare more than two banks.
Example Question:
Mr. Jiménez wants to borrow $6,850.00 from a bank to buy new furniture.

Bank X offers a 5-year loan at 3.5% simple interest.
Bank Y offers a 3-year loan at 4.75% simple interest.

At which bank will Mr. Jiménez pay less interest over the life of the loan, and how much less interest will he pay?

A. Mr. Jiménez will pay less interest at Bank X. He will pay $1,198.75 less interest over the life of the loan.

B. Mr. Jiménez will pay less interest at Bank X. He will pay $222.63 less interest over the life of the loan.

C. Mr. Jiménez will pay less interest at Bank Y. He will pay $976.13 less interest over the life of the loan.

D. Mr. Jiménez will pay less interest at Bank Y. He will pay $222.63 less interest over the life of the loan.

Correct: D

Substandard Description: Calculate and compare simple interest and compound interest earnings.
Task: Calculate simple interest earned and total balance for a one-time deposit at a given rate and time.
Allowed Content Types: Text Entry Question with multiple blanks
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST describe a **one-time deposit** of **$P** into an account that earns **$r$% simple interest**, with **no additional deposits or withdrawals**
- Instruction MUST be empty
- **Two text-entry blanks** embedded in one **completion sentence**:
  - **“After [t] years, [name]'s [account type] will have earned $<blank-1> in interest and will have a total balance of $<blank-2>.”**
- **Blank 1:** interest earned (**$I$**); **Blank 2:** total balance (**$A = P + I$**).
- **Do NOT** use drop-down menus, MCQ, or compound-interest scenarios.
- **Do NOT** ask students to compare simple vs. compound interest — that belongs in **TEKS.MATH.CONTENT.8.12.D+1**.
- Generated items MUST vary name, principal, rate, and time; **2025 Q2** is **format only**.
Example Question:
Janice deposits $4,500 into an investment account that earns 4% simple interest. She makes no additional deposits or withdrawals.

After 5 years, Janice's investment account will have earned [blank-1] in interest and will have a total balance of [blank-2].

Answer: $900; $5,400

Substandard Description: Estimate the cost of a two-year and four-year college education and devise a periodic savings plan.
Task: Determine equal monthly savings under two conditional funding scenarios for a real-world savings goal.
Example Question:
Desmond wants to attend a college that costs $15,000 for the first year. Desmond's family has saved $6,000, and he has applied for a $1,500 scholarship. To have the full amount needed to pay for the year's tuition, his family will set aside a fixed amount of money each month for the next 12 months.

How much should Desmond's family save each month for the next 12 months to have enough money to pay for his first year of college?

Move the correct answer to each box. Not all answers will be used.

$500
$625
$750
$1,125
$1,250

If Desmond wins the scholarship, his family will need to save a minimum of [ ] per month.

If Desmond does not win the scholarship, his family will need to save a minimum of [ ] per month.

Correct: with scholarship = $625; without scholarship = $750

Lesson Title: Identifying Real Numbers Greater than or Less than Given Values
Substandard Description: Identify numbers greater than a given rational number.
Task: Select all numbers with a value greater than or less than a given benchmark when the stem asks a direct comparison question.
Question Requirements:
- Items are **Multi Select** with **“Select TWO correct answers.”** (bold **TWO** as in STAAR).
- The stem MUST be **“Which numbers have a value greater than [benchmark]?”** where the benchmark may be **$\pi$**, a **fraction**, or a **decimal** — **2024 Item 37**.
- **Do NOT** use the inequality stem (**“Which values of $x$ make the inequality true?”**) — that belongs in **LO-2**.
- **Five** answer options; each option is a **single value** (fraction, decimal, or square root). **No** sentences as options.
- **Correct** options are **all and only** values **strictly greater than** the benchmark.
- **Distractors** SHOULD include: values **less than** the benchmark; a **decimal close to but less than** the benchmark (e.g. **$3.14$** vs **$\pi$**); **perfect-square roots** below the threshold.
- Generated items MUST vary the benchmark and option values; the `example_question` mirrors **2024 Item 37** for **format only**.
Example Question:
Which numbers have a value greater than $\pi$?

Select TWO correct answers.

A. $\frac{8}{3}$

B. $3.14$

C. $\frac{23}{7}$

D. $\sqrt{9}$

E. $\sqrt{10}$

Correct: C, E

Lesson Title: Identifying Real Numbers Greater than or Less than Given Values
Substandard Description: Identify numbers greater than a given rational number.
Task: Select all values of x that make a given inequality true when options are real numbers in mixed forms.
Question Requirements:
- Items are **Multi Select** with **“Select THREE correct answers.”** (bold **THREE** as in STAAR).
- The stem MUST be **“Which values of $x$ make the inequality true?”** followed by an inequality such as **$x > \frac{28}{5}$** on its own line
- **Do NOT** use the direct-comparison stem (**“Which numbers have a value greater than …?”**)
- **Six** answer options; each option is a **single value** (fraction, decimal, square root, or **$a\sqrt{b}$**). **No** sentences as options.
- **Correct** options are **all and only** values **strictly greater than** the benchmark in the inequality.
- **Distractors** SHOULD include: values **less than** the benchmark; **negative** values when the benchmark is positive; **decimals close to but less than** the benchmark (e.g. **$5.28$** vs **$5.6$**).
- Generated items MUST vary the inequality and option values; the `example_question` mirrors **2023 Item 18** for **format only**.
Example Question:
Which values of $x$ make the inequality true?

$x > \frac{28}{5}$

Select THREE correct answers.

A. $\sqrt{34}$

B. $-8$

C. $\frac{19}{3}$

D. $4.7$

E. $2\sqrt{11}$

F. $5.28$

Correct: A, C, E

Lesson Title: Slope and Y-Intercept From a Table
Substandard Description: Use data from a table to determine the slope and y-intercept in mathematical and real-world problems.
Task: Determine the slope and y-intercept of a linear relationship from a table in a mathematical context.
Allowed Content Types: Text Entry Question with multiple blanks
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST begin **“The table shows a linear relationship between the variables $x$ and $y$.”** followed by an **HTML table** with **at least three** $(x, y)$ pairs - **Table format (required — HTML, not markdown):** - `` - `` with one ``; each header in `` with one `` per data row; each value in `
` - `
` - Column headers MUST be **$x$** and **$y$** - The question stem MUST be ""The table shows a linear relationship between the variables x and y. 
<> 
What are the slope and y-intercept of the linear relationship?
 The slope of the linear relationship is , and the y-intercept is "
Example Question:
The table shows a linear relationship between the variables $x$ and $y$.
$x$ 5 7 11
$y$ 16.5 21.5 31.5
What are the slope and $y$-intercept of the linear relationship? What are the slope and y-intercept of the linear relationship? The slope of the linear relationship is , and the y-intercept is Answer: 2.5; 4

Lesson Title: Slope and Y-Intercept From a Table
Substandard Description: Use data from a table to determine the slope and y-intercept in mathematical and real-world problems.
Task: Determine the rate and initial deduction from a real-world linear table.
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST describe a **real-world** situation modeled by a linear relationship in the form **$y = mx - b$**, where: - **$x$** represents the **input quantity** (e.g. hours worked, items sold, tickets sold, weeks, months, or another countable quantity). - **$y$** represents the **resulting total** after a **one-time amount** has been subtracted. - **$m$** is the **rate of change**. - **$b$** is the **one-time deduction**, fee, cost, or starting offset that is subtracted from the total. - The context MUST involve a quantity increasing at a **constant rate** while a **one-time amount** is deducted or removed. Examples include: earnings after a uniform cost is deducted; profit after a booth fee is paid; account balance after a one-time fee is charged; fundraising total after supply costs are deducted; ticket revenue after a setup cost is subtracted; paycheck amount after an equipment fee is taken out. - The stem MUST **define both variables**, **$x$** and **$y$**, in context. - The stem MUST include an **HTML table** with **at least three** data values showing the relationship between **$x$** and **$y$**. - **Table format (required — HTML, not markdown):** horizontal **two-row** layout. **Column-style** tables with `` and one row per data point **MUST NOT** be used. - Optional title: `
[context-appropriate table name]
` - `` - `` with **exactly two** `` rows: - **Row 1:** row header for the **input variable** (with **$x$** label), followed by **$x$** values as centered `
` cells. - **Row 2:** row header for the **output variable** (with **$y$** label and units if needed), followed by **$y$** values as centered `` cells. - Row headers: `` - Data cells: `` - Both rows MUST have the **same number of cells** (one header + one cell per data point). - The question MUST ask for the **rate of change in context** and the **one-time deduction, fee, cost, or starting offset**. Wording MUST be context-specific, e.g.: - “Based on the table, what is the hourly wage and what is the cost of the uniform?” - “Based on the table, what is the amount earned per ticket and what is the booth fee?” - “Based on the table, what is the amount added each week and what is the one-time fee?” - “Based on the table, what is the rate per item and what is the supply cost?” - Instruction MUST include: **“Move the correct answer to each box. Not all answers will be used.”** - **Six** draggable answer choices. If the context involves money, use **$__.__** format; otherwise use context-appropriate numeric values with units as needed. - **Two** inline drop boxes: one for the **rate of change** (with a context unit such as **per hour**, **per ticket**, **per item**, **per week**, or **per month**) and one for the **one-time deduction, fee, cost, or starting offset**. - **Correct** values: the **rate of change** from the table and the **absolute value** of the negative **$y$-intercept** (the one-time deduction). - **Distractors** SHOULD include: a **$y$-value** from the table; an **$x$-value** from the table; an incorrect rate from dividing a **$y$-value** by an **$x$-value**; a **decimal-place error**; the **final amount** instead of the one-time deduction.
Example Question:
Eva is starting a new job. She earns an hourly wage, and the cost of her uniform is taken out of her first paycheck. She makes a table showing different numbers of hours she could work, $x$, and the amount that her first paycheck would be, $y$.
First Paycheck Amounts
Number of Hours Worked, $x$ 2 5 7 9 12
First Paycheck Amount, $y$ (dollars) $3.50 $46.25 $74.75 $103.25 $146.00
Based on the table, what is Eva's hourly wage and what is the cost of her uniform? Move the correct answer to each box. Not all answers will be used. $2.00 $3.50 $9.25 $12.00 $14.25 $25.00 Hourly wage: [ ] per hour Uniform cost: [ ] Correct: hourly wage = $14.25; uniform cost = $25.00

Lesson Title: Slope and Y-Intercept From a Graph
Substandard Description: Use data from a graph to determine the slope and y-intercept in mathematical problems.
Task: Determine the slope and y-intercept of a linear function from its graph on a coordinate grid.
Stimulus Specification:
Stimulus Type Description for draw_linear_function_graph

The draw_linear_function_graph function generates a coordinate plane image with a straight line passing through two marked points. It draws:
- A square coordinate grid with x and y axes, arrowheads, and tick labels
- A straight line extending across the full visible grid area
- Two filled circle markers at the specified points
- Coordinate labels next to each point (e.g., "(3, 2)")

Required Format:
{
    "point1_x": int,               // Required: x-coordinate of the first point (must be 0)
    "point1_y": int,               // Required: y-coordinate of the first point
    "point2_x": int,               // Required: x-coordinate of the second point
    "point2_y": int,               // Required: y-coordinate of the second point
    "grid_range": int,             // Optional: axis range (default 10, grid shows ±(grid_range-1))
    "line_color": str,             // Optional: color of the line (default "black")
    "point_color": str,            // Optional: color of the point markers (default "black")
    "show_grid": bool,             // Optional: whether to draw grid lines (default true)
    "show_point_labels": bool      // Optional: whether to show "(x, y)" labels next to points (default true)
}

Constraints:
- point1_x, point1_y, point2_x, point2_y: Must be integers between -10 and 10 (inclusive)
- The two points must be different (not identical)
- The line cannot be vertical (point1_x and point2_x cannot both be the same value)
- grid_range: Must be between 5 and 20 (default 10)
- line_color / point_color: MUST be "black"
- show_point_labels: MUST be TRUE

Example 1 - Orange line with labeled points (EASY difficulty):
{
    "point1_x": 0,
    "point1_y": -1,
    "point2_x": 3,
    "point2_y": 2,
    "grid_range": 10,
    "line_color": "orange",
    "point_color": "black",
    "show_grid": true,
    "show_point_labels": true
}
Allowed Content Types: Text Entry Question with multiple blanks with stimulus
Question Requirements:
- This question includes a **coordinate-grid graph stimulus** showing a **linear function**. - The stem MUST be **“The graph of a linear function is shown on the coordinate grid.
What are the slope and the $y$-intercept of the function?

Slope:
y-intercept: (,).” - The graph MUST show a **straight line** with **at least two labeled points** on the line (e.g. **$(-4, -3)$** and **$(4, -1)$** in **2023 Item 24**). - **Three text-entry blanks (multi blank):** - **slope:** one blank; accepts a **whole number**, **decimal**, or **fraction** (e.g. **$\frac{1}{4}$**). - **$y$-intercept:** ordered-pair format **$( [blank] , [blank] )$** — one blank for the **$x$-coordinate** and one for the **$y$-coordinate**. - The correct **$y$-intercept** MUST be **$(0, b)$** where **$b$** is the value where the line crosses the **$y$-axis**.
Example Question:
The graph of a linear function is shown on the coordinate grid. What are the slope and the $y$-intercept of the function? Enter numbers in the boxes provided.

<<alt: Coordinate grid from $-5$ to $5$ on both axes. A line passes through labeled points $(-4, -3)$ and $(4, -1)$. The line crosses the $y$-axis at $(0, -2)$.>>

Slope: [blank-1]

$y$-intercept: ( [blank-2] , [blank-3] )

Answer: 1/4; 0; -2

Lesson Title: Represent Real-World Linear Proportional Situations as Equations
Substandard Description: Equations Represent real world linear proportional situations with equations.
Task: Select the equation in the form $y = kx$ that models a real-world linear proportional relationship.
Question Requirements:
- Stem MUST describe a **real-world** context where a **total** corresponds to a **count** (or equivalent pair) at a **constant per-unit rate**, and state that each unit costs/changes the **same amount**.
- Stem MUST define the **dependent** and **independent** variables (e.g. **$c$** = total cost in dollars, **$n$** = number of oranges) and ask **“Which function can be used to find …?”** or equivalent 
- **Answer options (all four):** Each option MUST be a single **equation** in the form **(dependent) $=$ (constant)$\times$(independent)** using the **same variable names** as the stem. **Do NOT** use tables, graphs, or sentences as options.
- **Correct** option: constant $k = \text{total} \div \text{count}$ (unit rate).
- **Distractor patterns** (**2023 Item 39** family): **total as $k$** (B: $c = 2.52n$); **count as $k$** (C: $c = 6n$); **product instead of quotient** (D: $c = 15.12n$).
- **No** $+b$ term in any option.
- Generated items MUST vary context, variable names, and numbers; 
- **Correct: [A-D]**.
Example Question:
Jerri paid $2.52 for 6 oranges. Each orange costs the same amount. Which function can be used to find the total cost in dollars, $c$, for purchasing $n$ oranges?

A. $c = 0.42n$

B. $c = 2.52n$

C. $c = 6n$

D. $c = 15.12n$

Correct: A

Substandard Description: Solve problems involving direct variation.
Task: Find the missing value in a symbolic directly proportional relationship between $x$ and $y$.
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST state that **the value of $y$ is directly proportional to the value of $x$** (or equivalent STAAR wording).
- The stem MUST give **one** ordered pair ($x$, $y$) and ask for **$y$** when a **new** $x$ value is given.
- **Easy:** whole-number $x$ and $y$ in the given pair; correct answer a whole number.
- **Hard:** may use fractions and mixed numbers in the stem as in **2024 Q23**.
- **Answer options (all four):** Each option MUST be a **whole number**, **proper fraction**, or **mixed number** only. **Do NOT** use decimal numerals (e.g., no **75.0**, no **37.5**). **Do NOT** use **$** or units on options.
- The `example_question` mirrors **2024 Q23** for **format only**; generated items MUST change numeric values while keeping option **types** (whole / fraction / mixed) and distractor roles.
Example Question:
The value of $y$ is directly proportional to the value of $x$. When $x = \frac{3}{4}$, $y = 56\frac{1}{4}$. What is the value of $y$ when $x = \frac{1}{2}$?

A. 75
B. $37\frac{1}{2}$
C. 56
D. $84\frac{3}{8}$

Correct: B

Substandard Description: Solve problems involving direct variation.
Task: Find the missing value in a real-world direct variation word problem when answer choices are decimal numerals (not money).
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST describe a **real-world** context where one quantity **varies directly with** another (not a **cost in dollars** primary question—use **LO-3** for money options).
- The stem MUST give **one** known pair and ask for the missing amount when a **new** value of the other quantity is given.
- **Answer options (all four):** Each option MUST be a **decimal numeral** (tenths, hundredths, or thousandths as appropriate). **Do NOT** use **$** on any option. **Do NOT** use fractions or mixed numbers as answer options (e.g., no **$37\frac{1}{2}$**, no **$\frac{3}{4}$**).
- When the correct value is a whole number, you MAY show it as a whole numeral (e.g., **6**) or with a decimal point (e.g., **6.0**) consistent across options for that item.
- You MAY include a **context unit** on each option when the quantity is not money (e.g., **lawns**, **hours**, **gallons**), matching STAAR style.
- **Easy:** whole-number givens; decimal options without **$**.
- **Medium/Hard:** decimals in the givens or target (e.g., **7.5** hours).
Example Question:
Malik owns a lawn-mowing business. The number of lawns he mows varies directly with the number of hours he works. On Tuesday he mowed 4 lawns in 5 hours.

How many lawns can Malik mow on Wednesday if he works for 7.5 hours?

A. 6.5
B. 10
C. 6
D. 9.375

Correct: C

Substandard Description: Solve problems involving direct variation.
Task: Find the missing value in a real-world direct variation word problem when every answer choice is a dollar amount.
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST describe a **real-world** context where **cost (or charge) in dollars** varies directly with another quantity (e.g., kilowatt-hours, pounds purchased).
- The stem MUST give **one** known **dollar** amount paired with a usage/count and ask for the **cost** (or **charge**) for a **new** usage/count.
- **Answer options (all four):** **Every** option MUST include a leading **$** and show money to the **hundredths** (e.g., **$108.96**). **Do NOT** use non-money decimal options without **$** (those belong in **LO-2**).
- **Do NOT** use whole numbers, fractions, or mixed numbers without **$** and hundredths on options.
- **Hard:** may use **four-digit** usage counts and **hundredths** in the given cost (**2022 Q40**).
- Example mirrors **2022 Q40** for **format only**; vary context and numbers in generation.
Example Question:
The cost for electricity varies directly with the amount of kilowatt-hours of electricity used. The cost for using 1,079 kilowatt-hours of electricity was $129.48.

What was the cost for using 908 kilowatt-hours of electricity?

A. $41.52
B. $153.86
C. $75.67
D. $108.96

Correct: D

Substandard Description: Solve problems involving direct variation.
Task: Find the missing value in a real-world direct variation word problem with a numeric griddable response.
Question Requirements:
- **Allowed difficulties: Medium and Hard only** — **no Easy** Text Entry items.
- The stem MUST describe a **real-world** context where one quantity **varies directly with** another.
- The stem MUST give **one** known pair and ask for the missing amount when a **new** value of the other quantity is given.
- The answer MUST be a **single numeric value** for the griddable response (no **$** in the grid unless a released item requires it; **2017/2018 Q34** use whole-number counts only).
- The `example_question` mirrors **2018 Q34** for **format only**; generated items MUST vary contexts and numeric values.
Example Question:
The amount of water an electric dishwasher uses to wash dishes varies directly with the number of loads of dishes. The dishwasher uses 32 gallons of water to wash 4 loads of dishes.

How many gallons of water will the dishwasher use to wash 10 loads of dishes?

Answer: 80

The number of gift baskets Nikki can make varies directly with the amount of time she spends making the baskets. She can make 4 baskets in $\frac{1}{2}$ hour.

How many baskets can Nikki make in 5 hours?
Answer: 40

Lesson Title: Write a Real-World Problem From a Given Equation
Substandard Description: Write a corresponding real-world problem when given a one-variable equation with variables on both sides of the equal sign.
Task: Select the real-world situation that matches a one-variable equation with variables on both sides of the equal sign.
Question Requirements:
- Stem MUST show **“Which situation can be represented by this equation?”** (or equivalent) and a **one-variable equation with the variable on both sides of the equal sign** — e.g. **$px + q = rx$** or **$px = q + rx$**. **Never** give an equation with the variable on only one side.
- The story MUST compare **two** changing quantities (each involving **rate $\times$ $x$**) and may include a **one-time** amount (head start, deposit, starting balance) on **one** side.
- **Correct** option: each **coefficient of $x$** matches a **rate per unit** in the story; any **constant** matches the **one-time** amount on the **correct side**; the question asks when totals are **the same**.
- **Distractor patterns** (combine families from **2018 Item 26** and **2022 Item 42**): constant on the **wrong side**; **two rates with no** one-time amount; **deposit paired with the wrong rate**; **swapped** coefficients; **wrong meaning of $x$**; **percent/discount** instead of a flat constant.
- Four options **A.–D.** full sentences
- Generated items MUST vary names, contexts, and numbers; examples mirror released items for **format only**.
Example Question:
Which situation can be represented by this equation?

$7x + 1 = 10x$

A. Brody ran at a constant rate of 7 miles per hour in a race. Candice ran at a constant rate of 10 miles per hour in a race and started 1 mile in front of the starting point. What is $x$, the number of hours that Brody and Candice would have to run in order for their distance to be the same?

B. Brody played a game where he scored 8 points per hour. Candice played the game and scored 10 points per hour. What is $x$, the number of hours that Brody and Candice would each have to play the game in order for their scores to be the same?

C. Brody went to two different amusement parks. The first park charged $7 per hour and gave a 1% discount for showing a student ID. The other park charged $10 per hour. What is $x$, the number of hours that Brody would have to stay at each park to have to pay the same amount?

D. Brody rode his bicycle on two different days. The first day he rode 7 miles per hour and started 1 mile from his house. The second day he started from his house and rode 10 miles per hour. What is $x$, the number of hours that Brody would have to ride his bicycle to travel the same distance on both days?

Correct: D

Lesson Title: Write a Real-World Problem From a Given Inequality
Substandard Description: Write a corresponding real-world problem when given a one-variable equation or inequality with variables on both sides of the equal sign, using rational number coefficients and constants.
Task: Select the real-world situation that matches a one-variable inequality with variables on both sides of the inequality sign.
Question Requirements:
- The item MUST show a **one-variable inequality with the variable on both sides** (e.g. **$5x < 3x + 2$**). **Never** use an inequality with the variable on only one side. **Never** use an **equation**.
- **Stem pattern** **One or two context sentences first** that define the variable (e.g. *At a store each notebook has a cost of $x$ dollars.*), **then** **“Which situation can be represented by this inequality?”** and the inequality.
  - Do **not** require every item to use the same stem wording; both patterns are valid.
- Each side of the inequality MUST map to a **real-world total**: typically **(count) $\times$ (unit cost/rate)** plus or minus an optional **fixed amount**.
- **Correct** option: **counts** match coefficients of the variable; **fixed amount** uses **$+$ or $-$** as in the inequality; **comparison words** match the symbol (**less than** / **greater than** / **at most** / **at least** for $<$, $>$, $\leq$, $\geq$).
- **Distractor patterns** (**2019 Item 25** family): **reversed inequality** (A: $>$ for $<$); **wrong sign on constant** (B: coupon **subtracts** instead of pen **adds**); **equation wording** (D: “$2$ greater than” for $=$); **swapped counts/rates**.
- Four options **A.–D.** full sentences.
- Generated items MUST vary contexts, counts, constants, and **stem pattern** 
- **Correct: [A-D]**.
Example Question:
At a store each notebook has a cost of $x$ dollars. Which situation can be represented by this inequality?

$5x < 3x + 2$

A. The cost of 5 notebooks is greater than the cost of 3 notebooks plus a $2 pen.

B. The cost of 5 notebooks is less than the cost of 3 notebooks with a $2-off coupon.

C. The cost of 5 notebooks is less than the cost of 3 notebooks plus a $2 pen.

D. The cost of 5 notebooks is $2 greater than the price of 3 notebooks.

Correct: C

Lesson Title: Solve Equations With Variables on Both Sides
Substandard Description: Solve equations with variables on both sides.
Task: Solve a one-variable equation that has the variable on both sides of the equal sign, using rational number coefficients and constants.
Allowed Content Types: MCQ with Worked Example, Text Entry Question
Common Misconceptions:
- Subtracts the variable terms instead of adding when collecting them on one side.
- Distributes the factor to only the first term inside the parentheses.
- Makes a sign error when dividing both sides by a negative coefficient.
- Adds the variable coefficients instead of subtracting when combining like terms.
- Divides both sides by the constant instead of the coefficient.
- Subtracts a constant from one side instead of adding it to both.
Question Requirements:
- The equation MUST contain exactly one variable, and that variable MUST appear on BOTH sides of the equal sign.
- The equation MUST use only integer or fraction coefficients and constants (no decimal coefficients or constants).
- The equation MUST appear on its own line directly beneath the question stem.
- The question stem MUST use one of these STAAR phrasings verbatim: "What value of x makes this equation true?", "What value of x makes the equation true?", or "What is the solution to this equation?"
- The stem MUST NOT tell the student which method or operation to use to solve the equation.
- For MCQ items, the answer options MUST be candidate values of the variable, using 3 or 4 options total. Each distractor MUST be a value produced by a single STAAR-style error that actually applies to the given equation: always include a sign error from subtracting the variable terms instead of adding when collecting variables on one side, and the reciprocal value from inverting the final division. Include a distribution error (factor applied to only the first term inside the parentheses) ONLY when the equation has parentheses, and a combining-like-terms error ONLY when a side has like terms to combine; when neither applies, use a sign error from failing to change a constant's sign when moving it across the equal sign. Adapt only the numbers to the new equation.
- For Text Entry items, the answer MUST be a single integer or fraction value of the variable.
Example Question:
MCQ:
What value of x makes this equation true?
10x - 8 = 4 - 6x

A. 3/4
B. 3
C. 4/3
D. -1/4

Correct: A

Text Entry:
What is the solution to this equation?
3x + 5 = x - 9

Answer: -7
Additional Worked Examples:
Solve for x: $4(x - 2) = 6x + 10$ Step 1: Simplify each side using the distributive property and combining like terms
$4(x - 2) = 6x + 10$Use the distributive property: $4(x - 2) = 4x - 8$.
$4x - 8 = 6x + 10$Simplify.
Step 2: Apply inverse operations to both sides to solve for x
$4x - 6x - 8 = 10$Subtract $6x$ from both sides.
$-2x - 8 = 10$Simplify.
$-2x - 8 + 8 = 10 + 8$Add $8$ to both sides.
$-2x = 18$Simplify.
$\dfrac{-2x}{-2} = \dfrac{18}{-2}$Divide both sides by $-2$.
$x = -9$
The solution is $x = -9$.

Lesson Title: Write Equations With Variables on Both Sides
Substandard Description: Write one-variable equations with variables on both sides.
Task: Write a one-variable equation with the variable on both sides that represents a real-world word problem.
Common Misconceptions:
- Reverses coefficient/constant (multiplies the fixed amount by the variable) 
- Pairs each starting amount with the other scenario's rate 
- Multiplies every term, including the starting amount, by the variable
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST describe two scenarios, each with a fixed starting amount and a constant per-unit rate (for example, per month, per week, or per day).
- Write the stem in this exact structure so it reads naturally for an 8th grader:
  (1) One framing sentence naming both subjects and the shared activity. Example: "Maria and Tom are each saving money."
  (2) One sentence per subject, in parallel structure, giving that subject's starting amount and per-unit rate. Example: "Maria has $40 saved and saves $15 each week. Tom has $25 saved and saves $20 each week."
  (3) The closing question in this exact form: "Which equation can be used to find [variable], the number of [units] it will take [subject A] and [subject B] to have the same [quantity]?" Example: "Which equation can be used to find w, the number of weeks it will take Maria and Tom to have saved the same amount of money?"
- The variable MUST appear on both sides of the equation. Each side MUST consist of one constant term (the starting amount) and one variable term (the rate times the variable), which may appear in either order.
- Each answer option MUST be a complete equation. The three distractors MUST each use a different one of these STAAR-grounded error patterns, adapting only the numbers: (a) reverse the coefficient and constant roles by multiplying the fixed starting amount by the variable and leaving the rate as a constant; (b) pair each starting amount with the other scenario's rate; (c) multiply every term, including the starting amounts, by the variable; (d) apply the multiply-all error to one scenario's expression and the reverse-roles error to the other.
Example Question:
Maria and Tom are each saving money. Maria has $40 saved and saves $15 each week. Tom has $25 saved and saves $20 each week. Which equation can be used to find w, the number of weeks it will take Maria and Tom to have saved the same amount of money?
A. 40 + 15w = 25 + 20w
B. 40w + 15 = 25w + 20
C. 40 + 20w = 25 + 15w
D. 40w + 15w = 25w + 20w
Correct answer: A

Lesson Title: Write Equations With Variables on Both Sides
Substandard Description: Write one-variable equations with variables on both sides.
Task: Write a one-variable equation with the variable on both sides that represents the perimeter of a geometric figure shown in a labeled diagram.
Stimulus Specification:
STIMULUS DESCRIPTION — draw_polygon_with_string_sides

OVERVIEW
Draws a single polygon (triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon ... up to a decagon) whose sides are labeled with strings. Labels render verbatim, so they can be plain numbers ("12"), algebraic expressions ("7x", "(4g + 12)"), or any text. Use this for write-an-equation / perimeter problems where side measures are unknowns (e.g. TEKS.MATH.CONTENT.8.8.A: "the perimeter is 17x; find the equation"). For triangles (3 sides) only, you can explicitly control the shape and side-congruence tick marks, independently of the labels, because a label like "7x" cannot be turned into a real length.

FUNCTION
draw_polygon_with_string_sides(side_data: PolygonStringSides) -> str  (returns image file path)

INPUT FIELDS (PolygonStringSides)
- side_lengths: list of 3 to 10 strings. REQUIRED. One per side, in order around the polygon. Examples: "15", "7x", "(x + 5)".
- side_labels: list of strings or null. Optional (default null). Custom text drawn on each side; if omitted, side_lengths is used as the labels. Use "" to show no label on a side.
- unit: string or null. Optional (default ""). Appended after every label, e.g. "units", "cm", "in.".
- triangle_shape: one of "auto", "isosceles", "scalene", "equilateral". Optional (default "auto"). TRIANGLES ONLY; ignored for 4+ sides.
- triangle_proportions: list of exactly 3 positive floats or null. Optional (default null). TRIANGLES ONLY. Relative visual side lengths [side0, side1, side2]; must satisfy the triangle inequality; overrides the shape implied by triangle_shape.
- tick_marks: list of ints or null. Optional (default null). Congruence ticks per side; length must equal side_lengths. 0 = none, 1/2/3 = single/double/triple. Equal counts read as congruent sides.

triangle_shape VALUES (triangles only)
- "auto" (default): legacy behavior. Shape derived from side_lengths if numeric; algebraic labels fall back to a near-equilateral FILLED, COLORED shape with white rounded label boxes. Use only for backward compatibility / numeric sides.
- "isosceles": apex-up triangle. side_lengths[0] and [1] are the two equal LEGS; side_lengths[2] is the BASE. Clean thin black outline, no fill, borderless labels.
- "scalene": three visibly different sides, apex up. Clean black outline, no fill, borderless labels.
- "equilateral": three equal sides. Clean black outline, no fill, borderless labels.
Rule of thumb: for the modern STAAR-style look, set triangle_shape to isosceles/scalene/equilateral (or pass triangle_proportions). Leave "auto" only if you want the old filled look.

WHAT THE OUTPUT (DRAWING) LOOKS LIKE
- A single polygon from your side_lengths/labels, each side's text placed just OUTSIDE its edge.
- Clean triangle modes (isosceles/scalene/equilateral, or triangle_proportions): thin BLACK outline, NO fill, NO box around labels; the base label sits close to the bottom edge; apex points up.
- tick_marks: small perpendicular dashes at each marked side's midpoint (one/two/three); equal counts signal congruent sides (e.g. the two legs of an isosceles triangle).
- Legacy "auto" and all 4+ sided polygons: a colored, semi-transparent FILLED polygon with each label inside a white rounded box.
- The function returns the saved image file path (WebP by default).
- Not to scale: the shape is a clear schematic. Visual proportions come from triangle_proportions (if given) or sensible defaults, never from parsing algebraic labels.

EXAMPLES (JSON)
1) Isosceles perimeter triangle (TEKS 8.8.A reference): two equal legs "15 units" (tick-marked), base "7x units".
{"side_lengths": ["15", "15", "7x"], "unit": "units", "triangle_shape": "isosceles", "tick_marks": [1, 1, 0]}
2) Scalene with algebraic sides.
{"side_lengths": ["2x", "3x", "(x + 5)"], "unit": "units", "triangle_shape": "scalene"}
3) Equilateral, all sides marked congruent.
{"side_lengths": ["s", "s", "s"], "unit": "cm", "triangle_shape": "equilateral", "tick_marks": [1, 1, 1]}
4) Scalene with exact visual proportions.
{"side_lengths": ["a", "b", "c"], "side_labels": ["3x", "4x", "5x"], "unit": "in.", "triangle_shape": "scalene", "triangle_proportions": [3.0, 4.0, 5.0]}
5) Quadrilateral / pentagon (no triangle fields needed).
{"side_lengths": ["(6g + 6)", "(3g + 9)", "(6g + 6)", "(3g + 9)"], "unit": "cm"}

CHOOSING INPUTS
1. Count sides; put each side's measure in side_lengths in order.
2. Triangle? Pick triangle_shape: two equal -> "isosceles" (equal legs are the first two entries); all different -> "scalene"; all equal -> "equilateral".
3. Mark congruent sides? Set tick_marks (same count on congruent sides).
4. Need a specific visual size relationship? Add triangle_proportions.
5. Units? Set unit once; it is appended to every label.
6. Need different display text than the length value? Use side_labels.

COMMON MISTAKES TO AVOID
- Expecting algebraic labels to size the shape; they do not. Use triangle_shape / triangle_proportions.
- tick_marks length not equal to side_lengths length -> validation error.
- triangle_proportions with non-positive values or violating the triangle inequality (e.g. [1, 1, 5]) -> validation error.
- Using triangle_shape/triangle_proportions/tick_marks on a 4+ sided polygon; they are silently ignored.
- Leaving triangle_shape "auto" when you want the clean borderless STAAR look; "auto" gives the old filled shape with boxed labels.
- For an isosceles triangle, putting the base first; the equal legs must be the first two side_lengths entries, base is third.
Common Misconceptions:
- Combines a constant side length and a variable term as if like terms 
- Places the variable on the sum of the constant side lengths 
- Drops the variable from a term, treating a coefficient as a constant
Question Requirements:
- The question includes a diagram stimulus of a triangle with each side length labeled.
- Write the stem in this exact structure:
  (1) State the perimeter as a variable expression. Example: "The perimeter of the triangle shown is 14x units."
  (2) Add this sentence verbatim: "The dimensions of the triangle are given in units."
  (3) The closing question in this exact form: "Which equation can be used to find the value of [variable]?" Example: "Which equation can be used to find the value of x?"
- Two of the three side lengths MUST be whole numbers and the third side MUST be a single variable term (for example, 4x). The correct equation sets the given perimeter expression equal to the sum of the three side lengths, with the whole-number sides combined into one constant. Example: "17x = 30 + 7x".
- Each answer option MUST be a complete equation with the given perimeter expression on the left side. The three distractors MUST use these error patterns, adapting only the numbers: (a) combine one whole-number side length with the variable term as if they were like terms, leaving the other constant side; (b) place the variable on the sum of the two whole-number side lengths instead of on the third side; (c) add a whole-number side length to the variable term's coefficient as a constant and attach the variable to a side length.
Example Question:
The perimeter of the triangle shown is 17x units. The dimensions of the triangle are given in units. Which equation can be used to find the value of x?
[Diagram: a triangle with side lengths labeled 15 units, 15 units, and 7x units.]
A. 17x = 30 + 7x
B. 17x = 15 + 22x
C. 17x = 7 + 30x
D. 17x = 22 + 15x
Correct answer: A

Lesson Title: Write Inequalities With Variables on Both Sides
Substandard Description: Write one-variable inequalities with variables on both sides.
Task: Write a one-variable inequality with the variable on both sides that represents a real-world word problem comparing two quantities.
Common Misconceptions:
- Reverses the inequality symbol when interpreting greater than or less than.
- Treats a decreasing quantity's rate as added instead of subtracted.
- Pairs a starting amount with the other scenario's per-unit rate.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST describe two scenarios, each with a fixed starting amount and a constant per-unit rate (for example, per hour, per week, or per mile). At least one scenario MAY decrease over time, in which case its quantity is the starting amount minus the rate times the variable.
- The question stem MUST end by asking which inequality represents the situation when one quantity is greater than (or less than) the other, with the variable defined as the number of units (hours, weeks, etc.).
- The comparison may be strict ('greater than' / 'less than' -> > or <) or inclusive ('at least' / 'no fewer than' -> >=, 'at most' / 'no more than' -> <=); the correct inequality MUST use the symbol that matches the phrasing.
- The variable MUST appear on both sides of the inequality, and one side MAY be a single variable term with no constant.
- Each answer option MUST be a complete inequality. For an item in which a quantity decreases, distractors MUST mirror these STAAR error patterns, adapting only the numbers: (a) keep the correct expressions but reverse the inequality symbol; (b) add the decreasing quantity's rate times the variable instead of subtracting it, keeping the correct symbol; (c) both reverse the symbol and add instead of subtract. For an all-increasing item, distractors MUST instead: (a) keep the correct expressions but reverse the inequality symbol; (b) pair each starting amount with the other scenario's per-unit rate; (c) both pair the starting amounts with the other scenario's rate and reverse the inequality symbol.
Example Question:
A storage tank contains 1,200 gallons of water, and 40 gallons of water leak from the tank each hour. At the same time, an empty container is filled with water at a rate of 500 gallons each hour. Which inequality represents all possible values for x, the number of hours that have passed when the tank contains less water than the container?

A. 1200 + 40x > 500x
B. 1200 - 40x > 500x
C. 1200 - 40x < 500x
D. 1200 + 40x < 500x

Correct: C

Lesson Title: Write Inequalities With Variables on Both Sides
Substandard Description: Write one-variable inequalities with variables on both sides.
Task: Write a one-variable inequality with the variable on both sides by entering the starting amounts and rates into a scaffolded template for a real-world word problem.
Allowed Content Types: Text Entry Question with multiple blanks
Common Misconceptions:
- Swaps the constant and coefficient, entering each value in the wrong blank.
- Pairs a starting amount with the other scenario's per-unit rate.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST describe two real-world scenarios, each with a fixed starting amount and a constant per-unit rate, with both quantities increasing over time.
- The question stem MUST end by asking the student to write an inequality for the minimum number of units, x, for which the first quantity is greater than the second.
- The question MUST present a scaffolded template of the form [ ] + [ ]x > [ ] + [ ]x with four numeric entry blanks; the inequality symbol and the variable x are part of the template and are NOT entered by the student.
- Each blank MUST accept a single numeric value (whole number or decimal). The two starting amounts go in the constant blanks and the two per-unit rates go in the coefficient blanks, keeping each scenario on its own side so the variable appears on both sides.
Example Question:
Lena can rent a moving truck from Company A for $55.00 plus $0.70 per mile. Lena can rent a moving truck from Company B for $68.00 plus $0.58 per mile. Write an inequality that represents the minimum number of miles, x, for which the cost of renting a moving truck from Company A is greater than the cost of renting a moving truck from Company B.

Enter your answers in the boxes provided: [ ] + [ ]x > [ ] + [ ]x

Answer: 55 + 0.70x > 68 + 0.58x

Lesson Title: Write Inequalities With Variables on Both Sides
Substandard Description: Write one-variable inequalities with variables on both sides.
Task: Select the one-variable inequality with the variable on both sides that compares two labeled side lengths of a geometric figure shown in a diagram.
Stimulus Specification:
STIMULUS DESCRIPTION — draw_rectangle_labeled_sides

OVERVIEW
Draws a single rectangle ABCD, NOT to scale, whose four sides can be independently labeled with verbatim text. Labels render exactly as supplied: plain numbers ("12"), algebraic expressions ("9x - 16", "1.5x + 42"), or with units ("9x - 16 units"). Use this for write-an-equation / one-variable inequality problems on a rectangle, e.g. TEKS.MATH.CONTENT.8.8.A: "Rectangle ABCD is shown with the dimensions given in units. The length of AB is greater than the length of AD. Which inequality represents this situation?" The figure is decoupled from the label values (NOT to scale) so students reason from the labels, not the picture.

FUNCTION
draw_rectangle_labeled_sides(desc: RectangleLabeledSides) -> str  (returns image file path)

VERTEX / CORNER MAPPING (clockwise from top-left)
A = top-left, B = top-right, C = bottom-right, D = bottom-left.
Sides map to edges as: top = AB, right = BC, bottom = DC, left = AD.

INPUT FIELDS (RectangleLabeledSides)
- top_label: string or null. Optional (default null). Verbatim label for the top side (AB). null or "" = unlabeled.
- right_label: string or null. Optional (default null). Verbatim label for the right side (BC). null or "" = unlabeled.
- bottom_label: string or null. Optional (default null). Verbatim label for the bottom side (DC). null or "" = unlabeled.
- left_label: string or null. Optional (default null). Verbatim label for the left side (AD). null or "" = unlabeled.
- vertex_labels: list of exactly 4 strings, or null. Optional (default null -> ["A","B","C","D"]). Corner labels clockwise from top-left: [top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left]. Must be length 4 if provided.
- show_right_angles: bool. Optional (default true). Draw a small square right-angle marker at every corner.
- show_vertex_labels: bool. Optional (default true). Draw the four vertex labels just outside the corners.
- unit: string or null. Optional (default null). Appended to each non-empty side label; NOT double-appended if the label already ends with it.
- aspect_ratio: float > 0 or null. Optional (default null -> 1.15). Width/height of the drawn rectangle (visual only, not to scale). Use only to keep the figure clearly rectangular and labels legible.

UNIT HANDLING
Two equivalent ways to get "9x - 16 units": (a) top_label="9x - 16" plus unit="units", or (b) top_label="9x - 16 units" (with unit set or null). No double "units".

WHAT THE OUTPUT (DRAWING) LOOKS LIKE
- A single rectangle with a thin BLACK outline, NO fill, white background, no axes/ticks/grid.
- Right-angle (square) markers at all four corners (default on; toggle with show_right_angles).
- Vertex labels just outside each corner, italic (default A,B,C,D; toggle with show_vertex_labels).
- Side labels rendered verbatim, BORDERLESS (no boxes), centered just outside their edge: top above, bottom below, left to the left, right to the right. Sides left null/empty show no label.
- This default look is the STAAR-style render.
- NOT to scale: proportions come from aspect_ratio (default about 1.15, slightly wider than tall), never from the label values. Do not expect "AB > AD" to be reflected visually; that relationship lives in the labels/text only.
- The function returns the saved image file path (WebP by default).

EXAMPLES (JSON)
1) Reference case (TEKS 8.8.A): label only top + left.
{"top_label": "9x - 16", "left_label": "1.5x + 42", "unit": "units"}
2) All four sides labeled.
{"top_label": "2x + 5", "bottom_label": "2x + 5", "left_label": "3x", "right_label": "3x", "unit": "cm"}
3) Plain rectangle, no markers, no vertex letters.
{"top_label": "12", "left_label": "8", "show_right_angles": false, "show_vertex_labels": false}
4) Custom vertex labels + unit baked into the labels.
{"vertex_labels": ["P", "Q", "R", "S"], "top_label": "5x units", "left_label": "2x + 1 units"}

CHOOSING INPUTS
1. Which sides does the problem give? Set only those labels (top_label/left_label/etc.); leave the rest null.
2. The default look is correct for most STAAR items; just pass the labels.
3. Units: set unit once, or bake into each label; do not do both.
4. Custom corner names (e.g. PQRS, WXYZ)? Provide vertex_labels (exactly 4, clockwise from top-left).
5. Hide markers/letters (rare): set show_right_angles / show_vertex_labels to false.
6. Need a different rectangle proportion for legibility? Set aspect_ratio (purely visual; never encodes the real measures).

COMMON MISTAKES TO AVOID
- Expecting the picture to show "AB > AD" or any size relationship; it is NOT to scale, so that info must be in the labels / question text.
- Providing vertex_labels with a length other than 4 -> validation error.
- Double units (e.g. left_label="3x cm" AND unit="cm"); it is de-duplicated, but pick one approach to stay clear.
- Wrong corner order in vertex_labels; it must be clockwise from top-left: [top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left].
- Confusing which label is which side: top=AB, right=BC, bottom=DC, left=AD.
- Using this for triangles/other polygons; use draw_polygon_with_string_sides instead.
Common Misconceptions:
- Reverses the inequality symbol when interpreting greater than or less than.
- Adds the coefficients and combines the constants, forming a pre-solved inequality.
Question Requirements:
- The question includes a diagram stimulus of a geometric figure with two side lengths, each already labeled as a linear expression in x; the student does NOT construct the expressions.
- The question stem MUST state which side is greater (or less) than the other and MUST end by asking which inequality represents the situation.
- Across the bank, the correct option MUST sometimes be the original expressions and sometimes a correct simplified equivalent, so the answer cannot be identified by form alone; no distractor may be mathematically equivalent to the correct answer.
- Each answer option MUST be a complete inequality. Distractors MUST mirror these STAAR error patterns, adapting only the numbers: (a) keep the correct expressions but reverse the inequality symbol; (b) a pre-solved form that illegally combines the two variable terms and the two constants (for example, adding the coefficients and combining the constants) with the correct symbol; (c) the same pre-solved form with the symbol reversed.
Example Question:
Rectangle ABCD is shown. The length of AB is greater than the length of AD. Which inequality represents this situation?

<<alt: Rectangle ABCD with side AB labeled 8x - 12 units and side AD labeled 2x + 30 units.>>

A. 8x - 12 < 2x + 30
B. 8x - 12 > 2x + 30
C. 10x < 18
D. 10x > 18

Correct: B
Additional Worked Examples:
Rectangle ABCD is shown. The length of AB is greater than the length of AD. Which inequality represents this situation?
<<alt: Rectangle ABCD with side AB labeled 8x - 12 units and side AD labeled 2x + 30 units.>>

Step 1: Identify each quantity and the expression that represents it
The diagram shows side AB as 8x - 12 units and side AD as 2x + 30 units.

Step 2: Write the inequality that compares the two expressions
The length of AB being greater than the length of AD means 8x - 12 > 2x + 30.

The inequality is 8x - 12 > 2x + 30.

Lesson Title: Solve Equations With Variables on Both Sides: Real-Word Problems
Substandard Description: Problems Solve real word problems involving equations with variables on both sides.
Task: Solve a one-variable equation with the variable on both sides that is presented directly, either as a given equation or as two equal algebraic expressions, using rational number coefficients and constants.
Allowed Content Types: MCQ with Worked Example, Text Entry Question
Common Misconceptions:
- Applies an add or subtract step to only one side.
- Adds fractional coefficients instead of subtracting them.
- Uses the wrong reciprocal when isolating the variable.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST present the algebra directly: either an equation already shown in the stem with the variable on both sides, OR two algebraic expressions stated to be equal (for example, two angle measures that are equal).
- The equation or expressions MUST contain exactly one variable that appears on BOTH sides of the equal sign, with rational coefficients and constants (integers, fractions, or decimals).
- When the equation is shown, it MUST appear on its own line beneath the stem, and the stem MUST NOT tell the student which method or operation to use.
- The stem MUST ask for the value of the variable using STAAR phrasing such as "What is the value of x?" or "What is the solution to this equation?" (quote the STAAR wording verbatim when adapting a STAAR item).
- For MCQ items, the answer options MUST be candidate values of the variable. Each distractor MUST be the value produced by a single STAAR-grounded error, choosing only patterns that apply to the given equation: (a) applying an add or subtract step to only one side of the equation; (b) adding the variable terms instead of subtracting them (or vice versa) when collecting them on one side; (c) on the final isolation step, dividing by the wrong number or using the wrong reciprocal. Adapt only the numbers to the new equation.
- For Text Entry items, the answer MUST be a single rational value of the variable.
Example Question:
MCQ:
The measures of two angles are (1/8 x + 61) degrees and (3/4 x - 27) degrees. The angle measures are equal. What is the value of x?

A. 704/5
B. 272/7
C. 704/7
D. 272/5

Correct: A

Text Entry:
Classroom A had 70 students and Classroom B had 30 students. Classroom A sent groups of 4 students to Classroom B until both classrooms had the same number of students. The equation 70 - 4x = 30 + 4x can be used to find the number of groups Classroom A sent to Classroom B. How many groups did Classroom A send to Classroom B?
Additional Worked Examples:
The measures of two angles are $(2x + 30)^\circ$ and $(4x - 10)^\circ$. The angle measures are equal. What is the value of $x$? A. $10$ B. $20$ C. $40$ D. $30$ Step 1: Solve for $x$.
2x+30=4x−102x + 30 = 4x - 10Set the two angle measures equal.
2x+30−2x=4x−10−2x2x + 30 - 2x = 4x - 10 - 2xSubtract 2x from both sides.
30=2x−1030 = 2x - 10Simplify.
30+10=2x−10+1030 + 10 = 2x - 10 + 10Add 10 to both sides.
40=2x40 = 2xSimplify.
402=2x2\displaystyle\frac{40}{2} = \displaystyle\frac{2x}{2}Divide both sides by 2.
x=20x = 20
The value of $x$ is $20$, which matches choice B.

Lesson Title: Solve Equations With Variables on Both Sides: Real-Word Problems
Substandard Description: Problems Solve real word problems involving equations with variables on both sides.
Task: Write and solve a one-variable equation with the variable on both sides to represent a real-world or mathematical word problem with two parallel quantities (no diagram).
Allowed Content Types: MCQ with Worked Example, Text Entry Question
Common Misconceptions:
- Writes an equation with the wrong operation from the context.
- Moves a term with the wrong sign when solving the equation.
- Uses the wrong inverse operation or stops before the final step.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST describe a real-world or mathematical situation with two parallel quantities (two people, two groups, two containers, or one geometric measure equal to another) whose described amounts are stated to be equal.
- The stem MUST give enough information for the student to write an equation with the variable on both sides; the equation MUST NOT be shown in the stem.
- All numbers in the stem MUST be written as digits, and coefficients and constants MUST be rational (integers, fractions, or decimals such as money amounts or rates).
- The stem MUST end with a question asking for the value the situation requires (for example, the number given away, the cost of one item, or a length), phrased in the context of the problem.
- For MCQ items, the answer options MUST be candidate values for the requested quantity. Each distractor MUST be the value produced by a single STAAR-grounded error, choosing only patterns that apply to the situation: (a) writing the expression with the wrong operation from the context, such as using addition instead of subtraction for an amount given away, or treating a draining rate as a filling rate; (b) moving a term with the wrong sign while solving, such as subtracting a variable term that should be added or adding a constant that should be subtracted; (c) adding the two variable coefficients instead of subtracting one to collect the terms; (d) solving for the other quantity in the problem rather than the one asked; (e) using the wrong inverse operation on the final step or stopping before the final divide. Adapt only the numbers and context.
- For Text Entry items, the answer MUST be a single rational value; when the answer is an amount of money, it MUST be expressed in dollars and cents.
Example Question:
MCQ:
Two student groups went to an amusement park on the same day. Group 1 bought 9 tickets and received a $120 discount. Group 2 bought 3 tickets and received a $30 discount. Both groups spent the same total amount of money on tickets. The price of each ticket was the same. What was the cost of each ticket?

A. $25
B. $12.50
C. $15
D. $7.50

Correct: C

Text Entry:
Two customers spent the same total amount of money at a restaurant. The first customer bought 8 hot wings and left a $4 tip. The second customer bought 10 hot wings and left a $2.80 tip. Both customers paid the same amount per hot wing. How much does one hot wing cost at this restaurant in dollars and cents?

Answer: 0.60
Additional Worked Examples:
Mia started with 24 stickers and gave away $x$ stickers. Leo started with 40 stickers and gave away twice as many stickers as Mia. They had the same number of stickers left. How many stickers did Mia give away? A. $8$ B. $32$ C. $16$ D. $20$ Step 1: Write an expression for each amount and set them equal. Mia started with 24 stickers and gave away $x$ stickers, so subtract $x$ from 24: $24 - x$. Leo started with 40 stickers and gave away twice as many stickers, so subtract $2x$ from 40: $40 - 2x$. $24 - x = 40 - 2x$ Step 2: Solve for $x$.
24−x=40−2x24 - x = 40 - 2xStart with the equation.
24−x+2x=40−2x+2x24 - x + 2x = 40 - 2x + 2xAdd 2x to both sides.
24+x=4024 + x = 40Simplify.
24+x−24=40−2424 + x - 24 = 40 - 24Subtract 24 from both sides.
x=16x = 16Simplify.
Mia gave away 16 stickers, which matches choice C.

Lesson Title: Solve Equations With Variables on Both Sides: Real-Word Problems
Substandard Description: Problems Solve real word problems involving equations with variables on both sides.
Task: Write and solve a one-variable equation with the variable on both sides from a geometric figure shown in a diagram, using labeled side lengths or angle measures.
Common Misconceptions:
- Uses the wrong multiplier for a figure’s perimeter.
- Distributes a factor to only one term.
- Adds variable terms instead of subtracting them.
- Omits a labeled side when writing the perimeter.
Question Requirements:
- The question MUST include a diagram stimulus (drawn by
  draw_equal_perimeter_shapes) showing two shapes chosen from triangle, square,
  and rectangle, with every side labeled by a linear expression in the single
  variable x using rational coefficients and constants.
- FIGURE COMPOSITION (mandatory): exactly ONE labeled side across the whole
  diagram MUST require the distributive property — a numeric multiplier applied
  to a parenthesized binomial, e.g. 2(x + 4). Every other labeled side MUST be a
  monomial (e.g. 3x, 7) or a simple grouped binomial (e.g. (2x - 1)); do NOT
  introduce a second distributive (multiplier-times-parenthesis) instance. In
  addition, at least one of the two shapes MUST be a square or a rectangle, so
  that writing its perimeter requires a perimeter multiple (×4 for a square;
  2L + 2W for a rectangle).
- The stem MUST state the relationship that makes the two measures equal (for
  example, "The perimeter of the triangle is equal to the perimeter of the
  square shown in the diagram."), MUST reference the diagram (for example,
  "shown in the diagram"), and MUST end with "What is the value of x?".
- The stem MUST NOT show the equation and MUST NOT tell the student which
  method or operation to use; the student writes the equation from the figure.
- SOLUTION VALIDITY (mandatory): choose the labeled expressions and the keyed
  value of x together so that, when x is substituted back: the equation has a
  unique solution; x is a positive integer; every labeled side on every figure
  is strictly positive (> 0); and every triangle satisfies the strict triangle
  inequality (sum of the two shorter sides strictly greater than the longest).
  Verify all of these by substitution BEFORE finalizing; if any fails, change
  the expressions or choose a different x and recheck.
- The answer options MUST be exactly four candidate values of x: one correct key
  plus three distractors. All four MUST be distinct, positive, rational values.
- Each distractor MUST be the value produced by applying exactly ONE of the
  following STAAR-grounded errors to THIS figure (use the three that apply):
  (a) using the wrong multiple for a figure's perimeter (for example,
      multiplying a square's side by 2 instead of 4, or forgetting to double a
      rectangle's length and width);
  (b) failing to distribute the multiplier to every term inside the
      parentheses (for example, treating 2(x + 4) as 2x + 4);
  (c) adding the variable terms instead of subtracting them when collecting them
      on one side.
  Adapt only the numbers to the figure. Do NOT use any other error type, random
  values, or arithmetic slips.
- The worked example MUST: (1) write each shape's perimeter from its labeled
  sides, applying the correct perimeter multiple and distributing where needed;
  (2) set the two perimeters equal (the equation MAY appear here — only the STEM
  must omit it); (3) solve step by step with one operation per step to reach x;
  and (4) state which lettered choice matches.
- DIFFICULTY: Medium = small positive-integer coefficients/constants, the single
  distribution on a triangle side, a square as the second shape, single-digit
  integer solution. Hard = larger coefficients/constants and/or a rectangle as
  the second shape (perimeter 2L + 2W), still exactly one distribution and a
  positive-integer solution.
Example Question:
The side lengths in yards of a triangle and a square are shown in the diagram. The perimeter of the triangle is equal to the perimeter of the square. What is the value of x?

>

A. -4
B. 2/3
C. 6
D. 5/2

Correct: C
Additional Worked Examples:
The side lengths in inches of a triangle and a square are shown in the diagram. 3x in(2x - 1) in2(x + 4) in2x in The perimeter of the triangle is equal to the perimeter of the square. What is the value of $x$? A. $x = 1$ B. $x = 8$ C. $x = 14$ D. $x = 7$ Step 1: Write an equation setting the two perimeters equal. Add the three triangle sides for its perimeter: $3x + (2x - 1) + 2(x + 4)$. The square's perimeter is 4 times its side: $4(2x)$ Set the perimeters equal. $3x + (2x - 1) + 2(x + 4) = 4(2x)$ Step 2: Solve for $x$.
3x+(2x−1)+2(x+4)=4(2x)3x + (2x - 1) + 2(x + 4) = 4(2x)Start with the equation.
7x+7=8x7x + 7 = 8xDistribute, then combine like terms.
7x+7−7x=8x−7x7x + 7 - 7x = 8x - 7xSubtract 7x from both sides.
7=x7 = xSimplify.
The value of $x$ is $7$, which matches choice D.

Lesson Title: Model Equations With Variables on Both Sides
Substandard Description: Objective: Model equations with variables on both sides.
Task: Find the value of x from an algebra-tile model of a linear equation with variables on both sides.
Stimulus Specification:
STIMULUS: draw_equation_model_tiles
PURPOSE: Draws a static algebra-tile model of an equation so a student can read and solve it. Shows the left side, an equals sign, and the right side using x-tiles (bars) and unit-tiles (squares). Supports positive AND negative tiles, so it works for one-sided models (e.g. 3x + 2 = 8) and two-sided models with negatives (e.g. -3x + 5 = -x + 1).

WHAT TO SEND (input fields):
- type: always the string "draw_equation_model_tiles".
- left_terms: a non-empty list describing the left side. Each entry is an object {"kind": <tile type>, "count": <how many>}.
- right_terms: same structure for the right side. Also non-empty.
- kind (per term) is one of: "x" = positive variable tile (white bar labeled x); "unit" = positive 1-tile (white square labeled 1); "neg_x" = negative variable tile (solid GRAY bar labeled -x); "neg_unit" = negative 1-tile (solid GRAY square labeled -1).
- count (per term): integer 1 to 30. Each side's total tiles must be at least 1 and at most 40.
- IMPORTANT: negative tiles ("neg_x"/"neg_unit") are ONLY supported when layout = "equation" and layout_variant = "generic". Use those for any model that has negatives.
- layout (optional, default "equation"): one of "equation", "panels", "balance".
- layout_variant (optional, default "generic"): use "generic" for the normal auto-layout renderer. Other STAAR presets exist ("triangle_equation", "balance_scale", "panel_3x_plus_1", "panel_2x_plus_5", "row_equation") but only "generic" supports negatives.
- x_shape (optional, default "bar"): "bar", "tile", or "cube".
- x_orientation (optional, default "vertical"): "vertical" or "horizontal".
- unit_shape (optional, default "square"): "square", "triangle", or "circle".
- show_key (optional, default true): if true, draws a small key/legend explaining what x and 1 mean. Set false to hide it.
- show_panel_borders (optional, default true): if true, draws container boxes around each side. Set false for a borderless model.
- max_items_per_row (optional, default 8): integer 2 to 12. Caps how many tiles appear per visual row.
- x_label (optional, default "x"): 1 to 6 chars; the text printed inside x-tiles (negatives auto-show "-"+label).
- unit_label (optional, default "1"): 1 to 6 chars; the text printed inside unit-tiles.

WHAT YOU GET BACK (output):
A SINGLE image. It shows the left-side tiles, a large equals sign, then the right-side tiles. Positive tiles are white with a black outline; negative tiles are solid gray and labeled with a minus sign. On each side, x-bars are drawn in one row and unit-squares are arranged in a compact grid beside the bars (the grid height is capped by the bar height; if a side has only units they sit in a single row up to max_items_per_row). If show_key is true a legend is added; if show_panel_borders is true each side is boxed.

EXAMPLE INPUT (two-sided with negatives, -3x + 5 = -x + 1):
{"type":"draw_equation_model_tiles","layout":"equation","layout_variant":"generic","left_terms":[{"kind":"neg_x","count":3},{"kind":"unit","count":5}],"right_terms":[{"kind":"neg_x","count":1},{"kind":"unit","count":1}],"show_key":false,"show_panel_borders":false}
Question Requirements:
- The question MUST include an algebra-tile model stimulus that is required to answer the question.
- The algebra-tile model MUST represent a linear equation with exactly one variable, x, appearing on both sides of the equal sign.
- The stimulus MUST be an algebra-tile model that represents the equation with x-tiles and unit tiles; each side MUST have at most 3 x-tiles and at most 8 unit tiles.
- The stem MUST use one of these STAAR phrasings verbatim: "What is the value of x in the equation represented by the tiles?" or "A model representing a linear equation is shown. Based on the model, what is the value of x?"
- The stem MUST NOT write the equation or tell the student which operation to use; students must infer the equation from the tile model.
- For MCQ items, the answer options MUST be candidate values of x. Each distractor MUST be produced by a STAAR-grounded error pattern that fits the generated model: (a) canceling x-tiles incorrectly and combining remaining unit tiles; (b) stopping at ax = b or -x = b without completing the division step; (c) counting total x-tiles and total unit tiles across both sides as one equation; (d) subtracting constants in the wrong order; (e) dividing by the wrong number or using the reciprocal in the final division. Most distractors should reflect one error; the -5/3-style distractor may combine subtracting constants in the wrong order with inverting the final division, matching the 2024 STAAR rationale. Adapt only the tile counts and resulting values.
- Answer options MUST use the same numeric format as the correct answer: integers for integer-solution items and simple fractions for fractional-solution items.
Example Question:
A model representing a linear equation is shown. Based on the model, what is the value of x?

<<alt: Algebra tiles show 2 positive x-tiles and 4 positive unit tiles on the left side, and 3 negative x-tiles and 7 positive unit tiles on the right side.>>

A. 3/5
B. -5/3
C. -3/5
D. 5/3

Correct: A
Worked Example Stimulus Specification:
STIMULUS: draw_equation_model_tiles_two_sided_we
PURPOSE: Generates a step-by-step WORKED EXAMPLE that solves a linear equation with the variable on BOTH sides (form: a*x + b = d*x + e) using algebra tiles and the two-phase zero-pair method. It emits ONE image per solving step so a student can follow the full solution visually.

WHAT TO SEND (input fields):
- type: always the string "draw_equation_model_tiles_two_sided_we".
- left_coefficient: signed integer -10 to 10, NONZERO. This is "a" (coefficient of x on the left).
- left_constant: signed integer -10 to 10, NONZERO. This is "b" (constant on the left).
- right_coefficient: signed integer -10 to 10, NONZERO and DIFFERENT from left_coefficient. This is "d" (coefficient of x on the right).
- right_constant: signed integer -10 to 10, NONZERO and DIFFERENT from left_constant. This is "e" (constant on the right).
- x_label (optional, default "x"): 1 to 6 chars; text inside x-tiles.
- unit_label (optional, default "1"): 1 to 6 chars; text inside unit-tiles.
- steps (optional): the ordered list of step images to render. Default renders all six: "step1_initial", "step2a_variable_zero_pair_add", "step2b_variable_after_cancel", "step2c_constant_zero_pair_add", "step2d_constant_after_cancel", "step3_equal_groups". Pass a subset to render only some steps.

RULES THE INPUT MUST SATISFY (validation will reject otherwise):
- a and d both nonzero (variable genuinely appears on both sides).
- a not equal to d (so there is a unique solution).
- b and e both nonzero (each side has a constant to work with).
- e not equal to b (so the solution is nonzero).

HOW IT SOLVES (so you can predict the output):
- Phase 1 cancels the variable on ONE side by adding x-tiles to both sides. The renderer AUTOMATICALLY chooses whichever side leaves a POSITIVE remaining x-coefficient; you do not control which side.
- Phase 2 ALWAYS removes the constant from the side that still holds the variable, leaving "K*x = M".
- "step3_equal_groups" is only produced when the solution is an INTEGER (i.e. (a - d) divides (e - b)). For fractional solutions that step is skipped automatically.

WHAT YOU GET BACK (output):
A MAP of step-label -> image (multiple images, one per requested/renderable step). Each image is the algebra-tile model at that point in the solution: step1 shows the original equation; the "zero_pair_add" steps show the added tiles clustered beneath each side; the "after_cancel" steps show the simplified equation; step3 shows K equal groups each reading x = q. Positive tiles are white, negative tiles are solid gray (labeled with a minus). There is NO key/legend (labels live inside each tile). A side's unit-tile grid keeps the same column arrangement across all steps.

EXAMPLE INPUT (solves -3x + 5 = -x + 1, giving x = 2):
{"type":"draw_equation_model_tiles_two_sided_we","left_coefficient":-3,"left_constant":5,"right_coefficient":-1,"right_constant":1}

Substandard Description: Use the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse to solve geometric problems.
Task: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find a missing leg and a missing hypotenuse in a composite right-triangle figure.
Stimulus Specification:
draw_pythagorean_theorem_figure

Emits ONE image used to test the Pythagorean theorem. A `figure_type`
discriminator selects one of TWO figures: a single right triangle, or a kite
whose two diagonals meet at a right angle. The image is NOT drawn to scale -
each figure type uses a small set of FIXED shapes; the measures only carry the
numbers and decide which fixed shape/orientation is drawn.

Pydantic schema (PythagoreanTheoremFigure):
- figure_type: Literal['right_triangle', 'kite']   (required; selects the figure)
- right_triangle fields (set ONLY when figure_type == 'right_triangle'):
  - leg_a, leg_b, hypotenuse: LabeledSegment   (the right angle is between leg_a and leg_b)
- kite fields (set ONLY when figure_type == 'kite'):
  - horizontal_half: LabeledSegment   (half of the horizontal diagonal - the SHARED leg; both halves equal this)
  - upper_vertical:  LabeledSegment   (upper half of the vertical diagonal, Top -> center O)
  - lower_vertical:  LabeledSegment   (lower half of the vertical diagonal, center O -> Bottom)
  - upper_side:      LabeledSegment   (upper kite side - hypotenuse of the upper triangles; both upper sides congruent)
  - lower_side:      LabeledSegment   (lower kite side - hypotenuse of the lower triangles; both lower sides congruent)

LabeledSegment (every side/segment):
- measure: float   (required, > 0; the TRUE length - used for geometry validation and to pick the fixed shape; ALWAYS provide it, even for an unknown)
- label:   str     (required; the text rendered on the figure)

Label rendering (LaTeX / matplotlib mathtext):
- Number + unit ("8 in.", "15 cm")     -> number in math font, unit upright ("$8\ \mathrm{in.}$")
- Fraction ("1/2", "1/2 in.")          -> "\frac{}{}" notation
- Variable ("v", "p", "x", "x_1")      -> italic math (subscripts supported)
- Already contains "$"                 -> passed through verbatim (arbitrary LaTeX: radicals, etc.)
- A segment is an "unknown" simply because its label is a variable; its measure is still required.

Validation (enforced by model validators):
- right_triangle: requires leg_a, leg_b, hypotenuse (and NO kite fields). Checks leg_a^2 + leg_b^2 == hypotenuse^2 (relative tolerance).
- kite: requires all five kite fields (and NO right_triangle fields). Checks BOTH triples:
    upper_vertical^2 + horizontal_half^2 == upper_side^2
    lower_vertical^2 + horizontal_half^2 == lower_side^2
  (i.e. two Pythagorean triples sharing the horizontal half-diagonal, so BOTH kite unknowns are genuine Pythagorean solves.)

Fixed visual style (NOT to scale, NOT configurable):
- right_triangle: right angle marked with a small square; all three sides labeled; no tick marks.
    Two fixed shapes, chosen by leg measures: "wide" (horizontal leg longer, leg_a >= leg_b) or "tall" (vertical leg longer).
- kite: both diagonals drawn; right-angle square at the intersection; congruence ticks on the 3 congruent pairs
    (upper sides = double tick, lower sides = triple tick, horizontal halves = single tick); the two vertical halves are unique (no ticks).
    Five segments labeled (upper_side, horizontal_half, upper_vertical, lower_vertical, lower_side); congruent partners stay unlabeled.
    Two fixed orientations, chosen by vertical measures: "normal" (upper triangle smaller, upper_vertical <= lower_vertical) or
    "inverted" (lower triangle smaller) - the vertical mirror.
- 600 DPI; white background; no title/legend; saved in the configured stimulus image format.

Content rules:
- right_triangle: use for plain a^2 + b^2 = c^2 items; put the unknown variable (e.g. "x") on the side being solved, numbers elsewhere.
- kite: build the figure from TWO Pythagorean triples that SHARE a leg (the horizontal half-diagonal), e.g. (6,8,10) on top and
  (8,15,17) on the bottom (shared half = 8). Put the two unknowns on any two labeled segments (commonly one vertical leg + one side).
- Always supply real measures so the geometry validates and the correct fixed shape is selected.
- Rhombus and square are intentionally NOT supported: a rhombus collapses to a single triple (only one Pythagorean unknown is
  possible), and a square is a 45-45-90 case with irrational side/diagonal ratios (no clean integer answers).

Related functions (do NOT confuse):
