- draw_similar_right_triangles (SimilarRightTriangles): similarity / geometric-mean items on a right triangle with the altitude to
  the hypotenuse - not a plain Pythagorean solve.
- draw_triangle_side_segments (TriangleSideSegments): a triangle with labeled side segments for other side-relationship items.
- draw_triangle_with_ray (RightTriangleWithRay): a right triangle with an extended ray, for angle items rather than side lengths.
Allowed Content Types: Inline Drag and Drop Question with Stimulus
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST describe a real-world object whose frame or figure is shown, then ask, in a single sentence, for the two missing lengths, naming each unknown with a variable (for example: "What are the measures of the missing vertical length, v, and the missing side length, p, in inches?").
- The stem MUST NOT tell students which method to use (do not mention the Pythagorean Theorem or instruct them to square or take a square root).
- The figure is the stimulus: all known side lengths and the two variable lengths MUST be labeled on the figure, with right-angle marks shown at the relevant vertices.
- Provide draggable numeric tiles as the choices, and include more tiles than blanks so that not all answers are used (state "Not all answers will be used.").
Example Question:
A decorative kite frame is shown. What are the measures of the missing side length, s, and the missing vertical length, v, in inches?

<<alt: A kite with a vertical line of symmetry. The horizontal diagonal meets the vertical diagonal at a right angle at the center. From the center to the right vertex is 12 in. The top section of the vertical diagonal, from the top vertex to the center, is 9 in. The top-right side is labeled s. The bottom-right side is 13 in. The bottom section of the vertical diagonal, from the center to the bottom vertex, is labeled v.>>

Drag a value into each box. Not all values will be used.
Tiles: 5, 7, 8, 11, 15, 18, 21, 25

The measure of the missing side length, s, is ___ inches, and the measure of the missing vertical length, v, is ___ inches.

Correct: s = 15, v = 5

Lesson Title: Lateral and Total Surface Area of Prisms
Substandard Description: Solve problems involving lateral and total surface area of rectangular and triangular prisms.
Task: Calculate the lateral or total surface area of a rectangular prism or triangular prism when a diagram is provided.
Stimulus Specification:
# draw_labeled_solid

Emits ONE textbook-style measurement diagram of a single labeled 3D solid: a rectangular prism, a triangular prism, or a cylinder. The shape is selected by the `shape` discriminator.

Pydantic schema (LabeledSolid — flat JSON keyed by `shape`):

Common to every shape:
- `shape`: Literal['rectangular_prism', 'triangular_prism', 'cylinder']  (required; selects the solid)
- `unit`: Literal['in.', 'ft', 'yd', 'cm', 'm']  (default 'cm'; used to build default labels)

When `shape == 'rectangular_prism'`:
- `length`: float            (front-bottom edge; gt 0, le 100)
- `width`: float             (right-bottom depth edge; gt 0, le 100)
- `height`: float            (vertical edge; gt 0, le 100)
- `label_length`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<length> <unit>")
- `label_width`: str | None  (optional override; defaults to "<width> <unit>")
- `label_height`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<height> <unit>")

When `shape == 'triangular_prism'`:
- `base`: float              (base of the triangular face; gt 0, le 100)
- `triangle_height`: float   (altitude of the triangular face; gt 0, le 100)
- `length`: float            (depth of the prism body; gt 0, le 100)
- `slant_height`: float | None  (slant side of the isosceles triangle; auto-calculated as sqrt((base/2)^2 + triangle_height^2) when omitted)
- `label_base`: str | None             (optional override; defaults to "<base> <unit>")
- `label_triangle_height`: str | None  (optional override; defaults to "<triangle_height> <unit>")
- `label_length`: str | None           (optional override; defaults to "<length> <unit>")
- `label_slant`: str | None            (optional override applied to BOTH slant sides; defaults to "<slant_height> <unit>")

When `shape == 'cylinder'`:
- `radius`: float            (gt 0, le 100; REQUIRED)
- `height`: float            (gt 0, le 100; REQUIRED)
- `label_radius`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<radius> <unit>")
- `label_height`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<height> <unit>")

Fixed visual style (NOT configurable):
- Solids are drawn with their VISIBLE edges only — no dashed hidden back edges.
- The ONLY dashed elements are:
  - the triangle altitude (dashed, with a right-angle marker at its foot), and
  - the cylinder radius segment (dashed, centre dot → edge of a circular face).
- Rectangular prism: 3D box; length, width, height labeled on their visible edges.
- Triangular prism: base, altitude, prism length (depth), and BOTH slant sides labeled.
- Cylinder: radius (dashed segment) and height (vertical dimension line) labeled. The diameter is NEVER shown.
- Black outlines on a white background; no title or legend.

Content rules:
- Every numeric dimension must be positive and at most 100.
- A cylinder MUST provide both `radius` and `height`; `diameter` is not a valid field and is rejected.
- A bare `in` inside any label is auto-corrected to `in.`.
- If no label override is given, the label is built as "<value> <unit>".

Usage examples:
```json
{ "shape": "rectangular_prism", "length": 12, "width": 5, "height": 8, "unit": "in." }
```
```json
{ "shape": "triangular_prism", "base": 8, "triangle_height": 6, "length": 12, "unit": "cm" }
```
```json
{ "shape": "cylinder", "radius": 3, "height": 8, "unit": "cm" }
```
Question Requirements:
- The question provides a labeled diagram of the solid; the stem MUST direct the
  student to use the dimensions shown in the diagram.
- The stem MUST ask for either the lateral surface area or the total surface area,
  in square units, of the solid (vary which is asked across questions).
- The answer MUST be a single numeric value, with the unit named in the stem so the
  student types only the number.
- Use different numbers and context from any released STAAR item (do not reuse the
  40-by-34-by-30 toy box or the answer 7,160).
- Do not state the surface area formula in the stem.
- for problems with cylinder, the stem must mention to use 3.14 for pi (use pi symbol here)
Example Question:
1. A diagram of a cereal box shaped like a rectangular prism is shown. Its
   measurements are given in inches. What is the total surface area of the cereal
   box in square inches?

   <<alt: A rectangular prism (cereal box) with a base measuring 30 inches by 18
   inches and a height of 24 inches. Dashed lines show the hidden edges.>>

   Answer: 3,384

Lesson Title: Lateral and Total Surface Area of Prisms
Substandard Description: Solve problems involving lateral and total surface area of rectangular and triangular prisms.
Task: Given a real-world description with no diagram, calculate the lateral or total surface area of a rectangular prism or triangular prism.
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST give all needed dimensions in a real-world description, with no diagram.
- The stem MUST ask for the lateral or total surface area, in square units, of a
  rectangular prism, triangular prism, or cylinder.
- For a cylinder, the stem MUST give the radius or diameter and the height, and MUST
  instruct the student to use 3.14 for pi.
- The answer MUST be a single numeric value, with the unit named in the stem so the
  student types only the number.
- Do not state the surface area formula in the stem.
Example Question:
1. A water tank is shaped like a cylinder with a radius of 5 feet and a height of
   10 feet. What is the lateral surface area of the tank in square feet? Use 3.14
   for pi.

   Answer: 314
Additional Worked Examples:
Additional Worked Example (cylinder, total surface area)

A water tank is shaped like a cylinder with a radius of 5 ft. and a height of 10 ft.
What is the total surface area of the tank in square feet? Use 3.14 for π.

**Step 1:** Determine which formula to use.

The problem asks for the total surface area, so use the total surface area formula.

S = Ph + 2B  
*where P is the perimeter of the base, h is the height of the cylinder, and B is the area of the base.*

**Step 2:** Find the perimeter of the base.

The base is a circle with radius 5 ft.  
  
P = 2πr  
*where r is the radius of the circle.*  
  
P = 2(3.14)(5)  
  
P = 31.4 ft.

**Step 3:** Find the area of the base.

B = πr²  
*where r is the radius of the circle.*  
  
B = 3.14(5²)  
  
B = 78.5 ft²

**Step 4:** Apply the total surface area formula.

S = Ph + 2B  
  
S = 31.4(10) + 2(78.5)  
  
S = 314 + 157  
  
S = 471  
  
The total surface area of the tank is **471 ft²**.

Lesson Title: Lateral and Total Surface Area of Prisms
Substandard Description: Solve problems involving lateral and total surface area of rectangular and triangular prisms.
Task: Given the lateral or total surface area and the other measurements (no diagram), calculate a missing dimension such as the height of a rectangular prism or triangular prism.
Question Requirements:
- The stem MUST give the lateral or total surface area and all linear measurements
  except one, then ask for the missing dimension (such as the height), in the stated
  unit.
- The solid MUST be a rectangular prism, triangular prism, or cylinder; for a
  cylinder, the stem MUST instruct the student to use 3.14 for pi.
- The missing value MUST be a single positive numeric value, with the unit named in
  the stem so the student types only the number.
- Use different numbers and context from any released STAAR item (do not reuse the
  252 square feet triangular prism with sides 6, 7, and 8, or the answer 12).
- Do not state the surface area formula in the stem.
Example Question:
1. The lateral surface area of a triangular prism is 220 square feet. The side
   lengths of the triangular base are 5 feet, 6 feet, and 9 feet. What is the height
   of the prism in feet?

   Answer: 11

Lesson Title: Lateral and Total Surface Area of Prisms
Substandard Description: Solve problems involving lateral and total surface area of rectangular and triangular prisms.
Task: Given a labeled diagram and the lateral or total surface area, calculate a missing dimension such as the height of a rectangular prism or triangular prism.
Stimulus Specification:
# draw_labeled_solid

Emits ONE textbook-style measurement diagram of a single labeled 3D solid: a rectangular prism, a triangular prism, or a cylinder. The shape is selected by the `shape` discriminator.

Pydantic schema (LabeledSolid — flat JSON keyed by `shape`):

Common to every shape:
- `shape`: Literal['rectangular_prism', 'triangular_prism', 'cylinder']  (required; selects the solid)
- `unit`: Literal['in.', 'ft', 'yd', 'cm', 'm']  (default 'cm'; used to build default labels)

When `shape == 'rectangular_prism'`:
- `length`: float            (front-bottom edge; gt 0, le 100)
- `width`: float             (right-bottom depth edge; gt 0, le 100)
- `height`: float            (vertical edge; gt 0, le 100)
- `label_length`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<length> <unit>")
- `label_width`: str | None  (optional override; defaults to "<width> <unit>")
- `label_height`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<height> <unit>")

When `shape == 'triangular_prism'`:
- `base`: float              (base of the triangular face; gt 0, le 100)
- `triangle_height`: float   (altitude of the triangular face; gt 0, le 100)
- `length`: float            (depth of the prism body; gt 0, le 100)
- `slant_height`: float | None  (slant side of the isosceles triangle; auto-calculated as sqrt((base/2)^2 + triangle_height^2) when omitted)
- `label_base`: str | None             (optional override; defaults to "<base> <unit>")
- `label_triangle_height`: str | None  (optional override; defaults to "<triangle_height> <unit>")
- `label_length`: str | None           (optional override; defaults to "<length> <unit>")
- `label_slant`: str | None            (optional override applied to BOTH slant sides; defaults to "<slant_height> <unit>")

When `shape == 'cylinder'`:
- `radius`: float            (gt 0, le 100; REQUIRED)
- `height`: float            (gt 0, le 100; REQUIRED)
- `label_radius`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<radius> <unit>")
- `label_height`: str | None (optional override; defaults to "<height> <unit>")

Fixed visual style (NOT configurable):
- Solids are drawn with their VISIBLE edges only — no dashed hidden back edges.
- The ONLY dashed elements are:
  - the triangle altitude (dashed, with a right-angle marker at its foot), and
  - the cylinder radius segment (dashed, centre dot → edge of a circular face).
- Rectangular prism: 3D box; length, width, height labeled on their visible edges.
- Triangular prism: base, altitude, prism length (depth), and BOTH slant sides labeled.
- Cylinder: radius (dashed segment) and height (vertical dimension line) labeled. The diameter is NEVER shown.
- Black outlines on a white background; no title or legend.

Content rules:
- Every numeric dimension must be positive and at most 100.
- A cylinder MUST provide both `radius` and `height`; `diameter` is not a valid field and is rejected.
- A bare `in` inside any label is auto-corrected to `in.`.
- If no label override is given, the label is built as "<value> <unit>".

Usage examples:
```json
{ "shape": "rectangular_prism", "length": 12, "width": 5, "height": 8, "unit": "in." }
```
```json
{ "shape": "triangular_prism", "base": 8, "triangle_height": 6, "length": 12, "unit": "cm" }
```
```json
{ "shape": "cylinder", "radius": 3, "height": 8, "unit": "cm" }
```
Question Requirements:
- The question provides a labeled diagram of the solid with one dimension left
  unknown; the stem MUST give the lateral or total surface area and ask for the
  missing dimension shown in the diagram.
- The missing value MUST be a single positive numeric value, with the unit named in
  the stem so the student types only the number.
- Do not state the surface area formula in the stem.
- for problems with cylinder, the stem must mention to use 3.14 for pi (use pi symbol here)
Example Question:
1. A diagram of a storage box shaped like a rectangular prism is shown. The total
   surface area of the box is 280 square inches. What is the height of the box in
   inches?

   <<alt: A rectangular prism (storage box) with a base measuring 10 inches by 6
   inches and an unknown height labeled h. Dashed lines show the hidden edges.>>

   Answer: 5
Additional Worked Examples:
Additional Worked Example (solve for height from total surface area)

A diagram of a rectangular prism is shown. The total surface area of the prism is
280 square inches. What is the height of the prism in inches?
<<Image: a rectangular prism with base 10 in. by 6 in. and an unknown height h.>>

**Step 1:** Determine which formula to use.

The problem gives the total surface area, so use the total surface area formula.

S = Ph + 2B  
*where P is the perimeter of the base, B is the area of the base, and h is the height of the prism.*

**Step 2:** Find the perimeter of the base.

The base is a rectangle measuring 10 in. by 6 in.  
  
P = 2ℓ + 2w  
*where ℓ is the length and w is the width of the rectangle.*  
  
P = 2(10) + 2(6)  
  
P = 32 in.

**Step 3:** Find the area of the base.

B = ℓw  
*where ℓ is the length and w is the width of the rectangle.*  
  
B = 10 × 6  
  
B = 60 sq in.

**Step 4:** Apply the total surface area formula and solve for h.

S = Ph + 2B  
  
280 = 32h + 2(60)  
  
280 = 32h + 120  
  
160 = 32h   Subtract 120 from both sides.  
  
h = 160/32   Divide both sides by 32.  
  
h = 5   
  
The height of the prism is **5 in.**

Lesson Title: Ratios of Corresponding Sides of Similar Shapes
Substandard Description: Generalize that the ratio of corresponding sides of similar shapes is proportional, including a shape and its dilation. 2018 Q36, 2017 Q17)
Task: Identify the proportion that correctly relates the corresponding sides of two similar figures when side lengths are given.
Common Misconceptions:
- Matches sides by screen position instead of corresponding vertices.
- Reverses one ratio by swapping its numerator and denominator.
- Writes the two ratios with the figures in opposite order.
- Checks correspondence for one ratio but not the other.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST end with exactly: "Which proportion must be true?"
- The stem MUST state that the two figures are similar and name them (e.g., "Triangle ABC and triangle DEF are similar." or "Figure I and Figure II are similar."); it MUST NOT restate the side lengths, which are read only from the stimulus
- Each answer option MUST be a single proportion written as two ratios joined by "=", using the side-length values and variable(s) shown in the stimulus
- The correct option MUST relate two pairs of corresponding sides with both ratios written in the same figure order
- The three distractors MUST each be a FALSE proportion (it must NOT be a true equality and must NOT simplify to the same value as the correct option) mirroring STAAR error patterns: (1) one with ONLY the FIRST ratio reversed (swap numerator and denominator of just that one ratio); (2) one with ONLY the SECOND ratio reversed; and (3) one pairing a NON-corresponding side. CRITICAL: NEVER reverse BOTH ratios together, because reversing both ratios produces an equivalent TRUE proportion (a/b = c/d is equivalent to b/a = d/c) and would create a second correct answer.
- No answer option may require computing the value of a variable
Example Question:
Quadrilateral ABCD and quadrilateral WXYZ are similar. Which proportion must be true?

<<alt: Two similar quadrilaterals shown in different orientations. Quadrilateral ABCD has sides AB labeled 12, BC labeled 6, CD labeled 10, and DA labeled 9. Quadrilateral WXYZ has sides WX labeled 18, XY labeled x, YZ labeled 15, and ZW labeled 13.5.>>

A. 10/15 = x/6
B. 15/10 = 6/x
C. 10/15 = 6/x
D. 12/15 = 6/x

Correct: C
Additional Worked Examples:
Triangle RST and triangle XYZ are similar. Which proportion must be true?
<<Two similar triangles. Triangle RST has side RS labeled 9 and side RT labeled 6. Triangle XYZ has side XY labeled 3 and side XZ labeled n.>>
A. $\dfrac{3}{9} = \dfrac{6}{n}$
B. $\dfrac{9}{3} = \dfrac{n}{6}$
C. $\dfrac{3}{9} = \dfrac{n}{6}$
D. $\dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{n}{9}$
Correct: C

Step 1: Match the vertices in order.
Since triangle RST is similar to triangle XYZ, the letters tell us which vertices match:
$R \leftrightarrow X$   $S \leftrightarrow Y$   $T \leftrightarrow Z$
So the corresponding sides are:
$RS \leftrightarrow XY$
$RT \leftrightarrow XZ$
The side RS matches XY, and the side RT matches XZ.

Step 2: Decide which triangle to compare first.
The answer choices compare sides from triangle XYZ to sides from triangle RST.
So each ratio should be written as:
$\dfrac{\text{side from triangle XYZ}}{\text{matching side from triangle RST}}$
This means we compare the smaller triangle to the larger triangle.

Step 3: Write a ratio using one pair of matching sides.
Side XY matches side RS.
From the diagram:
$XY = 3$
$RS = 9$
So the first ratio is:
$\dfrac{XY}{RS} = \dfrac{3}{9}$

Step 4: Write the second ratio in the same order.
Side XZ matches side RT.
From the diagram:
$XZ = n$
$RT = 6$
Because we already compared triangle XYZ to triangle RST, we must keep that same order:
$\dfrac{XZ}{RT} = \dfrac{n}{6}$

Step 5: Set the matching ratios equal.
In similar triangles, ratios of corresponding sides are proportional:
$\dfrac{XY}{RS} = \dfrac{XZ}{RT}$
Substitute the side lengths:
$\dfrac{3}{9} = \dfrac{n}{6}$
So the proportion that must be true is:
$\dfrac{3}{9} = \dfrac{n}{6}$
The correct answer is C.

Lesson Title: Ratios of Corresponding Sides of Similar Shapes
Substandard Description: Generalize that the ratio of corresponding sides of similar shapes is proportional, including a shape and its dilation. 2018 Q36, 2017 Q17)
Task: Identify the proportion that correctly relates the corresponding sides of two similar figures labeled by vertices, with no side lengths given.
Common Misconceptions:
- Matches sides by screen position instead of corresponding vertices.
- Reverses one ratio by swapping its numerator and denominator.
- Writes the two ratios with the figures in opposite order.
- Checks correspondence for one ratio but not the other.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST end with exactly: "Which proportion must be true?"
- The stem MUST state that the two figures are similar and either give a similarity statement naming corresponding vertices in order (e.g., "Figure ABCDE is similar to figure VWXYZ.") or label them "Figure I and Figure II"; no side lengths are given
- Each answer option MUST be a single proportion of the form (two-letter side)/(two-letter side) = (two-letter side)/(two-letter side), where each ratio compares a side of one figure to a side of the other figure
- Side names MUST use two-letter segment notation matching the figure's vertex labels
- The correct option MUST pair corresponding sides (matched by the similarity-statement order or by matching angle marks) with both ratios written in the same figure order
- The three distractors MUST mirror STAAR error patterns: one with a single ratio reversed, one pairing non-corresponding sides, and one that pairs a corresponding side incorrectly when the figures are rotated or reflected
Example Question:
Figure JKLMN is similar to figure PQRST. Which proportion must be true?

<<alt: Two similar pentagons shown in different orientations with vertices labeled and no side lengths. Figure JKLMN has vertices J, K, L, M, N. Figure PQRST has vertices P, Q, R, S, T.>>

A. JK/PQ = RS/LM
B. KL/PQ = LM/RS
C. JK/PQ = LM/RS
D. JK/QR = LM/RS

Correct: C
Additional Worked Examples:
Figure ABCDE is similar to figure FGHJK. Which proportion must be true?
<<Two similar pentagons shown in different orientations with vertices labeled and no side lengths. Figure ABCDE has vertices A, B, C, D, E. Figure FGHJK has vertices F, G, H, J, K.>>
A. $\dfrac{AB}{FG} = \dfrac{HJ}{CD}$
B. $\dfrac{AB}{FG} = \dfrac{CD}{HJ}$
C. $\dfrac{AB}{GH} = \dfrac{CD}{HJ}$
D. $\dfrac{BC}{FG} = \dfrac{CD}{HJ}$
Correct: B

Step 1: Use the similarity statement to match the vertices.
The problem says figure ABCDE is similar to figure FGHJK.
That means the letters are written in corresponding order:
A matches F
B matches G
C matches H
D matches J
E matches K
So the corresponding sides are:
AB matches FG
BC matches GH
CD matches HJ
DE matches JK
EA matches KF

Step 2: Start with a ratio from the answer choices.
One ratio shown in the answer choices is $\dfrac{AB}{FG}$.
This is a valid ratio because AB and FG are corresponding sides.
It compares a side from figure ABCDE to its matching side from figure FGHJK.

Step 3: Keep the same comparison order.
Since the first ratio compares a side from figure ABCDE to a matching side from figure FGHJK, the second ratio must use the same order.
So the second ratio should also be:
$\dfrac{\text{side from ABCDE}}{\text{matching side from FGHJK}}$
Side CD corresponds to side HJ, so the matching ratio is $\dfrac{CD}{HJ}$.
Do not switch the order in the second ratio. Both ratios must compare the first figure to the second figure.

Step 4: Write the proportion.
Corresponding sides of similar figures have proportional side lengths.
So the correct proportion is:
$\dfrac{AB}{FG} = \dfrac{CD}{HJ}$
The correct answer is B.

Lesson Title: Effect of Scale Factor on Coordinates
Substandard Description: Use an algebraic representation to explain the effect of a given positive rational scale factor on two-dimensional figures,.
Task: Determine the algebraic rule of a dilation from a figure and its image graphed on a coordinate plane with the origin as the center of dilation.
Allowed Content Types: Inline Drag and Drop Question with Stimulus
Common Misconceptions:
- Confuses dilation with translation, adding the scale factor instead of multiplying.
- Multiplies coordinates by a doubled or otherwise incorrect scale factor.
- Misreads corresponding vertices and computes the wrong scale factor.
Question Requirements:
- The interactive content MUST be wrapped in <interaction> ... </interaction>.
- Stem order (STAAR-aligned): (1) "Triangle [FIG] is dilated with the origin as the center of dilation to create triangle [FIG′]. Complete the rule to represent the dilation from triangle [FIG] to triangle [FIG′]." (2) the instruction line "Move the correct answer to each box. Not all answers will be used." (3) the rule line. Do NOT place the instruction line first.
- The figure and its image are shown ONLY in the stimulus image; do NOT restate their coordinates in the stem.
- Rule line MUST read "$(x, y) \rightarrow$ (<target-1>, <target-2>)" — wrap (x, y) and the arrow in LaTeX so the variables render italic. <target-1> and <target-2> are inline drop-target placeholders. Each placeholder MUST also appear in targets[] as { id, label, target: { placeholder: "<target-N>" } }.
- choices[] MUST be single coefficient expressions of the form [k]x and [k]y, each as { id, text, matchMax: 1 } (for example 2x, 3x, 4x, 2y, 3y, 4y); include extra unused choices.
- correctMapping[] MUST map the [k]x choice to <target-1> and the [k]y choice to <target-2>, where k is the scale factor of the dilation.
- Distractor choices MUST include a doubled coefficient (for example 4x/4y when k = 2) AND a near-miss factor (for example 3x/3y when k = 2).
Example Question:
<interaction>Triangle JKL is dilated with the origin as the center of dilation to create triangle J′K′L′. Complete the rule to represent the dilation from triangle JKL to triangle J′K′L′.

Move the correct answer to each box. Not all answers will be used.

$(x, y) \rightarrow$ (<target-1>, <target-2>)</interaction>

<<alt: Triangle JKL with vertices J(1, 3), K(3, 2), L(1, 2) and its image J′K′L′ with J′(2, 6), K′(6, 4), L′(2, 4) on a coordinate grid; the origin is the center of dilation.>>

choices:

- c2: 3x
- c3: 4x
- c4: 2y
- c5: 3y
- c6: 4y
targets:
- t1: placeholder <target-1> (x-coefficient)
- t2: placeholder <target-2> (y-coefficient)
correctMapping:
