Lesson Title: Represent Real-World Linear Proportional Situations as Graphs
Substandard Description: Represent real world linear proportional situations with graphs.
Task: Select the graph that represents a real-world linear proportional situation (y = kx) when given a verbal description of the rate.
Stimulus Specification:
Stimulus: draw_proportional_relationship_option — Each answer option is a separate first-quadrant line graph. Used for "which graph represents the real-world proportional relationship" MCQs.

Output (per answer option): a single JSON object named "stimulus_description" with these fields ONLY:
- type: "graph"  (always the literal string "graph")
- graph_title: short context title (<= 40 chars), e.g. "Paint Mix"
- graph_x_title: x-axis label, e.g. "Cups of paint"
- graph_y_title: y-axis label, e.g. "Cups of water"
- graph_points: array of EXACTLY 2 points [{"x": <number>, "y": <number>}, {"x": <number>, "y": <number>}] defining the line for this option
- graph_x_max: number > 0 (x-axis maximum)
- graph_y_max: number > 0 (y-axis maximum)
- graph_x_interval: number > 0 (x-axis tick spacing)
- graph_y_interval: number > 0 (y-axis tick spacing)

Do NOT use the fields "line", "x_axis", "y_axis", "x_min", "y_min", "x_max", "y_max", "title", "point1", "point2", or "gridlines". Use only the fields listed above.

Rules:
1. CORRECT option: a straight line through the origin — include {"x":0,"y":0} as the first point — using the unit rate k from the problem. The second point must be a lattice point on the gridlines, e.g. {"x":0,"y":0} and {"x":5,"y":2} for k = 2/5.
2. Distractors (each still a 2-point line, plausible misconceptions):
   - inverted unit rate (swap the x and y of the correct second point);
   - a non-proportional line that does NOT pass through the origin (first point has y > 0);
   - a line through the origin with a different (wrong) slope.
3. All coordinates are non-negative and within the axis maxima.
4. Choose graph_x_max / graph_y_max so both points are clearly visible with headroom; pick whole-number intervals giving ~5-8 gridlines per axis.
5. Keep graph_x_title / graph_y_title identical across all four options (same real-world context).
Common Misconceptions:
- Inverts the unit rate, swapping the x- and y-coordinates when plotting.
- Uses the given quantity as a y-intercept, so the line misses the origin.
- Uses a non-unit value directly as the unit rate without reducing.
- Uses the given quantity as an x-intercept on the horizontal axis.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST follow this format: one or two sentences describing a real-world proportional situation that states a rate or ratio, then the question "Which graph [best] shows the relationship between [first variable], [its meaning], and [second variable], [its meaning]?" Order the two variables to match the graphs' axis labels, and use "best" when the rate must be reduced to a unit rate.
- The stem MUST NOT contain a graph; all four answer options ARE graphs (answer stimulus), each a straight line in the first quadrant with identical axis labels, title, gridlines, and scale.
- The correct answer graph MUST show a line through the origin (0, 0) whose steepness equals the unit rate k.
- Distractor graphs MUST mirror these distractor errors (choose three): (a) inverted unit rate — a line through the origin with the x- and y-values swapped; (b) the given quantity used as a y-intercept — a line that crosses the y-axis at a non-zero value (not through the origin); (c) the given quantity used as an x-intercept — a line that crosses the x-axis at a non-zero value (not through the origin); (d) a non-reduced given value used directly as the unit rate — a line through the origin with the wrong steepness.
- Because every option is a graph of the same style, the options MUST differ only in the line so the answer cannot be eliminated by surface cues.
Example Question:
A survey at a bookstore revealed that 4 out of 5 customers preferred the store's new bookmark design. Which graph shows the relationship between y, the number of customers who preferred the new design, and x, the total number of customers?

<<alt: Four coordinate graphs labeled A–D titled "New Bookmark Design"; x-axis "Total Number of Customers", y-axis "Customers Who Preferred New Design". The four graphs share the same axes, gridlines, and scale; each shows one straight line as described in the options.>>

A. A line through (0, 0) and (5, 4).
B. A line through (0, 0) and (4, 5).
C. A line through (0, 4) and (5, 8).
D. A line through (4, 0) and (8, 5).

Correct: A
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Lesson Title: Represent Real-World Linear Proportional Situations as Tables
Substandard Description: Represent real world linear proportional situations with tables.
Task: Select the table that represents a real-world linear proportional situation (y = kx) when given a verbal description from which the constant rate must be found.
Common Misconceptions:
- Inverts the unit rate, dividing x by y instead of y by x.
- Adds a constant difference to x instead of multiplying by the rate.
- Applies the correct rate to only the first row of the table.
Worked Examples Stimulus Function: draw_vertical_data_table (existing) renders the correct y = kx table for the worked example — one table with the x-values and the matching k-multiples. The four-table A–D alt-text refers to the question's answer-option stimuli (see stimuli_categorization).
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST describe a real-world proportional situation in which a constant rate must be derived from two corresponding values (e.g., "After 5 seconds the height of the balloon is 20 feet"), then end with "Which table shows the relationship between [x-quantity], x, and [y-quantity], y?". Order the two variables to match the table column order.
- The stem MUST NOT contain a table; all four answer options ARE tables (answer stimulus), each rendered by draw_vertical_data_table.
- Each option's stimulus_description MUST use representation_type "vertical_data_table" with: table_title (a short context title, identical across all four options), col1_header (x-quantity with units, e.g. "Time, x (sec)"), col2_header (y-quantity with units, e.g. "Height, y (ft)"), and rows (4 or 5 two-element lists). All cell values MUST be JSON strings.
- All four option tables MUST share the same positive x-values and the same headers/title; only the y-values differ, and no two options may share the same set of y-values.
- The correct option's table MUST satisfy y = kx for every row (each y equals k times its x), representing a line through the origin.
- The three distractor tables MUST mirror these STAAR distractor errors: (a) inverted unit rate — each y equals x divided by k; (b) additive non-proportional — each y is a constant amount more than x (the difference of the two given values), not a multiple; (c) correct-rate-first-row-only — the first ordered pair satisfies y = kx but later rows use a different rate.
- x-values MUST be positive and evenly spaced; do not include x = 0.
Example Question:
A drone is released at ground level and rises at a constant rate. After 4 seconds the drone is 12 meters high. The drone continues to rise at the same rate. Which table shows the relationship between the time in seconds, x, and the height of the drone in meters, y?

<<alt: Four tables labeled A–D titled "Drone"; columns "Time, x (sec)" and "Height, y (m)". All four share x-values 3, 6, 9, 12, 15; each shows different y-values.>>

A. y-values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (each y = x ÷ 3 — inverted rate)
B. y-values 11, 14, 17, 20, 23 (each y = x + 8 — additive difference)
C. y-values 9, 15, 21, 27, 33 (first pair correct, then the rate changes)
D. y-values 9, 18, 27, 36, 45 (each y = 3x)

Correct: D
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Lesson Title: Represent Real-World Linear Proportional Situations as Tables
Substandard Description: Represent real world linear proportional situations with tables.
Task: Select the table that represents a real-world linear proportional situation (y = kx) when the relationship is stated directly as a multiple of x.
Common Misconceptions:
- Swaps the independent and dependent variable columns.
- Treats a given value as a starting amount, so the table misses the origin.
Worked Examples Stimulus Function: draw_vertical_data_table (existing) renders the correct y = kx table for the worked example — one table with the x-values and the matching k-multiples. The four-table A–D alt-text refers to the question's answer-option stimuli (see stimuli_categorization).
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST state the proportional relationship directly, e.g. "The [y-quantity], y, for [x-quantity] x is equal to [k] times the value of x.", then end with "Which table represents this relationship?". k is a positive whole number.
- The stem MUST NOT contain a table; all four answer options ARE tables (answer stimulus), each rendered by draw_vertical_data_table.
- Each option's stimulus_description MUST use representation_type "vertical_data_table" with: table_title (a short context title, identical across all four options), col1_header (x-quantity with units), col2_header (y-quantity with units), and rows (four two-element lists). All cell values MUST be JSON strings.
- The correct option's table MUST satisfy y = kx for every row, with x-values 1, 2, 3, 4 in the x-column and the matching k-multiples in the y-column.
- The three distractor tables MUST mirror these STAAR distractor errors: (a) columns swapped — the k-multiples placed in the x-column and the counts 1, 2, 3, 4 in the y-column; (b) columns swapped and shifted — k-multiples in the x-column and 0, 1, 2, 3 in the y-column; (c) non-proportional — x-values 0, 1, 2, 3 paired with the k-multiples, so y equals k when x = 0 (the relationship does not pass through the origin).
- No two option tables may share the same set of ordered pairs.
Example Question:
The number of eggs, y, in x dozens is equal to 12 times the value of x. Which table represents this relationship?

<<alt: Four tables labeled A–D titled "Eggs"; columns "Dozens, x" and "Eggs, y". Each shows four rows.>>

A. x: 12, 24, 36, 48 → y: 1, 2, 3, 4 (columns swapped)
B. x: 12, 24, 36, 48 → y: 0, 1, 2, 3 (columns swapped and shifted)
C. x: 0, 1, 2, 3 → y: 12, 24, 36, 48 (non-proportional; y = 12 when x = 0)
D. x: 1, 2, 3, 4 → y: 12, 24, 36, 48 (each y = 12x)

Correct: D
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Lesson Title: Represent Linear Non-Proportional Situations as Equations
Substandard Description: Represent linear non-proportional situations with tables, graphs, and equations in the form of y = mx + b, where b ≠ 0.
Task: Given a verbal description of a real-world linear situation with a constant rate and a one-time fee or discount, identify the table that represents the relationship y = mx + b.
Common Misconceptions:
- Reverses the rate and the y-intercept when writing y = mx + b.
- Adds the value of a discount instead of subtracting it.
- Verifies the rate of change but ignores the initial value.
- Makes a sign error when reading the rate from a graph or table.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST describe a real-world linear situation with a cost per item (the rate) and a one-time amount: either an added fee (e.g., "also paid a one-time shipping fee of $22") or a discount taken off the total (e.g., "had a $2-off coupon to use toward the total cost"), then end with "Which table shows the relationship between x, the number of [items] bought, and y, the total cost of the [items]?" (variable-letter form, mirroring STAAR 2021 Q13) or "Which table shows the relationship between the total cost of the [items] and the number of [items] bought?" (no-variable form, mirroring STAAR 2022 Q22).
- The stem MUST NOT contain a table; all four answer options ARE tables (answer stimulus), each rendered by draw_vertical_data_table.
- Each option's stimulus_description MUST use representation_type "vertical_data_table" with: table_title (a short context title, identical across all four options), col1_header (x-quantity, e.g. "Sets of Books"), col2_header (y-quantity with units, e.g. "Total Cost (dollars)"), and rows (4 two-element lists). All cell values MUST be JSON strings.
- The correct option's table MUST satisfy y = mx + b for every row, where m is the rate and b is the one-time fee (positive) or discount (negative).
- For added-fee contexts (b > 0), all four tables MUST share the same x-values, and the three distractor tables SHOULD mirror these STAAR (preferred) 2022 Q22 errors: (a) rate and fee reversed, y = bx + m; (b) fee times the sum, y = b(x + m); (c) rate times the sum, y = m(x + b).
- For discount contexts (b < 0), the three distractor tables SHOULD mirror these STAAR (preferred) 2021 Q13 errors: (a) inverted rate, y = x/m + b; (b) discount added instead of subtracted, y = mx - b; (c) inverted rate with discount added, y = x/m - b. Tables for inverted-rate distractors use x-values divisible by m and large enough that every y-value is positive.
- No two option tables may share the same set of y-values. If a specific distractor pattern cannot be produced cleanly within the Assessment Boundary, substitute another plausible misconception-based distractor (e.g., sign error, swapped rate/initial value, inverted rate, missing initial value) instead.
- ASSESSMENT BOUNDARY (hard limit): the rate of change m MUST be a nonzero value with magnitude at most 50 — a whole-number count, a money rate in dollars and cents, or another constant rate; the initial value b MUST be a nonzero value with |b| <= 45. NEVER generate a rate or initial value outside these ranges.
- Every option's table/graph MUST be internally consistent (its own values must fit the equation it is meant to represent), and any per-option explanation MUST match that option's own values.
Example Question:
A student bought packs of trading cards at a cost of $4 per pack. The student had a $3-off coupon to use toward the total cost of the cards bought. Which table shows the relationship between x, the number of packs bought by the student, and y, the total cost of the cards?

<<alt: Four tables labeled A-D titled "Trading Cards"; columns "Packs Bought, x" and "Total Cost, y". Each shows four rows.>>

A. x: 5, 10, 15, 25 -> y: 17, 37, 57, 97 (each y = 4x - 3)
B. x: 16, 24, 32, 40 -> y: 1, 3, 5, 7 (each y = x/4 - 3)
C. x: 5, 10, 15, 25 -> y: 23, 43, 63, 103 (each y = 4x + 3)
D. x: 16, 24, 32, 40 -> y: 7, 9, 11, 13 (each y = x/4 + 3)

Correct: A
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Lesson Title: Represent Linear Non-Proportional Situations as Equations
Substandard Description: Represent linear non-proportional situations with tables, graphs, and equations in the form of y = mx + b, where b ≠ 0.
Task: Given a verbal description of a real-world linear situation with an initial amount and a constant rate, identify the representation (table or graph) that best shows the relationship.
Common Misconceptions:
- Reverses the rate and the y-intercept when writing y = mx + b.
- Adds the value of a discount instead of subtracting it.
- Verifies the rate of change but ignores the initial value.
- Makes a sign error when reading the rate from a graph or table.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST state an initial amount and a constant rate for a real-world quantity (e.g., "has 15 toys in his toy box, and he adds 2 new toys every month"), then end with: "Based on this information, which representation best shows this relationship between [y-quantity], y, and [x-quantity], x?" (mirroring STAAR 2018 Q16).
- All four answer options ARE images (answer stimulus): exactly 2 tables and 2 graphs, each rendered by draw_linear_relationship_representation. All options share the same title (the real-world context, e.g. "Rudolfo's Toys").
- The correct option MUST be the graph whose y-intercept equals the initial value b and which rises by the rate m for each increase of 1 on the x-axis.
- The three distractors SHOULD mirror these STAAR (preferred) 2018 Q16 errors: (a) a table with the correct rate but a missing initial value (every row satisfies y = mx); (b) a table whose rate equals m + b (every row satisfies y = (m + b)x); (c) a graph with the rate and initial value reversed (y-intercept m, rate b).
- Graphs MUST be first-quadrant graphs with axis labels naming the real-world quantities and a y-axis scale that makes the y-intercept readable.
- Table x-values MUST NOT include 0 (otherwise the missing-initial-value distractor becomes obvious). If a specific distractor pattern cannot be produced cleanly within the Assessment Boundary, substitute another plausible misconception-based distractor (e.g., sign error, swapped rate/initial value, inverted rate, missing initial value) instead.
- ASSESSMENT BOUNDARY (hard limit): the rate of change m MUST be a nonzero value with magnitude at most 50 — a whole-number count, a money rate in dollars and cents, or another constant rate; the initial value b MUST be a nonzero value with |b| <= 45. NEVER generate a rate or initial value outside these ranges.
- Every option's table/graph MUST be internally consistent (its own values must fit the equation it is meant to represent), and any per-option explanation MUST match that option's own values.
Example Question:
Nadia has 9 books on her shelf, and she buys 4 new books every month. Based on this information, which representation best shows this relationship between the number of books Nadia has on her shelf, y, and the number of months that have passed, x?

<<alt: Four options labeled A-D, each titled "Nadia's Books": two tables with columns x and y, and two first-quadrant line graphs with x-axis "Number of Months" and y-axis "Number of Books".>>

A. Table - x: 3, 5, 8, 11 -> y: 12, 20, 32, 44 (each y = 4x; correct rate, missing initial value)
B. Graph - line crosses the y-axis at 9 and rises 4 for each month (y = 4x + 9)
C. Table - x: 1, 4, 7, 10 -> y: 13, 52, 91, 130 (each y = 13x; rate plus initial value combined)
D. Graph - line crosses the y-axis at 4 and rises 9 for each month (rate and initial value reversed)

Correct: B
Additional Worked Examples:
Additional Worked Example
Maria has 12 books on her shelf, and she buys 3 new books every month. Based on this information, which representation best shows this relationship between the number of books Maria has on her shelf, y, and the number of months that have passed, x?
A. <<alt: A table with columns x and y showing rows (2, 6), (5, 15), (8, 24), (10, 30).>>
B. <<alt: A coordinate plane showing a line that crosses the y-axis at 12 and rises 3 for each month.>>
C. <<alt: A table with columns x and y showing rows (1, 15), (3, 45), (6, 90), (9, 135).>>
D. <<alt: A coordinate plane showing a line that crosses the y-axis at 3 and rises 12 for each month.>>

Step 1: Write the equation from the description.
Maria starts with 12 books, so the initial value is b = 12. She buys 3 new books every month, so the rate of change is m = 3. Substitute m = 3 and b = 12 into y = mx + b to get y = 3x + 12.

Step 2: Check the tables.
For each table, check whether every row satisfies y = 3x + 12.
Option A: for x = 2, 3(2) + 12 = 18, but the table shows y = 6, so A does not match.
Option C: for x = 3, 3(3) + 12 = 21, but the table shows y = 45, so C does not match.

Step 3: Check the graphs.
For each graph, check the y-intercept (initial value) and the change for each step of 1 on the x-axis (rate of change).
Option B crosses the y-axis at 12 and rises by 3 for each month, so it matches y = 3x + 12.
Option D crosses the y-axis at 3 and rises by 12 for each month, so it does not match.

Step 4: Choose the correct answer.
The graph that crosses the y-axis at 12 and rises by 3 each month shows the relationship. The answer is B.

Lesson Title: Represent Linear Non-Proportional Situations as Equations
Substandard Description: Represent linear non-proportional situations with tables, graphs, and equations in the form of y = mx + b, where b ≠ 0.
Task: Given an abstract verbal description of a linear relationship with a negative rate, select the two representations (equation, graph, or table) that show the relationship.
Common Misconceptions:
- Reverses the rate and the y-intercept when writing y = mx + b.
- Adds the value of a discount instead of subtracting it.
- Verifies the rate of change but ignores the initial value.
- Makes a sign error when reading the rate from a graph or table.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST be exactly: "The value of y is [b] more than [multiplier word] times the opposite of x. Which of these represent this relationship?" followed by "Select TWO correct answers." (mirroring STAAR 2023 Q22). [b] is a positive integer and [multiplier word] is a number word such as "twice", "three", "four", or "five" (use "twice" alone, otherwise "[word] times").
- The intended relationship is y = -kx + b, where k is the multiplier and b is the initial value.
- There MUST be exactly 5 answer options in this order of types: 1 equation (plain text), 2 graphs (answer stimulus), 2 tables (answer stimulus). Exactly TWO options are correct: the equation y = -kx + b and the falling graph with y-intercept b and rate -k.
- Graphs MUST be 4-quadrant abstract coordinate planes with no real-world labels. The distractor graph MUST mirror the STAAR 2023 Q22 sign error: it rises with rate +k and has the same y-intercept b.
- Tables MUST have exactly 3 rows and MUST both be incorrect; they SHOULD mirror the STAAR 2023 Q22 table errors (preferred): (a) x- and y-values reversed (every row satisfies x = -ky + b); (b) sign error (every row satisfies y = kx + b). Each table MUST include x-values on both sides of zero, and the sign-error table may include the shared point (0, b).
- The correct count is always exactly 2; the stem MUST say "Select TWO correct answers." If a specific distractor pattern cannot be produced cleanly within the Assessment Boundary, substitute another plausible misconception-based distractor (e.g., sign error, swapped rate/initial value, inverted rate, missing initial value) instead.
- ASSESSMENT BOUNDARY (hard limit): the rate of change m MUST be a nonzero value with magnitude at most 50 — a whole-number count, a money rate in dollars and cents, or another constant rate; the initial value b MUST be a nonzero value with |b| <= 45. NEVER generate a rate or initial value outside these ranges.
- Every option's table/graph MUST be internally consistent (its own values must fit the equation it is meant to represent), and any per-option explanation MUST match that option's own values.
Example Question:
The value of y is 4 more than five times the opposite of x. Which of these represent this relationship?

Select TWO correct answers.

<<alt: Five options: an equation, two 4-quadrant line graphs, and two 3-row tables with columns x and y.>>

A. y = -5x + 4
B. Graph - line rises from left to right, passing through (0, 4) and (1, 9) (sign error: rate +5)
C. Graph - line falls from left to right, passing through (0, 4) and (1, -1) (rate -5, y-intercept 4)
D. Table - x: 9, 4, -6 -> y: -1, 0, 2 (x- and y-values reversed: each row satisfies x = -5y + 4)
E. Table - x: -1, 0, 2 -> y: -1, 4, 14 (sign error: each row satisfies y = 5x + 4)

Correct: A, C
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Lesson Title: Represent Linear Proportional Situations as Graphs or Tables
Substandard Description: Represent linear proportional situations with graphs or tables.
Task: Given an equation in the form y = kx, identify the graph or table that represents the proportional relationship.
Common Misconceptions:
- Chooses a graph with a positive slope when k is negative.
- Matches a proportional table or graph whose rate differs from k.
- Fails to recognize that a proportional graph passes through the origin.
Worked Examples Stimulus Function: draw_linear_relationship_representation (LinearEquationGraph with y_intercept = 0 for the y = kx line; VerticalDataTable for option tables)
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST be exactly: "Which [representations] shows the proportional relationship described by the function y = [k]x?" — where [representations] is "graph" when all options are graphs, "table" when all options are tables, and "graph or table" when options mix both (Medium); only the value of k varies otherwise.
- The stem MUST NOT contain a stimulus; all four answer options ARE images (answer stimulus), each showing either a coordinate graph or a two-column x/y table.
- Graph options MUST be straight lines through identical four-quadrant grids (same axes, scale, gridlines, and styling); table options MUST share identical column headers (x, y) with three or more rows of integer values.
- EVERY option — correct and distractors — MUST itself be proportional (a line through the origin, or a table whose y-values are a constant multiple of its x-values), mirroring STAAR 2024 Q34 where no option can be eliminated by an origin check.
- Exactly one option MUST show y = kx — a line through (0, 0) and (1, k), or a table whose every y-value equals k times its x-value.
- At Medium, the three distractor roles MUST mirror STAAR 2024 Q34: (b) one graph through the origin whose slope has the opposite sign of k; (c) one proportional table whose rate is the sign of k times 1 (the sign pattern applied without the magnitude, y = ±x); (d) one proportional table whose rate applies k in the wrong direction (each y equals x divided by k).
- At Easy, distractors use the same three error patterns adapted to the single representation in use (opposite-sign rate, rate ±1, inverted rate).
- The correct answer MAY be the graph or a table; vary which role is correct across the bank so representation type does not predict the answer.
- No two options may represent the same relationship.
Example Question:
Which graph or table shows the proportional relationship described by the function y = −4x?

<<alt: Options A and B are coordinate graphs with x- and y-axes from −5 to 5; each shows one straight line through the origin. Options C and D are two-column x/y tables with three rows each.>>

A. A line through (0, 0) falling from left to right through (1, −4)
B. A line through (0, 0) rising from left to right through (1, 4)
C. Table — x: 2, 4, 6 and y: −2, −4, −6
D. Table — x: 4, 8, 12 and y: −1, −2, −3

Correct: A
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Lesson Title: Represent Linear Proportional Situations as Graphs or Tables
Substandard Description: Represent linear proportional situations with graphs or tables.
Task: Identify the graph that represents a proportional relationship given as a single value pair.
Stimuli Categorization: Graph answer options: each of the four answer options is a four-quadrant coordinate graph (−7 to 7) showing one straight line through the origin. The correct option is the line through the origin and the given value pair; distractors are proportional lines with the wrong rate/sign (draw_linear_equation_graph). NOTE: built as graph-selection MCQ because the platform has no native point-plotting interaction; the assessed skill is identify/select, not plot/construct.
Common Misconceptions:
- Chooses a graph with a positive slope when k is negative.
- Matches a proportional table or graph whose rate differs from k.
- Fails to recognize that a proportional graph passes through the origin.
Worked Examples Stimulus Function: draw_linear_equation_graph (four-quadrant coordinate grid, −7 to 7, showing the line through the origin and the given value pair) for answer-option and worked-example images.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST be exactly: "Which graph shows the proportional relationship in which y = [y1] when x = [x1]?" — value-pair framing adapted from STAAR 2025 Q7; only the numbers vary.
- Each of the four answer options is a coordinate graph (−7 to 7 on both axes, gridlines at every integer) showing one straight line through the origin.
- The correct option is the line through the origin (0, 0) and the given pair ([x1], [y1]).
- Distractors MUST each be proportional (through the origin) but with the wrong rate or sign, so no option can be eliminated without checking the rate (matches STAAR distractor design).
- The stem MUST NOT name the origin or tell the student the line passes through (0, 0); recalling the origin is part of the assessed skill.
- Single-select; exactly one correct option.
Example Question:
Which graph shows the proportional relationship in which y = 4 when x = 3?

Answer: the graph of the line through (0, 0) and (3, 4)
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Substandard Description: Solve problems involving parallel lines cut by a transversal.
Task: Determine the value of an unknown angle formed when two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, using the angle relationship shown in a diagram.
Stimulus Specification:
Stimulus Function: draw_four_transversal_grid

Emits ONE image: a 2x2 grid of four "two parallel lines cut by a transversal"
diagrams, used for teaching angle-pair relationships (corresponding, alternate
interior, alternate exterior). All four panels teach the SAME
angle-pair type; only the angle values (and randomized variant placement) differ.
Panels are rendered in order: top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right.

Pydantic schema (FourTransversalGrid):
- angle_pair_type: Literal['corresponding','alternate_exterior','alternate_interior']
                   (required; the relationship taught by all four panels)
- variant_layout:  Literal['uniform','by_column']  (required)
                   - uniform   = all four panels use one randomly chosen variant
                   - by_column = left column uses one variant, right column a second distinct variant
- seed:            int  (required; seeds the variant randomization so output is deterministic)
- panels:          list[TransversalPanel]  (EXACTLY 4)
- top_line_label:    str  (default 'g'; single char)
- bottom_line_label: str  (default 'k'; single char)
- transversal_label: str  (default 't'; single char; drawn at the lower end of the transversal)

Per-panel schema (TransversalPanel):
- angle:       float  (required; 25-155 inclusive. Exactly 90 renders a perpendicular
                       transversal with right-angle squares and NO degree labels.)
- arc1_label:  str | None  (optional; first angle label. If None, auto = int(angle).)
- arc2_label:  str | None  (optional; second angle label. If None, auto = angle for
                            equal pair types.)

Label coercion (arc1_label / arc2_label):
- Bare text only - do NOT include a degree symbol; the function appends it.
- e.g. send "x" or "61", not "61°".

Fixed visual style (NOT configurable):
- Two horizontal parallel lines (filled arrowheads both ends) cut by one slanted transversal.
- Fixed tilt - acute uses one tilt, obtuse another; NOT drawn to scale.
- Degree labels placed at the corners that match angle_pair_type/variant, kept clear of the lines.
- Line labels g/k/t italic; transversal label parked at its lower end.

Content rules:
- Keep all four panels' values consistent with the chosen relationship so the taught
  pattern is obvious across the grid.
- Use bare labels (no degree symbol); send variables like "x" to leave an unknown.
- Never use same_side_interior
Question Requirements:
ANSWER OPTIONS — each option: "The value of x is N, because <justification>."
Never bare numeric. Exactly one correct.

Subject phrasing <ANG> — choose ONE, used for all 4 options in the item:
  (S1) "the labeled angles in each diagram"
  (S2) "the two angles shown in each diagram"
G = the given angle in panel 4.

CORRECT (always — congruent, x = G):
  "The value of x is G, because <ANG> are congruent."

DISTRACTORS — pick 3 DISTINCT (vary across items). For each, use any listed
justification shape:

1. Supplementary subtraction  (value = 180 - G)
   - "The value of x is 180 - G, because <ANG> are supplementary."
   - "The value of x is 180 - G, because <ANG> are supplementary and the sum of the measures of supplementary angles is 180 degrees."
   - "The value of x is 180 - G, because the sum of the measures of <ANG> is 180 degrees."

2. Complementary subtraction  (value = 90 - G)
   - "The value of x is 90 - G, because <ANG> are complementary."
   - "The value of x is 90 - G, because <ANG> are complementary and the sum of the measures of complementary angles is 90 degrees."

3. Selects 180  (value = 180)
   - "The value of x is 180, because <ANG> are supplementary and the sum of the measures of supplementary angles is 180 degrees."
   - "The value of x is 180, because <ANG> are supplementary."

4. Selects 90  (value = 90)
   - "The value of x is 90, because <ANG> are complementary and the sum of the measures of complementary angles is 90 degrees."
   - "The value of x is 90, because <ANG> are complementary."

5. Obtuse reasoning  (value = "greater than 90")
   - "The value of x is greater than 90, because <ANG> are obtuse angles."

Constraints: all 4 final options distinct in value (or claim, for "greater than 90");
each value a whole number; the congruent option is the only true statement.
Example Question:
In each diagram, line g is parallel to line k, and line t intersects lines g and k.

<<alt: Four diagrams, each showing two parallel lines g and k cut by a transversal t.
Diagram 1: a 55-degree angle and a matching 55-degree angle marked. Diagram 2: a 124-degree
angle and a matching 124-degree angle marked. Diagram 3: a 68-degree angle and a matching
68-degree angle marked. Diagram 4: a 130-degree angle marked at the upper intersection and
the angle x marked at the lower intersection on the same side of the transversal (same-side
interior position).>>

Based on the diagrams, which statement about x is true?

A. The value of x is 180, because the related angles are supplementary and supplementary angles sum to 180 degrees.
B. The value of x is 90, because the related angles are complementary and complementary angles sum to 90 degrees.
C. The value of x is 130, because the related angles have the same measure.
D. The value of x is 50, because the related angles are supplementary, so x = 180 - 130.

Correct: D
Additional Worked Examples:
CRITICAL:
- last line of last step of worked exaple must state the full correct opton (label + text)

Lesson Title: Geometric Problems Involving Exterior Angles of Triangles
Substandard Description: Solve unknown measurements in geometry diagrams involving exterior angles of triangles.
Task: Solve for an unknown exterior angle of a triangle, or a missing interior angle, given a triangle with one side extended to a ray, across a set of four diagrams.
Stimulus Specification:
Stimulus Function: draw_exterior_angle_triangles

Emits ONE image of exactly four triangles in a fixed 2x2 grid (row-major:
[0] top-left, [1] top-right, [2] bottom-left, [3] bottom-right), each
illustrating the exterior-angle relationship. Every triangle uses the SAME
fixed schematic shape (apex on top, horizontal base, right end of the base
extended into an arrow). Angles are NOT drawn to scale - only the labels carry
meaning, so any combination of measures renders identically.

Pydantic schema (ExteriorAngleTrianglesGrid):
- triangles: list[ExteriorAngleTriangle]  (length EXACTLY 4)

Per-triangle schema (ExteriorAngleTriangle):
- exterior_angle: int | str   (required; label at the bottom-right vertex, on the extended ray)
- apex_angle:     int | str    (optional; interior angle at the apex - a remote/opposite interior)
- base_angle:     int | str    (optional; interior angle at the bottom-left vertex - the other remote/opposite interior)
- adjacent_angle: int | str    (optional; interior angle adjacent to the exterior angle - bottom-right interior, supplementary to exterior_angle)

Label coercion (all four fields):
- Numeric / numeric-string (120, "120", "120°") -> rendered as "120°"
- Algebraic / verbatim ("x", "(2x + 10)", "3y") -> rendered verbatim with a trailing degree sign ("x°", "(2x + 10)°")

Configuration (one of two per triangle, enforced by validators):
- 'adjacent'  -> set adjacent_angle ONLY (leave apex_angle/base_angle unset). Shows exterior + the adjacent interior.
- 'opposite'  -> set BOTH apex_angle AND base_angle (leave adjacent_angle unset). Shows exterior + the two remote interiors.
- ALL FOUR triangles in the grid MUST use the SAME configuration; mixing 'adjacent' and 'opposite' in one grid is rejected.
- Rejected per-triangle: adjacent_angle together with apex/base; only one of apex_angle/base_angle ('opposite' needs both); no interior angle at all.

Fixed visual style (NOT configurable):
- 2x2 subplot grid, one schematic triangle per cell; black outline, equal aspect, axes off
- Bottom-right base always extended to the right into a solid filled arrowhead (the exterior-angle ray)
- Exterior angle label placed just above the ray, right of the bottom-right vertex
- 'opposite': apex label below the apex (centered), base label up-right of the bottom-left vertex
- 'adjacent': single interior label inside the bottom-right corner, left of the exterior label
- No vertex letters, side-length labels, right-angle squares, or angle arcs
- 600 DPI; white background; no title/legend; saved in the configured stimulus image format

Content rules:
- Use 'opposite' for exterior-angle-theorem items (exterior = sum of the two remote interiors)
- Use 'adjacent' for linear-pair items (exterior + adjacent interior = 180)
- Keep the configuration uniform across all four triangles
- For solve-for-x items, put the unknown (e.g. "x") on whichever angle is asked; numeric values elsewhere
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST present a set of four triangles in the stimulus, each with one side
  extended to a ray, and MUST state that the triangles are not drawn to scale.
- The question stem MUST end by asking which statement about the unknown angle is true
  (e.g., "Based on these triangles, which statement about x is true?").
- The unknown (labeled x°) appears in only one of the four triangles; the other three show
  fully labeled angle relationships of the same type.
- Each answer option MUST state a numeric value for x followed by a "because" clause that
  gives the supporting reasoning (the value and its justification), matching the STAAR
  statement format.
- Distractors MUST mirror the STAAR error patterns: one option gives the unlabeled interior
  angle instead of the exterior angle (stops after step 1); one combines the interior angle
  with a given angle by adding instead of using the linear pair; one adds 180° to the
  interior-angle sum instead of subtracting from 180°.
Example Question:
Four triangles are shown. One side of each triangle lies on a ray, and the triangles are not
drawn to scale. Based on these triangles, which statement about x is true?

<<alt: Four triangles, each with one side extended to a ray forming an exterior angle. The
fourth triangle has interior angles labeled 115° and 20° and an exterior angle labeled x°.>>

A. x = 135, because 180 − (115 + 20) = 45 and 180 − 45 = 135
B. x = 45, because 115 + 20 = 135 and 180 − 135 = 45
C. x = 85, because 180 − 115 = 65 and 65 + 20 = 85
D. x = 315, because 115 + 20 = 135 and 135 + 180 = 315

Correct: A
Additional Worked Examples:
CRITICAL:
- last line of last step of worked exaple must state the full correct opton (label + text)
Worked Example Stimulus Specification:
draw_exterior_angle_triangles_we

Emits ONE image of a
SINGLE triangle (the 4th cell of that function's 2x2 grid) at the SAME size as a
grid cell, with the unknown bottom-right interior angle highlighted in BLUE with
an angle arc. Use it for the worked-example / explanation step of an
"exterior angle of a triangle" item.

Related functions:
- draw_exterior_angle_triangles (PR #1121) - the 2x2 grid of four triangles this
  is the worked example for. SAME input schema (see below).

Pydantic schema (ExteriorAngleTrianglesGrid)  <-- IDENTICAL to draw_exterior_angle_triangles:
- triangles: list[ExteriorAngleTriangle]  (length EXACTLY 4)

Per-triangle schema (ExteriorAngleTriangle):
- exterior_angle: int | str   (required; label at the bottom-right vertex, on the extended ray)
- apex_angle:     int | str    (optional; interior angle at the apex - a remote/opposite interior)
- base_angle:     int | str    (optional; interior angle at the bottom-left vertex - the other remote/opposite interior)
- adjacent_angle: int | str    (optional; interior angle adjacent to the exterior angle - bottom-right interior, supplementary to exterior_angle)

What it consumes:
- Renders ONLY triangles[3] (the 4th / bottom-right cell). triangles[0..2] are
  accepted but NOT consumed - the schema is kept identical to the grid function
  so the exact same payload works for both.

Blue (unknown) bottom-right interior angle - how it is determined by triangles[3]'s configuration:
- 'opposite' (apex_angle + base_angle set): computed as 180 - apex - base when
  BOTH are numeric; if either is algebraic (e.g. "x") the blue arc is drawn
  WITHOUT a numeric label.
- 'adjacent' (adjacent_angle set): uses the given adjacent_angle directly.

Label coercion (all fields): same as the grid function.
- Numeric / numeric-string (120, "120", "120°") -> rendered as "120°"
- Algebraic / verbatim ("x", "(2x + 10)", "3y") -> rendered verbatim with a trailing degree sign ("x°")

Fixed visual style (NOT configurable):
- One schematic triangle: black outline, equal aspect, axes off; apex on top,
  horizontal base, right end of the base extended into a solid filled arrowhead.
- Remote interior labels (apex below the apex, base up-right of the bottom-left
  vertex) drawn in black when present ('opposite' config).
- Exterior angle label placed just above the ray, right of the bottom-right vertex (black).
- Bottom-right interior angle drawn in BLUE with a blue angle arc at the vertex.
- No vertex letters, side-length labels, or right-angle squares.
- Size consistency: rendered into the bottom-right cell of the SAME figsize=(8,6)
  2x2 layout as draw_exterior_angle_triangles, then the empty cells are removed so
  the image crops tightly to just that triangle (verified ~1740x1045 px @ 600 DPI,
  matching a grid cell).
- 600 DPI; white background; no title/legend; saved in the configured stimulus image format.

Content rules:
- Pair it with draw_exterior_angle_triangles using the SAME triangles payload; put
  the unknown (e.g. "x") on the exterior angle of triangles[3] and numeric values
  on the remote interiors so the blue third angle can be computed (180 - apex - base).
- For 'adjacent' items, the blue angle is just the given adjacent_angle.

Substandard Description: Write similarity statements.
Task: Identify the correct similarity statement for a pair of similar triangles with marked angle measures, using the angle-angle criterion and corresponding vertex order.
Allowed Generator Configs: MCQ - Math - Grade 3-8 - SB - Worked Example
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST be exactly two sentences separated by a single space: it MUST begin with "Four pairs of similar triangles are shown." and MUST end with "Based on the triangles shown, which similarity statement about the fourth pair of triangles is true and can be used to complete the table?"
- PLAIN-TEXT RULE (every string field — stem, each option answer, every explanation): use ONLY plain characters. NEVER use a dollar sign ($) or a backslash (\) anywhere. NEVER use LaTeX or math mode. Write "is similar to" as the literal tilde ~ (never \sim or $\sim$). Write a degree as the ° character (never ^\circ, \degree, or "deg"). If you are about to output $ or \, rewrite it as plain text.
- Every answer option is a single statement "Triangle ___ ~ Triangle ___" using the literal tilde (~).
- TRIANGLE NAMES: every option names the two FOURTH-PAIR triangles using EXACTLY the same set of three letters that label each of those two triangles in the stimulus (do NOT introduce any letter not on that triangle). The ORDER of the three letters within a name is free — it encodes the correspondence and will differ between the correct option and the distractors. The two triangles' letter sets share no common letter.
- The two marked angle measures in each fourth-pair triangle MUST differ from each other (and from the unmarked third/apex angle), so the correspondence is unique. Each marked angle MUST be a whole number between 40 and 80 degrees, the two marked angles MUST differ by at least 8 degrees, AND their SUM MUST be between 95 and 130 degrees so the unmarked third angle is between 50 and 85 degrees and the triangle is well-proportioned (never long-and-thin); this keeps the figure clear.
- CORRECT OPTION: vertices with EQUAL angle measures correspond. Order the names so each position pairs equal-measure vertices. Mark ONLY this statement correct=true.
- DISTRACTORS (exactly three, all correct=false): keep the FIRST triangle's name identical and written first in ALL four options; vary ONLY the order of the SECOND triangle's three letters. Each distractor permutation MUST make at least one position pair UNEQUAL angle measures (so it is false). All four second-name orderings MUST be distinct. NEVER reverse the whole statement or swap which triangle is first (that stays true); never include an option equivalent to the correct one. Exactly ONE option may be true.
- EXPLANATION HOUSE STYLE (MANDATORY and must be MATHEMATICALLY CORRECT):
  * The CORRECT option's explanation MUST begin with "Correct." then list the three true matching pairs with their shared measures, e.g. "Correct. Angle R matches angle X (both 73°), angle S matches angle Y (both 49°), and angle Q matches angle W (both 58°)."
  * EACH distractor's explanation MUST begin with "Not quite" and cite ONE vertex pair THAT THAT OPTION'S ORDER ACTUALLY CREATES whose two angle measures are genuinely different, stating the two TRUE measures, e.g. "Not quite. This pairs Q with X, but 58° does not match 73°." The two measures you cite MUST be the real measures of those two named vertices in the fourth pair, and they MUST be unequal. Do not invent measures.
  * NEVER write "Not quite" on the correct option and NEVER write "Correct" on a distractor. Every option needs a non-empty plain-text explanation.
- BEFORE OUTPUT, verify for each option: (1) correct option pairs equal angles in all three positions; (2) each distractor pairs unequal angles in at least one position and its explanation cites a truly-mismatched pair with the real measures; (3) the four second-name orderings are distinct and none equivalent to the correct one; (4) names match the drawn fourth-pair labels; (5) NO $ or \ characters anywhere.
- STIMULUS KEY RULE (ONLY for angle objects inside each triangle's "marked_angles" array): each angle object has exactly the keys "vertex" (string) and "degrees" (integer). Do NOT use "vertex_label", "vertexLabel", "label", "at_vertex", "measure_degrees", "measure", "angle", "angle_degrees", or "degree". (Normal triangle keys like vertex_labels and caption are fine.)
Example Question:
Four pairs of similar triangles are shown.

<<alt: A two-column table titled "Triangle Pair" and "Similarity Statement" with four rows. Row 1: two triangles each marked 79 degrees and 46 degrees, named Triangle 1 and Triangle 2; statement "Triangle 1 ~ Triangle 2". Row 2: Triangle CDE with angle D = 56 degrees and angle E = 39 degrees, and Triangle PQR with angle Q = 56 degrees and angle R = 39 degrees; statement "Triangle CDE ~ Triangle PQR". Row 3: Triangle FGH with angle F = 67 degrees and angle G = 42 degrees, and Triangle F'G'H' with angle F' = 67 degrees and angle G' = 42 degrees; statement "Triangle FGH ~ Triangle F'G'H'". Row 4: Triangle JKL with angle K = 83 degrees and angle L = 54 degrees, and Triangle STU with angle T = 83 degrees and angle U = 54 degrees; the similarity statement cell is empty.>>

Based on the triangles shown, which similarity statement about the fourth pair of triangles is true and can be used to complete the table?

A. Triangle JKL ~ Triangle SUT
B. Triangle JLK ~ Triangle STU
C. Triangle JKL ~ Triangle STU
D. Triangle JLK ~ Triangle UST

Correct: C
Additional Worked Examples: N/A

Lesson Title: Solve Systems of Linear Equations by Graphing
Substandard Description: Identify and verify the values of x and y that simultaneously satisfy two linear equations in the form y = mx + b from the intersections of the graphed equations.
Task: Identify the ordered pair that represents the solution to a system of two linear equations from the intersection of the graphed lines.
Stimulus Specification:
STIMULUS TYPE: draw_system_of_equations_graph

Abstract 4-quadrant coordinate grid showing TWO straight lines that cross at ONE point. The student reads the ordered pair (x, y) where the lines intersect. The intersection is NEVER marked or labeled — finding it is the whole task.

INPUT (pass this object to draw_system_of_equations_graph):
- line_1: {slope, y_intercept} (required). y = slope*x + y_intercept.
- line_2: {slope, y_intercept} (required). MUST have a different slope from line_1.
- labeled_points: up to 4 decoy points, each {x, y, label_position}. Each MUST lie on a line. NEVER the intersection. label_position one of: above, below, left, right, above_left, above_right, below_left, below_right.
- x_min, x_max, y_min, y_max: integers (default -9, 9, -9, 9). x_min and y_min MUST be negative (4-quadrant grid).
- ALWAYS explicitly include "mark_intersection": false AND "intersection_label": "" (an EMPTY string) in the JSON. NEVER omit intersection_label and NEVER give it any text — omitting it defaults to "solution", which labels the answer and FAILS validation.

RULES:
1. Choose the two lines so they intersect at an INTEGER (lattice) point with both coordinates between -7 and 7, at least 1 unit inside every grid edge. Compute the intersection and CONFIRM the keyed correct option equals it BEFORE finalizing.
2. The two lines must have different slopes (never parallel).
3. The FOUR MCQ options are exactly: (a) CORRECT = the intersection point; (b) SWAPPED = (y, x) of the intersection; (c) DECOY-1 = a point lying on line_1; (d) DECOY-2 = a point lying on line_2. Put DECOY-1 on one line and DECOY-2 on the OTHER line (one decoy per line, e.g. an axis crossing of each line).
4. labeled_points MUST contain EXACTLY the two decoy points DECOY-1 and DECOY-2 (one on each line) and NOTHING else. The CORRECT answer (the intersection) MUST NOT be in labeled_points. The SWAPPED pair MUST NOT be in labeled_points. Every point in labeled_points must also be one of the four options.
5. All four options must be DISTINCT ordered pairs — make sure the swapped pair does not coincide with a decoy or the intersection.
6. ALWAYS explicitly include "mark_intersection": false AND "intersection_label": "" (an EMPTY string) in the JSON. NEVER omit intersection_label and NEVER give it any text — omitting it defaults to "solution", which labels the answer and FAILS validation.

EXAMPLE (lines y = -x + 7 and y = 2x - 2 intersect at (3, 4); options: C=(3,4) correct, A=(4,3) swapped, B=(7,0) on line_1, D=(0,-2) on line_2):
{"line_1": {"slope": -1, "y_intercept": 7}, "line_2": {"slope": 2, "y_intercept": -2}, "labeled_points": [{"x": 7, "y": 0, "label_position": "below_right"}, {"x": 0, "y": -2, "label_position": "below_right"}], "x_min": -9, "x_max": 9, "y_min": -9, "y_max": 9, "mark_intersection": false, "intersection_label": ""}
Common Misconceptions:
- Switches the x- and y-coordinates when writing the intersection point as an ordered pair.
- Selects the point where one line crosses an axis instead of the intersection.
- Selects a labeled point on one line instead of the intersection point.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST use one of the two STAAR sentence pairs exactly:
  (a) First sentence: "The two lines graphed on the coordinate grid represent a system of equations." Final sentence: "Which ordered pair is the solution of the system of equations?"
  (b) First sentence: "The two lines graphed on the coordinate grid each represent an equation." Final sentence: "Which ordered pair represents a solution to both equations?"
- Each answer option MUST be an ordered pair in the form (x, y)
- Distractor roles MUST mirror STAAR: one option is the intersection with coordinates switched (y, x); one option is a labeled point where a line crosses an axis; one option is another labeled decoy point on one of the lines
- Every distractor ordered pair (except the switched pair) MUST appear as a labeled point in the stimulus; the intersection point MUST NOT be labeled
Example Question:
The two lines graphed on the coordinate grid represent a system of equations.

<<alt: A coordinate grid from -9 to 9 on both axes with gridline interval of 1. Two lines intersect at (2, 3), which is not labeled. Line 1 rises from left to right with labeled points (-1, 0) and (0, 1). Line 2 falls from left to right with labeled points (0, 7) and (5, -3).>>

Which ordered pair is the solution of the system of equations?

A. (3, 2)
B. (2, 3)
C. (0, 7)
D. (5, -3)

Correct: B
Additional Worked Examples: N/A
Worked Example Stimulus Specification:
WORKED-EXAMPLE FIGURE for draw_system_of_equations_graph.

Identical to the question stimulus EXCEPT this figure TEACHES the answer, so it SHOWS the intersection:
- Set mark_intersection: true.
- Set intersection_label: "solution".
- Optionally set mark_color: "red" to highlight the crossing.
Build the two lines exactly as in the question (integer lattice intersection between -7 and 7, different slopes, 4-quadrant grid). Keep any labeled_points consistent with the matching question figure.

EXAMPLE (lines y = -x + 7 and y = 2x - 2 intersect at (3, 4); intersection SHOWN):
{"line_1": {"slope": -1, "y_intercept": 7}, "line_2": {"slope": 2, "y_intercept": -2}, "labeled_points": [], "x_min": -9, "x_max": 9, "y_min": -9, "y_max": 9, "mark_intersection": true, "intersection_label": "solution", "mark_color": "red"}

Lesson Title: Solve Systems of Linear Equations by Graphing
Substandard Description: Identify and verify the values of x and y that simultaneously satisfy two linear equations in the form y = mx + b from the intersections of the graphed equations.
Task: Identify the x-coordinate of the ordered pair that represents the solution to a system of two linear equations from the intersection of the graphed lines.
Stimulus Specification:
STIMULUS TYPE: draw_system_of_equations_graph

Abstract 4-quadrant coordinate grid showing TWO straight lines that cross at ONE point. The student must read ONLY the x-coordinate of the intersection. Show two clean lines with NO labeled points and NO marked intersection.

INPUT (pass this object to draw_system_of_equations_graph):
- line_1: {slope, y_intercept} (required).
- line_2: {slope, y_intercept} (required). Different slope from line_1.
- labeled_points: MUST be an empty list []. The distractors for this task are slope values, NOT points, so nothing is labeled on the graph.
- x_min, x_max, y_min, y_max: integers (default -9, 9, -9, 9). x_min and y_min MUST be negative.
- ALWAYS explicitly include "mark_intersection": false AND "intersection_label": "" (an EMPTY string) in the JSON. NEVER omit intersection_label and NEVER give it any text — omitting it defaults to "solution", which labels the answer on the graph and FAILS validation.

RULES:
1. The two lines intersect at an INTEGER lattice point with both coordinates between -7 and 7, at least 1 unit inside the grid. Confirm the keyed x-coordinate equals the true intersection x before finalizing.
2. Different (non-parallel) slopes. Prefer integer slopes so the slope-based distractors are clean (e.g. slopes 4 and 1 give distractors 4, 1, and the reciprocal 1/4).
3. labeled_points = [] (no labeled points — STAAR 2022 Q28 style).
4. ALWAYS explicitly include "mark_intersection": false AND "intersection_label": "" (an EMPTY string) in the JSON. NEVER omit intersection_label and NEVER give it any text — omitting it defaults to "solution", which labels the answer on the graph and FAILS validation.

EXAMPLE (lines y = 4x - 6 and y = x intersect at (2, 2); the student reads x = 2):
{"line_1": {"slope": 4, "y_intercept": -6}, "line_2": {"slope": 1, "y_intercept": 0}, "labeled_points": [], "x_min": -9, "x_max": 9, "y_min": -9, "y_max": 9, "mark_intersection": false, "intersection_label": ""}
Common Misconceptions:
- Uses the slope of a line or its reciprocal as a coordinate.
- Switches the x- and y-coordinates when writing the intersection point as an ordered pair.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem first sentence MUST be: "The two lines graphed on the coordinate grid represent a system of equations."
- The question stem final sentence MUST be: "What is the x-coordinate of the ordered pair that best represents a solution to both equations?"
- Each answer option MUST be a single number (integer or fraction), never an ordered pair
- Distractor values MUST mirror STAAR: the slope of one line, the slope of the other line, and the reciprocal of one of the slopes; the correct answer is the x-coordinate of the intersection point
- The stimulus MUST show two lines with no labeled points, so the intersection must be read directly from the graph
Example Question:
The two lines graphed on the coordinate grid represent a system of equations.

<<alt: A coordinate grid from -9 to 9 on both axes with gridline interval of 1. Two lines intersect at the origin, which is not labeled. One line rises steeply from left to right with slope 4. The other line rises gently from left to right with slope 1/6. No points are labeled.>>

What is the x-coordinate of the ordered pair that best represents a solution to both equations?

A. 1/6
B. 0
C. 4
D. 6

Correct: B
Additional Worked Examples:
Additional Worked Example (LO-2)
The two lines graphed on the coordinate grid represent a system of equations.
<<A coordinate grid from -9 to 9 on both axes with gridline interval of 1. Two lines intersect at the origin (0, 0), which is not labeled. One line rises steeply from left to right with slope 3. The other line rises gently from left to right with slope 1/4. No points are labeled.>>
What is the x-coordinate of the ordered pair that best represents a solution to both equations?
A. 1/4
B. 0
C. 3
D. 4

Step 1: Find the point where the two lines intersect.
The solution to both equations is the point that lies on both lines, so we look for the one point where the two lines cross. The lines cross at the origin.

Step 2: Read the x- and y-coordinates of the intersection point.
The intersection point is at the origin, so x = 0 and y = 0.

Step 3: Answer the question using the coordinates of the intersection point.
The question asks for the x-coordinate of the solution, which is 0. The answer is B. 0.

WORKED-EXAMPLE FIGURE for draw_system_of_equations_graph.

Identical to the question stimulus EXCEPT this figure TEACHES the answer, so it SHOWS the intersection:
- Set mark_intersection: true.
- Set intersection_label: "solution".
- Optionally set mark_color: "red" to highlight the crossing.
Build the two lines exactly as in the question (integer lattice intersection between -7 and 7, different slopes, 4-quadrant grid). Keep any labeled_points consistent with the matching question figure.

EXAMPLE (lines y = -x + 7 and y = 2x - 2 intersect at (3, 4); intersection SHOWN):
{"line_1": {"slope": -1, "y_intercept": 7}, "line_2": {"slope": 2, "y_intercept": -2}, "labeled_points": [], "x_min": -9, "x_max": 9, "y_min": -9, "y_max": 9, "mark_intersection": true, "intersection_label": "solution", "mark_color": "red"}

Lesson Title: Solve Systems of Linear Equations by Graphing
Substandard Description: Identify and verify the values of x and y that simultaneously satisfy two linear equations in the form y = mx + b from the intersections of the graphed equations.
Task: Given a real-world graph of two linear relationships, determine the day on which the two quantities are equal by interpreting the intersection of the graphed lines.
Stimulus Specification:
STIMULUS TYPE: draw_real_world_system_graph

First-quadrant real-world graph (STAAR 2017 Q42 "Ribbon Sales" style) with a bold title, labeled axes, and TWO named straight lines that BOTH decrease and cross exactly once. The student reads the day (x) or amount (y) at the crossing. The intersection is NEVER marked or labeled.

INPUT (pass this object to draw_real_world_system_graph):
- graph_title: string (e.g. "Candle Sales").
- x_label: string (e.g. "Days").
- y_label: string (e.g. "Number of Candles Left to Sell").
- line_1, line_2: {name (<=12 chars), starting_value (>0), rate (<0), label_x (optional)}. Two DIFFERENT names; both rates negative (decreasing).
- x_max, y_max: positive numbers (largest axis values).
- x_grid_interval, y_grid_interval: gridline spacing (>0).
- x_label_interval, y_label_interval: tick-label spacing; each a positive whole multiple of the matching grid interval.
- ALWAYS explicitly include "mark_intersection": false AND "intersection_label": "" (an EMPTY string) in the JSON. NEVER omit intersection_label and NEVER give it any text — omitting it defaults to "solution", which labels the answer on the graph and FAILS validation.

RULES:
1. Both lines decrease (negative rate) and MUST cross at a point that sits ON the gridlines: x a multiple of x_grid_interval and y a multiple of y_grid_interval, so the answer reads exactly. Keep the crossing strictly inside the grid (never on an edge). Confirm the keyed answer equals the true crossing before finalizing.
2. The two lines must have different rates (not parallel) and different names.
3. Each starting_value must be <= y_max.
4. label_interval values must be whole multiples of the grid intervals (mirror STAAR, e.g. gridlines every 3 but labels every 6).
5. ALWAYS explicitly include "mark_intersection": false AND "intersection_label": "" (an EMPTY string) in the JSON. NEVER omit intersection_label and NEVER give it any text — omitting it defaults to "solution", which labels the answer on the graph and FAILS validation.

EXAMPLE (Maya starts at 35 selling 1/day, Josh starts at 25 selling 0.5/day; they cross at (20, 15)):
{"graph_title": "Candle Sales", "x_label": "Days", "y_label": "Number of Candles Left to Sell", "line_1": {"name": "Maya", "starting_value": 35, "rate": -1}, "line_2": {"name": "Josh", "starting_value": 25, "rate": -0.5}, "x_max": 40, "y_max": 40, "x_grid_interval": 5, "y_grid_interval": 5, "x_label_interval": 5, "y_label_interval": 5, "mark_intersection": false, "intersection_label": ""}
Common Misconceptions:
- Switches the x- and y-coordinates when writing the intersection point as an ordered pair.
- Selects the point where one line crosses an axis instead of the intersection.
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST follow the STAAR template: "[Name1] and [Name2] are selling [items] to raise money for [cause]. The graph shows the linear relationship between the number of [items] each of them has left to sell and the number of days that they have been selling [items]."
- The question stem final sentence MUST be: "On which day does it appear that [Name1] and [Name2] will have the same number of [items] left to sell?"
- Each answer option MUST be in the form "Day [number]"
- Distractor values MUST mirror STAAR: the y-coordinate of the intersection used as a day, the x-intercept of one line used as a day, and the starting value (y-intercept) of one line used as a day
- The two lines MUST be labeled inline with the two names; the axes MUST be labeled "Days" and "Number of [Items] Left to Sell"
Example Question:

Lesson Title: Geometric Problems Involving Parallel Lines and Transversals
Substandard Description: Solve unknown measurements in geometry diagrams involving parallel lines and transversals.
Task: Solve for multiple unknown angles (x, y, z) in a figure where parallel lines are cut by a transversal forming a triangle, using the triangle angle sum, alternate interior angles, and linear pairs, across a set of four diagrams.
Stimulus Specification:
Stimulus Function: draw_triangle_transversal_grid

Emits ONE image: a 2x2 grid of four triangles, each with its apex on a line parallel
to its base (the triangle's two non-base sides act as transversals between two
parallel lines). Six of the seven interior angles between the two parallel lines are
labelled at fixed positions. Used for teaching triangle angle-sum / exterior-angle
relationships via parallel-line angles. All four panels share one triangle_type;
only the angle values differ. Panels are rendered in order: top-left, top-right,
bottom-left, bottom-right.

Pydantic schema (TriangleTransversalGrid):
- triangle_type: Literal['acute','right','obtuse']  (required; fixed not-to-scale template)
                 - acute  = apex between the base vertices, all angles < 90
                 - right  = right angle at bottom-right vertex (vertical side + right-angle squares)
                 - obtuse = mirrored '\' slant; bottom-left angle is the obtuse one
- panels:        list[TrianglePanel]  (EXACTLY 4)

Per-panel schema (TrianglePanel) - geometry is fully set by the two base angles:
- base_left_angle:  float  (required; interior angle at bottom-left vertex B. For
                            'obtuse', this is the obtuse angle > 90.)
- base_right_angle: float | None  (interior angle at bottom-right vertex C. Ignored
                            for 'right' (C fixed at 90); defaults to 90 if omitted.)
  Apex angle is computed as 180 - base_left - base_right.
- a_left_label:  str | None  (override apex upper-left label;  default base_left_angle)
- a_mid_label:   str | None  (override apex interior label;    default 180-base_left-base_right)
- a_right_label: str | None  (override apex upper-right label; default base_right_angle)
- b_in_label:    str | None  (override bottom-left interior;   default base_left_angle)
- b_out_label:   str | None  (override bottom-left outer;      default 180-base_left_angle)
- c_in_label:    str | None  (override bottom-right interior;  default base_right_angle)

Label coercion (all *_label fields):
- Bare text only - no degree symbol; the function appends it.
- Any displayed value equal to 90 renders as a right-angle square instead of text.

Fixed visual style (NOT configurable):
- Two horizontal parallel lines (filled arrowheads); triangle's two non-base sides
  extend through them as transversals. NOT drawn to scale (3 fixed templates).
- Six angles labelled at fixed slots; labels nudged to stay clear of the lines.
- Obtuse template: the bottom-left interior angle uses a curved "U-turn" leader
  arrow from the vertex out into empty space so the tight wedge stays readable.

Content rules:
- Keep all four panels' values consistent with the chosen triangle_type so the
  taught pattern is obvious across the grid.
- Use bare labels (no degree symbol); send variables like "x" to leave an unknown.
Allowed Content Types: Inline Drag and Drop Question with Stimulus
Question Requirements:
- The question stem MUST present a set of four figures in the stimulus, each showing two
  parallel lines intersected by a transversal that forms a triangle, and MUST end by asking
  for the measures x, y, and z in degrees (e.g., "Based on the figures, what are the measures
  x, y, and z in degrees?").
- The unknowns x, y, and z appear in only the final figure; the other three figures show
  fully labeled angle relationships of the same type.
- The item MUST be drag-and-drop: provide an answer bank of 6 degree values and three target
  boxes labeled x, y, and z.
- The instructions MUST state that each answer may be used more than once and that not all
  answers will be used.
- The answer bank MUST contain the three correct values plus decoy values; decoys MUST include
  a given interior angle, the right-angle value (90°), and a value equal to 180° minus a given
  angle (the supplementary-instead-of-congruent error).
- The three unknowns MUST be interdependent: y from the triangle angle sum, z congruent to y
  as alternate interior angles, and x as the linear pair with y.
Example Question:
The figures shown each have a set of parallel lines intersected by a transversal. Based on the
figures, what are the measures x, y, and z in degrees?
Move the correct answer to each box in the table. Each answer may be used more than once. Not
all answers will be used.

<<alt: Four figures, each showing two parallel lines cut by a transversal forming a triangle.
The fourth figure has one interior angle of 40°, a right angle, the third interior angle
labeled y°, an alternate interior angle labeled z°, and angle x° forming a linear pair with y°.>>

Answer bank: 40°, 50°, 90°, 100°, 130°, 140°

Answer: x = 130°, y = 50°, z = 50°
